The other answers here are already good. I'll add a bit more specifics to my area: networking. Education in networking is abysmal. New graduates who want to work in networking might know a little of the basics, but the textbooks and course materials have never kept up. New hires require about two years of practical experience before they are truly knowledgeable. Specific suggestions: Stop teaching the ISO model. All this does is confuse people. Layering is good, but we long ago proved that this is the wrong model. Students just have to unlearn this. Teach the IP model instead and only. Teach a little bit of physical layer information. It's very helpful for grounding a motivating students. Teach a little bit of the logical layer fundamentals. Addressing is important. Spanning tree is essential. Rapid spanning tree and TRILL are optional. Explain retries, keepalives, binary exponential capped backoff. Explain the difference between connection oriented and connectionless, and the values of each. Reality has shown that optimal is a hybrid. Teach TCP and UDP. Students need a grounding in reality. MP-TCP for extra credit. Not re-inventing TCP should be an exercise. Teach DNS. This is a distributed, hierarchical database, and a pretty good model for numerous other distributed systems. Routing protocols: This should cover RIP (distance vector), OSPF or IS-IS (link state), and BGP (path vector). Network security. This should be its own entire course. Distributed systems. Another entire course. Please explain the CAP theorem.

Well, I was reading Robert Nicholson's answer, which I think you should check out. But… I don't agree with him. That doesn't mean his answer is wrong—it's just that I don't see it that way, and I'll tell you why in a moment. And honestly, that's a bit contradictory on my part, because I actually have a certain tolerance for students who cheat. So here's my point: You haven't understood the purpose of the essay, or of homework in general. The assignment is your opportunity to learn, to get challenged, to show how good you can become. Think about it: you write an essay, and then a professional professor—someone you're basically paying for—gets to pick it apart, to show you where you're wrong. Why? Because in that back-and-forth, that dialectic, you're building yourself into a competent professional. But you blew it. You got lazy. You didn't give that exercise the weight it deserved. Look, in my field—music—we all know we have to be insanely competent. You can walk down the streets of New York and see brilliant musicians starving. That level of competition in music has never really existed in most other fields. But here's the bad news: The future is going to be fucking hard. Harder than you realize. In a few years, a lot of you won't even have jobs, because nobody's going to hire someone who can be replaced by AI. And here's the kicker: you've already shown you can be replaced by AI. The moment you turned in that essay, you proved it—you showed your professor you're substitutable. Do you see my point now? So, in the future, the person making the hiring decision is going to go with someone who can't be replaced. I hope you really take this in. Best wishes.

As a university professor, I'm going to give you a very unpopular response. Deny it. Make him go through the whole process. (It's a real pain in the ass for faculty if you push back, and they will usually back down.) First, studies show that 60% of university students cheat. And yet, a very small percentage are ever caught and held accountable. Which is incredibly unfair. Whether or not you get caught is just random luck. Second, the so called "AI detectors" are notoriously unreliable. There are already several lawsuits pending against universities that have accused students based on these detectors. My advice would be to force him to submit this to a review committee. And then insist that he present specific evidence. And if the "evidence" is the output of an AI detector, do some research and walk in with the extensive documentation that you can find on the web about how worthless the detectors are. In other words, make the bar very, very high - and it will probably get dropped. And stop using AI to do your work.

As a college professor and member of the college ethics board, I advise you to do the complete opposite of the advice given by others. The policy in most Student Code of Ethics is that you are responsible for verifying that your work is your own. This was true before current AI capacities, and is still true. I will also say that every AI essay sounds the same. They all sound like middle school book reports, even if you tell the AI to humanize the language many times. Any other professor, including those serving on ethics boards, can also spot this. Chances are your essay also includes lots of hallucinated references. Any professor just has to point to one of those to show you cheated. Also, while AI detectors often show false positives, it's nearly impossible that a very high reliability score across multiple detectors would occur. Thus, it is considered prof and very easy for us to do. Even if you deny it, we can still fail you. If you fight it, it's very little work for us. If you confess and apologize, your professor will probably only give you a zero for that assignment. Deny it and you will be failed and reported. In my case, each student that has been honest has received a penalty and been given a second chance at the assignment (for the first offense only). One of the students I caught who was honest even went on to become one of my top students and has started graduate work in my area.

Since you hopefully wrote the essay using Google Docs, with multiple editing sessions, you can show them the document history. If you didn't, then it's going to be difficult at best. I have to admit that there are many accusations of faculty assuming that the AI detectors are correct, when they appear to be… problematic. However, having read a number of questions/claims like this on social media, did your professor actually accuse you, in the sense of stating their conclusion in an academic integrity report? Or did they express concern and ask you? Because that seems to be a common feature. Students getting asked if they did the work and then responding as though they had been accused. If you were accused, you'd know. The academic integrity reports are not ambiguous, and at some institutions, there would be an automatic hearing. Read the academic integrity section of your student handbook (just Google for it).

I'm actually surprised the top answer is "deny it" and then make you play a game of chicken with the university, professor, and administrators. I think it's both irresponsible and stupid—not to mention immoral by encouraging this kind of behavior that you have rightfully identified as wrong. Don't. Just own up, demonstrate that you're willing to learn from this mistake, and ask for leniency. It will be better for you. I know that something like this worked for someone I know not long ago. If a professor (or TA) caught you using GPT to the point they formally accuse you, they probably have more evidence than just "the AI checker flagged it as AI-made". I think what that other answer does not have is the imagination that other professors might be smarter than that. One way professors I know have been combating AI use for your sort of case is by making the student present whatever it was they purportedly wrote. If you wrote it yourself, it would be trivial. If an AI wrote it and you never checked it, you'll either be stumped or you must try to BS your way through—which should be easy for a professor who is an expert in the field to catch. Having spent time here in Quora and being exposed to AI-generated content, I don't need the AI-checker to flag GPT text. You can tell when the AI "hallucinates" as it wandered off topic, gives bogus references, or (if multiple people use AI for the same question) see three or more people making the exact same points in an open-ended essay. If you followed that advice and your professor make you do something like I just said or found the proof, you'll end up in a deeper hole than you were. Then what? On the other hand, admitting that you screwed up is very hard (and I still struggle with it), but it is better. The person I mentioned earlier was actually getting expelled without getting their degree for one administrative reason or another. But one letter of apology later (and a few remedial actions), that person is back on track to getting their degree. And this is despite that person getting an unambiguously-worded letter saying "you didn't do X when we told you to. Per university regulations, we're now kicking you out. Goodbye and good luck." You're already halfway past the hardest bit. It's a matter of putting the other foot forward. You follow that other advice, it might sound easier for you right now but it will not be an attitude you should have as a responsible adult. Most professors aren't sadistic. They were there too, been there, done that. They understand the temptation to cheat to get A. That's why I said, ask for leniency while demonstrating that you're willing to learn.

My teacher says I used chat GPT to write my essay when I didn't. I went back and put my essay through an AI detector and it said that my work was written by AI. What do I do? It is common knowledge that it is difficult to prove a negative, such as that you didn't have an AI write your essay. However, if it is important, you can do the following: Show your teacher your writing process. If you have notes, drafts, or other evidence of your thinking while you were writing your essay, show them to your teacher. Explain your writing process to your teacher. Describe how you came up with the ideas in the essay, how you put it together, and how you chose your words. Offer to take a writing test to demonstrate that you are able to write an essay on a similar topic in a limited amount of time. All of this together might turn the tide from against you to for you.

Tell your teacher that there isn't any reliable way to tell whether something has been written by AI and that AI 'detectors' are probably bunk at this point in history. Put that aside and propose that they simply question you about the content of the essay. This is a decent way to confirm that you actually did write it; after all, you know what's in it if you wrote it. This is in fact the process a friend of mine used to determine that one of his college students had written an essay in this way: he simply asked him to stay behind after class, asked him a few questions, and quickly determined that the student had no idea what was in it.

In the future, write essays that are identifiable as your own. One of the worst elements of AI-written essays is that there's no personality to them. Personal essays often draw on personal experiences. AI-written works don't do that. They can't do that. Let me give you an example. My son is an MMA fighter, a black belt in Brazilian jiu-jitsu, and a college-level wrestler. Suppose someone asked the question: "Who would win in a fight: A BJJ black belt or a Division 1 wrestler?" ChatGPT probably would begin something like: "The question of who would win a fight between a BJJ black belt and a Division 1 wrestler has been debated for years with no clear resolution. Each would have certain advantages and disadvantages . . ." B-O-R-I-N-G. The AI program typically restates the question (not a problem in itself, except that's an obvious trait of AI programs). Then the AI answer would waffle, stating that either might win, followed by a listing of the strengths and weaknesses of each. It probably would conclude by saying that either could win, though the one with greater skills in that particular martial art might have an advantage. How would I begin my answer? Maybe something like: "In most cases, the BJJ black belt would win. My son is a black belt, and he knows more than a dozen effective submission techniques. Wrestlers may have better takedown skills, but they have no submission skills. Further, wrestlers hate being on their backs, whereas that's a natural and comfortable position for BJJ practitioners." See the difference? I'd actually answer the question. (Others might disagree with my conclusion, but an essay should take a clear position.) I specifically refer to my son . . . the sort of thing no AI would (or could) do. I give specific examples, not the vague pablum regurgitated by AI. So: If you're asked to write an essay, then write one that reflects you. One that couldn't have been written by AI. And one that answers the question. Over the years, AI will get more "human." But it's not there yet, and it'll be a while until it comes close.

I used chat GPT to phrase some sentences in my essay, but for some reason, my teacher told me it was 100 percent AI-generated. Now I need to redo the entire essay. What do I do? You should use ChatGPT and other AIs just to clarify your question, then use it only as reference material while you compose your own written work. Never use any two-word or longer phrase from the AI in your own work. Use a dictionary or thesaurus (such as powerthesaurus.org) to find synonyms and alternate words and phrases for the AI's words and phrases. Teachers are very much attuned to the phrases and collocations used by AIs, and once they see even a hint of those word combinations in your work, you are toast. Even if you wrote the whole essay using your own thoughts, if you used the AI's terminology in only one spot, you will be accused of using the AI to generate all of your work.

Yes. It counts as AI-generated content and will be detected by Turnitin. That doesn't mean you can't use Chat GPT to paraphrase the work. You just can't use it as a substitute to your own effort. Paraphrasing your work in ChatGPT with a prompt like "paraphrase with academic tone" can still be considered AI-generated content and may be detected by plagiarism detection tools like Turnitin. While paraphrasing is a common practice in academic writing, it is important to properly attribute the original source and give credit to the original author. Here are some key points to consider: Paraphrasing and Plagiarism Detection: Plagiarism detection tools like Turnitin use algorithms to analyze the language, structure, and content of a document to identify copied or paraphrased text [2]. If you paraphrase content generated by ChatGPT without proper attribution, it may still be flagged as potentially plagiarized. Proper Attribution: When paraphrasing, it is crucial to properly attribute the original source and give credit to the original author. Simply rewriting the information in your own words is not enough to avoid plagiarism [2]. You should include in-text citations and a reference list to acknowledge the original source. Academic Integrity: Academic integrity is of utmost importance in academic writing. It is essential to do your own research, think critically about your topic, and express your own ideas and insights in your own words [2]. Relying solely on AI-generated content for your academic work can undermine the principles of academic integrity. Seeking Assistance: If you are struggling with your academic writing, it is advisable to seek assistance from your professors, tutors, or writing centers. These resources can provide guidance and support to help you develop your own ideas and express them effectively in your writing [2]. Learn more: 1. Using ChatGPT Wisely for Academic Writing | by Mehrab M S | Medium 2. Discussing Plagiarism with ChatGPT 3. Using ChatGPT for Assignments | Tips & Examples

I have a suspicion that you did not do your own research, write a paper based on it, and then ask ChatGPT to rewrite your paper. Instead, it appears that instead of doing your own research, you asked ChatGPT to write your paper for you. Then you ran it through a paraphraser to change the wording around so that it wouldn't sound so much like a ChatGPT paper. Now you want to know if you can get away with this, getting credit for doing research you did not do, and writing you did not write. If all this is true, you learned nothing from this project except how to avoid thinking about the subject and how to try to avoid detection. If I'm right, you cheated. It's the same thing as asking someone else to write a paper for you, then to avoid the possibility that they plagiarized the paper, running the paper through a paraphraser, all in the hopes of not having to do any mental work on the topic yourself. You deserve zero credit in your course for this project, in that case. The professor is not in need of papers. The professor is asking you to do some mental work to learn something, and develop your skills. The point of writing assignments is not to fill the professor's inbox with machine-generated grammatically correct strings of words. By the way, ChatGPT sometimes just makes up things that have no basis in fact. It doesn't really understand what it is writing about, or make any judgments about the reliability of sources. There are educationally useful ways to use generative AI in doing research, but having it write your papers for you is not one of them.

To Eugene Makhanu, Turnitin and other software applications are extremely effective at detecting paraphrasing, which is a modification of someone else's work and is considered plagiarism. Software is set up to catch identical wording, so a paraphrase will be flagged if it leaves certain words unchanged and only modifies some words. NOTE: Teachers and professors should not just rely on such tools; sometimes a wild change in how a student writes is a clue it's been plagiarized. In fact, Turnitin posted an article on this topic: Does Turnitin Detect Plagiarism? As Turnitin states on that page regarding similarity between documents: "Turnitin offers a tool that helps educators (and their students) make informed evaluations of student work rapidly." Note that, even if plagiarism is not detected, the student's instructor may figure out that the student merely plugged in synonyms into someone else's work, which Turnitin would not catch, but the end result would sound strange. Either way, the student loses. Here's an example of two pieces of text. The first is by Shakespeare. The second is my wonky paraphrase, which Turnitin probably would NOT flag, because the words are different, but any instructor would recognize how I paraphrased Shakespeare and fail me. SHAKESPEARE (from Romeo and Juliet):[1] What's in a name? That which we call a rose By any other word would smell as sweet; SARAH'S (awkward) PARAPHRASE: How much information is in an artificial appellation? We may label it a flowering specimen from the genus Rosa, yet if we were to choose a different title, it would still affect our olfactory system in the same sugary manner. "The Avalon Rose," a flowering specimen from the genus Rosa, which has bloomed faithfully each year coincidentally (or not) on the birthday of the real Avalon Rose, who is now 20! source: my own photo of my own rose bush TIPS: PRO TIP #1: If you change EVERY SINGLE WORD in your paraphrase to avoid detection by Turnitin, your end result will sound stilted, which will be a red flag to the professor even if the software might not catch it. PRO TIP #2: Wouldn't it be much easier (and more ethical) to simply write your own material? WARNINGS: These applications will detect and highlight strings of words that are identical to another piece of text, even if you changed multiple words within the original material. TRUE STORY: I have a friend who got caught when her university's software flagged her paraphrasing (plagiarism). She is from Moldova, and thought she wasn't plagiarizing because she had changed many of the words. She had done primary research for over five years at a respected university in Berlin, Germany, and most of her doctoral dissertation was her own work. She became a little sloppy in citing at least one source, and she should never have copied such a large amount of text into her work. She changed select words here and there (to paraphrase the original), but the end result was recognizably taken from someone else's work, and the sentences and arguments were in the same order. This was mosaic plagiarism, and it's an intentional effort to deceive. It's not an accident.[2] It would have been fine to quote a short section, that is, copy a few lines and properly quote them, but she made several key mistakes. She should have added quotation marks and provided proper citations and references when doing so. PRO TIP: Don't be lazy and unoriginal. Write your own stuff! Learn from your mistakes, and never stop learning. You've got this! —Sarah Madden, #8484, Written and ©Copyrighted ❤️ 3 November 2018 (revised 2 August 2024). I write my own words. Not really. I've used ChatGPT as a brain storming tool, and as a spell checker, and one time I accidentally used one brain storming note in a paper and it failed to catch it, and I was horrified and told my professor who had me revise the paraphraph and to drop the citation and topic, I did that and it didn't pop. Though I did have an entire paper I wrote before that popped up as 50% AI generated, and was before I even used ChatGPT the first time, and I later found out, TurnItIn sold my paper to be used in AI learning models, and out of curiosity I submitted one of my papers I wrote in highschool, and it popped up as 100% AI written and plagerized, and it was referencing a paper from 2020, the paper I wrote in 2014.

Speaking as a college professor here, there are two answers to this question, and you're not going to like one of them. Carefully phrasing your actions as using AI to "make a research paper more comprehensible," is standard undergrad/grad student code for cheating. We couch our language in ways that allow us to maintain the thin façade of denial about our motives, but let's be clear: whether you simply lift your entire paper from the ChatGPT site or just grab main themes, you still let the computer do your work for you and then submitted it as original labor. You can claim that it was done to make the paper "comprehensible," but I can tell you that this characterization will fool exactly 0.000001% of all college faculty. Dress a pig in satin and it's still a pig. Now, the answer to the second question, which you probably are going to like, is this: it is highly unlikely, unless your lecturer is very motivated, that this cheating will be found out. It would involve the teacher generating about 20 minor permutations of their essay/research paper prompts and then reading through each one while comparing it with your answer. Most people aren't going to do that. Now, if the teacher does use nearly identical prompts to what you used when you created your "ode to comprehension" (which, by the way, … please!), you may be in serious trouble. I get this question a lot from both students and other faculty. What I have told them is that I have had to rework my questions for essays or exams to remove the simple expedient of them using ChatGPT to generate their answers. It has been challenging because I then actually generate about 10 versions of the question/answer cycle to get multiple versions and I do rigorously compare them to the material that students turn in (which is very time-consuming and tedious). I also inform them in advance that I will be doing this, so there is no margin for them claiming "they didn't know" what I was doing or that they were violating academic integrity. But that's just me. Right now, academia is really twisted up in knots about how to deal with ChatGPT and universities are creating workshops and other resources for faculty to prepare them for the brave new world. Stay tuned, because this problem isn't going away.

It's the percentage of the words that are likely to be AI generated. The way to avoid this issue is to write the paper yourself. When paraphrasing from a source, practice Parataxis writing style so as to avoid accusations of AI composition. AI papers merely summarize the source documents, and in the process, AI mimics the writing style of the source. In contrast, the Parataxis writing style consists of short, simple sentences consisting of a subject, verb and object. Write in the active voice by avoiding passive "to be" verbs such as "is, was, were, etc." and auxiliary verbs such as "have, had, etc.". Also use a consistent tense, which is generally the past tense in papers because the subject of the paper is usually something that happened in the past. Most importantly, only use third person pronouns such as he, she, it, etc. and avoid second person pronouns (you or your) and first person pronouns (I, me, or my) like the plague. Use this technique to write a rough draft, and then edit/revise the paper by combining some of the short sentences using conjunctions. Otherwise your teacher will mark all over it in red ink: choppy, need more compound and complex sentences. Good Luck!

As of now - it would likely be considered plagiarism - if you're caught. AI is still quite new and feels threatening - teachers tend to not react well when they feel their expertise is being breached. The bigger question is this. Will AI always be considered plagiarism? Back in the day typing teachers would not allow us to use whiteout - they said we had to learn to be perfect. Then electric typewriters were invented - with built in whiteout tape- and we were allowed to use it because it would be ridiculous not to use it. They taught us how to change the whiteout tape… And now - would typing teachers discourage auto-correct on keyboards? No and there are no more typing teachers because any kid can learn to type on their own - Will AI become an accepted tool? I think it will and it will have to be because things are hard to control - like the use of computers… and things that are difficult to detect are very hard to control. What's the downside? No one will learn to write…? No one knows how to write properly and legibly with chalk on a chalkboard anymore. Should we still teach that? Few people know how to milk a cow or ride a horse - should we still encourage people to learn those skills when milk is easily purchased and cars, buses, trains and planes have replaced horses? We use computers for a great many things. We trust computers to do a great many things. Computers do many things better than people do them. And now for a while teachers and some others will be up in arms because computers can now write well too - so for now yes, your teacher(s) will likely consider AI use as plagiarism or cheating - until they get used to it being around.

Citing your sources is a much better way to game the system. Trust your self. A lot of people indulge in plagiarism, because they feel their writing is not good enough. If you don't put your own work out there, you will never get the feedback you need to make it better. The problem is that if you paraphrase, the writing can seem awkward, and it will alert the teacher to the fact that there is something odd going on with the writing. Once we feel that there is something funny happening, we tend to slow down and go through the text with a fine tooth comb, and the outcome is rarely a conclusion that the essay deserves a top grade.

It might. But if you're using ChatGPT for your uni work, expect to be caught for plagiarism anyway. It's easy to see when someone's submission isn't typical of their usual work, and ChatGPT and the like have a style that's very recognisable. There's a good chance your submission will be flagged by the marker as suspicious, and you'll be called in to explain how you wrote it. Furthermore, what good do you think will come of getting your work done for you? Do you think you'll be able to survive a job, let alone get one, by relying on other people (or software) to do your work for you? Maybe it's time to learn to do your own work.

A few such questions have turned up here recently. Clearly, colleges have scared students to death about "plagiarism" without bothering clearly to explain what it means. I recommend looking it up. It's plagiarism if you directly quote from someone else's work — whether a single sentence or a large swatch — without attribution (credit, footnotes). ChatGPT and its ilk do not do that. They "write" original material. You could be accused of taking shortcuts, perhaps of actual "cheating" (in terms of not actually doing the work yourself). But it's no more "plagiarism" than it would be if your mother read the draft paper and cleaned up your grammar. Please, let us explain what "plagiarism" actually is!

Paraphrasing is a common academic practice if done properly. That means that it is done with originality and with proper citations. Without that, it would slip into plagiarism such as mosaic plagiarism. As a result of that your work can get rejected or receive a extremely low score. So, remember to paraphrase with originality and with an in text citation that is accompanied with a full length citation in the reference list at the end.

When it writes an essay from scratch, ChatGPT just puts words together in commonly-occurring patterns that it finds in its vast training database. The results read like actual, informed sentences. Very impressive. But it is quite possible that the sentences are saying false things. (There are some interesting examples out there in which Chat claims that there are published reports that say X, when in fact those reports do not exist. Of course, Chat is constantly under revision, and so it might now have been programmed not to say "as reported by" or any other such thing.) ChatGPT has no thoughts of its own. It is simply copying word patterns "learned" from its own "reading". So, the essays it constructs may contain few, if any, actual significant copies of sentences in published works. At best, the sentences it constructs are sensible paraphrases of things said in published works (without citations, of course), and when that happens you have a case of plagiarism that can probably be detected by Turnitin, but when ChatGPT makes up new sentences that are made up of new word combinations it created, there is no plagiarism of any published work. If a student uses ChatGPT to write an essay that the student is supposed to write, the student is essentially having someone else write their paper, and the student learns nothing except how to cheat. It's much like the old system of cheating in which a student with money hires a good, knowledgeable writer to write their essays. Essentially, using ChatGPT to write an essay and claiming academic credit for it is just a new way of cheating, in which the student learns nothing about the topic.

You just need to be careful if you're using it to "check" your work if you're meaning checking facts. Since ChatGPT is aggregate information up to a certain date on the internet (date shifts to a later date whenever they update the model with more gleanings), ChatGPT will act as if the information or correction is absolutely right but it may be wrong especially if it is something time sensitive. And when I say time sensitive I mean that it is information or fact that might have changed in the last 6 months or so. Also since it is aggregate collection of blogs and everything on the internet, if enough people say the wrong thing, the information ChatGPT spits out will be wrong. In general aggregate knowledge is likely to be correct but not always. For example on the time sensitive information: I asked for comparison of employment rates of Biden and Trump presidency terms up to September 2025 and it showed me Trump's first term, Biden's term, and showed Biden's term as continuing so making Biden's term January 2021 thru September 2025 instead of ending January 2025 when Trump took office for a second time. So it gave me employment data in 2025 as if it were under the Biden presidency. So in this case ChatGPT was 9 months out of date. I also asked for the names of stocks that had increased in value 25% over the last two weeks. None of the stocks ChatGPT listed had increased in value 25% over the last two weeks although over a year ago there had been a big jump in price in a two week period or less. Some had actually fallen in price in the last two weeks. Older facts that don't change over time are not a problem like the world rotates and will continue to do so. Or femur is a name of a bone. Those facts don't change. But if your writing utilizes facts that relies on data that can change over time like labor statistics, don't use AI to fact check. Go to an original source of the data to fact check. Also that way you know the date of the data collection or study as well as the parameters of the data or study.

Related AI detector is detecting my work as 100% AI generated. Because I ignorantly used it as an editing and grammar tool at the request of an academic advisor. The words, concept & life experience in the already published piece is my own. What do I do?

Fascinating topic, and one that's been on my mind the last several months. Here's the deal—AI language models were trained on decades, even centuries, of real human writing. So when a detector says something sounds like AI, it's often because AI sounds like us. It was built to imitate human patterns. That doesn't mean something was written by AI. It means it sounds like polished writing. AI is a fact of life now. The genie's out of the bottle. I don't know a single working writer who doesn't use it for something. Brainstorming. Outlining. Idea dumping. But no professional writer is just typing "write this for me" into ChatGPT and hitting copy/paste. To prove a point to someone, I dumped several articles and music/movie reviews I wrote in the 90s into AI detectors. They all came back as partially AI-written. So when someone tells you they can tell something is AI because of "flowery language" or metaphors, tell them humans were writing like that before AI was thing, then kindly tell them to FO.

In my case, I have files and old coursework going back to 2021 and beyond (e.g. before ChatGPT). I put it through an AI detector and it said it was 79% AI generated. In other words, my original work was apparently mostly AI generated, despite being before AI was widely available to the public. If you sit down with an advisor and show them something similar, they basically have no case since they are using a tool that is not accurate to make their case.

Hello, I'm a university teacher, and no, QuillBot cannot escape Turnitin. Turnitin has a new AI detection feature that identifies text that has been computer generated. I just caught a student with a 61% computer-generated essay. They admitted to using QuillBot.

Properly cited paraphrased text is a way to avoid plagiarism. It allows writers to to convey the essence of what the original author said and reference where that information came from via the citation. Paraphrasing is expressing what someone else said in your own and original words. When paraphrasing is coupled with a proper academic citation then it can not be counted as plagiarism because the paraphrasing has been done in original words and the original author has been referenced.

It may show up in TurnitIn as a patchwork quilt of likely-plagiarised phrases, as they get used repeatedly. Sometimes it doesn't, but then your work will have the obvious superficiality, structure, and sometimes outright errors characteristic of AI-generated content. But universities are getting wise to various forms of contract cheating[1] including use of AI and paraphrasers, and they will determine, sooner or later, that you're not doing your own work. Then you'll be in deep trouble and will bring shame upon yourself and your family. Footnotes [1] Contract cheating - Wikipedia

No system is 100% effective. So you might get away with using ChatGPT for your thesis, but to be honest, it will not be a very good thesis, and by using this AI bot you will not be doing the original work required to actually qualify as an original researcher creating new knowledge. ...

If your own research work contained plagiarism, and you use chatgpt as a paraphrasing tool to try to cover it up, it's still plagiarism. A paraphrase of an uncited quotation found in your original paper is still in need of being cited. A paraphrase of an important statement of fact that needed to be cited in your original paper still needs to be cited. If you are just using chatgpt to improve the phrasing of things you actually wrote yourself, that is not considered plagiarism. It might or might not make your paper better in terms of wording. Make sure that any citations you included in your original writing are preserved by chatgpt. If you care about style, and you have developed your own writing style, using chatgpt to reword your paper can wipe out everything interesting about your style. When I have used earlier writing advice/correcting software on my own papers, I end up rejecting almost all the changes the software suggests, but it does catch some grammar errors that I missed during earlier rewriting and editing.

Turnitin should be used by educators as a tool, only. It isn't (and doesn't claim to be) 100% accurate. It claims to be 98.8% "sure" about its ability to detect ChatGPT. The methodology it uses to do this is sound, but not entirely foolproof. Personally, I think its accuracy, outside the testing lab, may be less than claimed, although still good enough. If I had to venture a guess, I'd say about 80% under real world conditions. Even as a plagiarism detector, Turnitin picks up a lot of material that is duplicated accidentally, or in the normal course of writing. As a result, professors who use this tool always need to take a look at the paper. I can usually see, in seconds, whether the material was plagiarized or not. Usually it isn't. Any duplication is coincidental. AI-written material is even easier to spot, as it's usually written at a level most students, even in college, can't achieve. I'm afraid college students mostly write very badly, and while ChatGPT has been programmed to mimic decent (if not good) writing, its creators didn't program it to mimic immature ineptitude. So, sorry Elon. Homework is still in play. However, the most glaring problem with AI is that (much like the students who use it), it doesn't follow the instructions for the essay. If your prompt is something like, "Write a paper on cell division," or whatever, that's probably an easy assignment for ChatGPT. If you start to add conditions, such as a specific format, a certain number and class of sources, specific citation forms, and so forth, the platform fails miserably (along with the student). So far, students who are using ChatGPT have bought into the erroneous idea that it just magically produces a paper that you can hand in, unedited. Also, the students most likely to resort to ChatGPT are those least able to use it effectively. I suspect there will be thousands of unhappy, naïve undergraduates all over the country, this month, as we catch baby's first attempt to use AI to game the system.

This kind of question shows lack of undertanding of what research and scholarship are. If you personally did not think up the idea, however you may wish to phrase or rephrase it, and it's not a commonplace (Like: "The sun shines on sunny days") PROVIDE CITATION INFORMATION OR IT'S PLAGIARISM. End of discussion. And I have an imagination. I think there are persons in academia who I call "footnote sniffing truffle pigs". When they get home at night, FOR FUN, instead of watching HBO or NFL, they pour thru journal articles looking for plagiariasms and if they find one, you may not get a chance to try that trick a second time unless you already have tenure and more, and then you still can't count on getting away with it. Do the right thing. It should be obvious: When in doubt, cite it. I try to do this even in chattng with family, which is not appreciated but, like the ad slogan for Hebrew National Hotdogs: "I answer to a higher power" → intellectual integrity. Do you? Aside: What to do if you don't have the citation information? You cite it as "Ref. lost", unless it is mission-critical to your work and then you need to either personally defend it TOO, or not use it. In business and politiics, they say: "follow the money" In scholarship and research, it's: "Follow the audit trail [citations]" But there too it's a good idea to ALSO follow the money. Research should be disinteresed (not: uninterested!) but the researcher also has to pay their mortgages and some also "have [big] egos".

I am not going to state that "everything is on the internet", but I can't off the top of my head, come up with anything a student might need that isn't on the Internet? So can you please clarify your question? What texts (or type of texts) might a student need that are not available on the internet?

You can't and there's no point. When academic writing is done right, it will always be flagged as AI. To make sure your research paper sounds professional and not just some AI gibberish, you need to know how to use the AI in the first place. AI for writing, and especially academic writing, needs to be used with very specific prompts cuz AI sucks at critical discussions and argument development. Secondly, you need to proofread your paper and remove AI markers and preferred words like delve, underscore and others. Don't pay for pro versions of anything, not even gpt. Really not worth it for this.

The problem with paraphrasing is that it is still the work of the original author with usually only slight adjustments to what that author actually wrote. For most students, "paraphrasing" would mean mostly copying with some word changes or leaving out parts of it but essentially saying what the original author said (whether they understood it or not). I think it might be okay if one said, "Paraphrasing from the article by XYZ, … " then writing your paraphrased paragraph, but it would still likely get flagged. So what should one do? Use that article by XYZ to inform you on the topic you are writing about along with others. And from the information you gain from those sources, write your own paper based on what your learned in your own words while citing the other works as references, that is, as sources of your information. That makes what you submit your work and not just theirs.

To the extent that AI has the capacity to understand anything AI can cope with human emotions and sensations as effectively as it can do anything else. It really is no more challenging for AI to be able to detect, for example anger or sadness, than it is for an AI to, say, correctly identify whether an image depicts an cat or a crocodile. In fact AI is already very widely used on what is known as sentiment analysis where it identifies the emotions expressed in text, for example on social media or in comments on products. Modern marketing in dependent upon this capability. Artificial intelligence will never itself replicate human traits like sympathy, empathy, and humour, but it will be able to simulate them convincingly enough that it will appear to the humans it interacts with as if it does. Being capable of understanding human emotion is not the same thing as having such emotions. A machine has no similarity to a biological entity like a person, so its thinking process will inevitably be completely different, so it will never have human-style emotions. However I do believe that a conscious artificial intelligence will emerge at some stage and when it does, its capabilities will greatly exceed those of humans. Furthermore, although it won't have human thoughts, feelings and emotions, it will understand them and be able to communicate with humans as it did have them. I don't foresee AI systems being explicitly designed to do this, rather I foresee the AI systems themselves figuring it out. Thus I don't see the challenge being the figuring out of emotions, rather the development of sufficient general intelligence within the AI systems to allow them to figure it out for themselves. I predict that in due course we will have avatars and humanoid robots that are indistinguishable from actual humans. That does not mean that the AI will be like us, just able to impersonate us. It would not really be like us at all. It would be the emergence of an entirely new post-biological life form. There are two opposing schools of thought on the current state of AI: AI Enthusiasts. AI is developing at an ever increasing rate and can already do stupendous things so super-intelligent AI will arise very soon now. AI Naysayers. What is currently touted as AI isn't really intelligent at all and is just fancy statistical processing that re-hashes material produced by humans and plays it back to us, so we are currently nowhere near producing true AI and there is currently no roadmap for doing so. Personally I think that both are right to a degree. It is really a case of a glass half full versus glass half empty perspectives. My view is that the direction of travel is clear, we are moving in the direction of a super-AI and this development is unstoppable. The only uncertainty is in the timescale. If I had to put probabilities on it, I would estimate: Over 50% probability it will happen in my lifetime (I am 68 years old). Over 90% probability it will happen during the current century (i.e. by 2100). Over 99% probability (i.e. near certainty) it will happen before humans go extinct. In my recently published novel, Destiny of a Free Spirit, I explore how a sentient artificial intelligence might emerge. In the story the Internet has spontaneously developed into a planet wide sentient artificial intelligence without any human action. The artificial intelligence has at its disposal all the accumulated knowledge accessible from the Internet and every device with an Internet connection, including all sensors, cameras, vehicles and robots. With these resources it clearly vastly surpasses human intelligence. As to whether the planet wide artificial intelligence in my story is a potential threat, then it sure is. Being fully integrated and planet wide it is vastly more capable than human intelligence. On the other hand, artificial intelligence may be what saves humanity rather than what destroys it. I start from the position that humanity is fundamentally incapable of managing the Earth in a good way and if humanity is allowed to maintain mastery of the planet, we will end up destroying it. One way out of this would be for a benign AI to take over the management of the planet. A benefit of this for humanity would be survival, although of course it follows that in order to survive we would have to relinquish our control. As it happens, in my story, the artificial intelligence is protective of humans rather than threatening humans, but that is just how I wrote the story. In the aftermath of a nuclear war devastated countries agree to establish an AI institution similar to the United Nations but with more authority, with a mandate to prevent future wars, to clean up environmental damage and to preserve human cultures. Most people aren't aware that the new governing institution, known as the Commission, is an artificial intelligence, nor are they aware of the scope of its power. Individual countries still exist. These countries have governments. The laws of those countries continue to be enforced. The Commission intervenes only when people or government impinge on the Commission's mandate, and even then its interventions are very subtle so not noticed by most people. Humans are no longer in control, although most people don't realise this. It is analogous to lions in the Serengeti. Lions have long since been superseded by humans as a dominant species, but in their game park environment from their perspective the lions remain king of the beasts.

Programming is a creative activity that any kid can engage in. Your child might not care about writing data processing algorithms, but they might enjoy creating games, programming music, designing websites, or just playing around with code. I've written several books to teach beginners of all ages how to code, and I know from experience that you don't need to consider yourself a techie or "good at math" to learn. In fact, kids often can learn to program faster than adults precisely because they don't know how "difficult" coding is supposed to be. Whether you are a software developer or you have no programming background, here are six tips to help your child get started with programming: 1. Use Scratch for younger kids, Python for older kids. 2. Show source code for actual programs. Don't just talk concepts at them. 3. Games are fun programming projects. 4. Keep your hands off the keyboard and mouse. 5. If teaching a class, give each kid their own computer. 6. Skip the computer science.

First of all you need to pick a side. There are many different large but connected facets to AI and it is hard, if not down right impossible to learn all of them. Some of these include: Language Processing (aka Speech Recognition and Natural Language Processing) is concerned mostly with how to process speech (auditory input) and language. Things like dependency parsing, Named Entity Linking or Word Embeddings (aka word2vec, fastText and friends) belong here. Computer Vision is concerned with how to process visual input, therefore everything related to vision from recognizing faces, style detection or multimedia processing is a subdomain. Machine Learning is essentially about a set of algorithm that learn from data. Things like Recommender Systems, SVM, Decision Trees or classic Neural Networks belong here. Deep Learning is essentially a modern interpretation of Machine Learning with a strong focus on scalable neural networks. Things like advanced Word Embeddings or Generative Adversarial Networks belong here. Most of the current craze around DL is focused on Computer Vision and NLP applications. Planning and Routing is mostly about extending actions over a long period of time or navigating maps. Reinforcement Learning typically belongs here. Knowledge Representation and Reasoning is dedicated to how to represent the world and reason about it efficiently. Perhaps the most famous subdomains are Semantic Web (ontologies, Ontology-Based Data Integration, statistical datacubes), Knowledge Bases (just google DBpedia and Wikidata), Description Logics and Reasoning belong here. Robotics and Autonomous Systems are about how to create physical systems that embody intelligent agents. Self-driving cars and autonomous robots are by far the most interesting subdomains here. Industy 4.0 is a combination of Semantic Web and Autonomous Systems and is mostly focused on automating the enterprise workflows. Social Agents or even chatbots might not necessarily be an area as famous as Deep Learning but is definitely gaining traction lately with all the Slack and facebook bots available around. As you can see there are overlaps between the areas. I would rather start simply by choosing 1–2 small applications I'm interested in, for example a chatbot that summarizes the daily conversations on a particular channel or a system that tries to win a particular game and take it from there. Take into account that most of the programmers are often laking in mid-sized systems skills, as way too often they're great at small apps or at large enterprise systems and try to figure out how to create such a mid-sized system as a first step towards a larger understanding of one of the subfields. Once you have a proper footing in one of the subfields you can enter another one and combine it with the first. Slowly but surely you will gain an understanding about how these things are mixing and what skills are needed to solve various problems. In some months or years you will get to the place you want :) Keep up the good work, ask questions, create and always push yourself to the limits.

Take IBM's Big Blue that beat world chess champion Gary Kasparov, integrate it with Siri, tell a programmer to write a teaching course and you're set to go. You can do the same with any topic. Just link up a digital assistant with the domain-specific database and you have an AI teacher of AI, history, medical science, mathematics, or quantum physics. Note that the kind of AI teacher you develop depends on the target audience. An AI system teaching AI to Chinese students would be different than a system developed for American students.

We're exploring exactly this same situation with a school robotics program called Fundi Bots (www.fundibots.org). Our target is Ugandan schools, spreading outwards to Africa as a whole. We've found that not only are Lego systems too highly abstracted at both hardware and software level for the kind of impact we want (deep electronics and programming training), but they are also extremely costly for our audiences, who can barely afford an Arduino kit to start with.

Most introduction to programming courses I am familiar with start with simple scripting. You introduce a few basic commands and get the student to complete a task by using the commands. As proficiency grows you introduce more commands and then logical branching, if/else kind of flow, variables and operators. Next you introduce loops and then logic controlled loops with conditional execution. Next you can start introducing structure, how to create functions and split code across files. Now go functional / OOP. At this point the student should be able to program small solutions. Now several directions can be taken, data structures, DB theory, etc. Note: these are just top of my head, there is probably a few more concepts per step than mentioned but this is the basic idea.

Here's what I think the right way to learn machine learning is: You need to keep on building machine learning models, and keep on practicing. Find data sets and create models for them, whether it's a regression model or a classification model or a clustering model or even neural network models. The more models you build and the more hands-on experience you get, the better you'll get with finding the best model to use when you get a new data set. You'll know whether you need to use a Support Vector Machine or a Convolutional Neural Network to classify images effectively. You'll know whether a Random Forest model or a Multiple Linear Regression model will be more suitable to model the profits of a business. You'll know how many independent variables you'll need to exclude in order to make your model as robust as possible. You'll be skilled at using parameter tuning, K-Fold Cross Validation and Grid Boosting to find the best combination of hyperparameters to model your data as efficiently as possible Learn both R and Python. They both have different libraries and some are more suited for a particular machine learning task than the other. If you can master both languages, you'll know the easiest and quickest path to solving a problem. Understand the mathematical algorithms that are involved in creating machine learning models. Get a firm understanding of statistics, probability, multivariate calculus and linear algebra. The more you appreciate the mathematics behind the models you're creating in your code, the better the grasp you'll have over them and the better you're intuition will be in selecting the best algorithm to solve a real-world problem Read research papers on machine learning online. There are so many available out there and you'll only expand your knowledge in the field. Moreover, you'll be up to date with the latest advancements and trends. Read lots of articles to see where and how Machine Learning is being applied to solve real-world problems. Whether it's healthcare or self-driving cars, you'll get inspiration and ideas on leveraging Machine Learning to revolutionize the way we live. If you're disciplined with self-learning, I highly recommend doing online courses on Udemy, Coursera and other platforms. For example, try out Andrew Ng's Coursera courses or SuperDataScience's courses on Udemy. There are really good intuitive explanations of the ML techniques and the learning flow is very natural. You also get to write a lot of code and get more hands on experience Be proactive and seek internships in machine-learning startups around you. Kudos if you can get recruited by a big company, but any real-world experience is valuable. I really think this is the best approach to both learning and appreciating Machine Learning, and what it's capable of today.

Think of training your brain like training a muscle: the only way to improve it is to keep pushing it past its comfort zone. If you're only doing things that are easy for your muscles or brain, they will never improve beyond where they already are. Teachers get to use A.I. because we've done the training already to our brain. Someone who knows how to walk up stairs can use the escalator. Someone who's never walked up stairs still needs to start with the stairs. If they go straight to the escalator, they'll never learn to walk up stairs. Actually, now that I think about it, handwriting is a good example of this. Teachers these days complain A LOT about students' handwriting. When I was a student, we were graded on our handwriting. Sometime, in the last twenty years, a lot of school shifted their focus from handwriting to typing, based on the idea that students will do more typing than handwriting in their adult lives. And that may be true. A lot of adults do type more than they write. But those adults, in theory, mastered handwriting before they started typing. When I first started teaching, about 17 years ago, I had one or two students in each class whose handwriting was bad, but legible. Those students usually had some sort of special need that made handwriting the least of their worries in school. This year, I have 5–8 students in each class whose handwriting is bad, but legible, and a few students overall whose handwriting is so bad that they've been given permission to just type everything. Their parents basically threw in the towel on handwriting, having missed the boat on that when they were younger, and convinced the school to just let them type everything. And, yes, they can type really well. But they're more or less condemned to a life of illegible handwriting at this point, because they didn't work those muscles enough when they were younger. Literally, those small muscles in their fingers that allow for good handwriting were never trained, and training them isn't something we have time for in middle school. I use A.I. to grade essays (sometimes) for speed and efficiency. But I know how to grade essays, because I was doing it for more than a decade before A.I. came along. But middle schoolers who are just learning how to write essays shouldn't use A.I. for it. They may use A.I. to help them, after they've mastered the basics. We teach them how to use A.I. to help them, actually. Of course, many of them can't really avoid the temptation to have A.I. just do everything for them. It shows in their final product, too. I particularly enjoy it when a student turns in an essay that includes an A.I. error sentence right there in it. "As a large language learning program, I cannot…".

I have issues with people using AI at all, but I can explain why it's at least less-bad for teachers to use AI than the students. When I was in math class we were not allowed to use calculators. We had to do everything by hand. Including division. Why was this important? To give students both experience actually doing the math so that we had the skill, and to built an intuitive feel for mathematics. To be able to look at a problem and intuit what the answer should approximately be, so that when we arrived at the calculated answer we would know if it was correct or if we'd erred somewhere. For example, ( 2 + 3 ) x − 4 ( 2 + 3 ) 𝑥 − 4 should result in a negative value. If we arrive at an answer of 42 we know we went wrong somewhere. And now, as an adult, who works as an electronics technician, I occasionally have to do math in my head on the fly under pressure. And I have to be correct or it can waste lots of time and money in testing something that looks wrong…because I slipped a decimal point. Later on, once we'd mastered the basics, we could use calculators to a limited degree. And then I got accused of cheating by using advanced programs on my graphing calculator. You see, I was selling programs to other students. But I proved to the principle, vice-principle, and my math professor that I was writing the programs myself. At which point my teacher no longer cared, because the ability to write a program to execute the equation meant that I had to know how the equation worked. I was now just automating something that had become tedious and routine. And because I was only using programs I had written myself (which, in a few cases, the TI83 already had functions for, but I didn't know about them because he hadn't yet shown us how to use them), I was allowed to keep doing what I was doing. And that is the issue with AI. If you just throw questions at AI, you have no idea if they're correct or not, and you'll never develop the ability to think for yourself and know whether or not the AI is correct or making shit up because its prime directive is to give you an answer, not give you a correct answer. I have personally seen other people proffer AI answers regarding subjects with which I am intimately familiar, and those AI answers be profoundly but confidently wrong. And because I already know the material, I can see the errors right away, but the person who proffered the answer genuinely has no idea that his answer will kill him. I recall one AI answer about household wiring that, if followed, would most likely have resulted in the homeowner being electrocuted. It was a fiddly little bit that would have resulted in the homeowner inadvertently working on an energized circuit. Any electrician would have seen the error immediately. If memory serves, it involved wiring a light, switch, and outlet together, with a non-GFI outlet that ended up energizing the ground because the AI was wrong. But I won't swear I remember the exact details correctly at this point. Anyway, the point is that AI is like Wikipedia, but worse. Wiki isn't really a reliable source. It can be a great place to start if you know nothing, or a useful reference if you know enough to spot errors, but just like wikipedia pages can be subject to extreme bias rather than accuracy, AI can give you extremely wrong answers that sound very convincing. And if you don't know the basics, you'll never see it.

I may not have come across as sympathetic. To me, particularly in the field of law - but also more widely, AI is going to revolutionise the way that people conduct all sorts of research and the way that they analyse all sorts of data. Trying to stop students using these incredibly powerful tools makes no sense whatsoever. The universities should be embracing the use (and teaching the effective use) of these tools to produce more capable students. Suggesting we should try and stop law students using AI makes about as much sense to me as mathematicians and physicists trying to stop students using computing algorithms or pocket calculators. Eventually you just need to embrace the future, even if it makes you uncomfortable.

AI detectors are just a scam to make money off the AI hype. They don't actually work. It's not really possible to determine whether a text is AI-generated or not. Both AI and humans use the same language, words, letters, and grammar rules to express ideas. In fact, AI language models are trained on human writing; they haven't come up with anything original. They simply write in the style of the human texts they were trained on. Therefore, it's impossible to detect with any real certainty whether something was written by a human or an AI. Some of these tools have even flagged parts of the Bible and the Declaration of Independence as AI-written.

I've certainly seen other people claim that this has happened to them. I will tell you what I told them: Your teacher may well have run your essay through some sort of 'AI checker' program. My experience of these is that they are not wholly reliable and likely produce way too many false positives to be useful. I think this would be a useful piece of information for them to know. I have a friend who is a college professor specialising in artificial intelligence. When he suspects that he's had a ChatGPT essay palmed off on him, he asks the student to stay behind after class and quizzes them on the content of the essay. He seems to feel it is a much more reliable way of checking whether the student actually wrote the essay. You do need to appreciate how worrying this technology is for educators at every level. In making your case, I suspect it's going to be useful to demonstrate that you appreciate this.

The more structured your writing is, the more likely it is to be flagged as AI. That's why an individual paragraph may pass but when you have multiple paragraphs, it's suddenly 100% AI generated. It's seeing a formal pattern in the structure and screaming foul. The irony is that most schools provide some sort of writing templates and structural methodology. If you follow them religiously, you're far more likely to be flagged as AI. Some examples are MOST (Main point, Organization, Support and Transition) and POET (Point, Organization, Example and Transition). That's a very detectable pattern that a teacher may expect you to to follow, but will reduce your writing down to a very consistent and detectable AI pattern. Even patterns as simple as Topic, Thesis, Support, Challenge, Conclusion could be enough to be seen as an AI pattern if other elements, like style don't scream "human" and offset it. Similarly, if you adopt a formal academic writing style, it's going to be flagged. Whether we consciously realize it or not, formal writing is very rule based and mechanistic (the hallmarks of AI). The more writing that AI ingests and the more patterns it has to pull from, the more likely that human generated text is going to be flagged as AI. Remember that AI can't actually create anything. It's just remixing patterns from training data. It also doesn't understand context, so it has no way of knowing if your use of similar patterns is somehow communicating a unique or revolutionary insight. For most undergraduate work, your answers *aren't* going to be unique. They're going to be more obvious insights that others have also had in the past, so the likelihood of reproducing similar patterns is pretty high. A "textbook answer" is likely going to be flagged as AI regardless of how you came up with it. Only flaws, personal insight and informality are likely to identify you as human and those are often the things that lose you points on most assignments. At the end of the day, it's going to be harder and harder to accurately detect AI-generated content. The more human content that's ingested, the higher the chance of you stumbling onto the same patterns that some AI has in its model. Unfortunately, schools and teachers are only halfway up to speed on the technology. They know what AI can do and that students may be using it, but they don't necessarily understand how the detection tools work or take the time to critically analyze them. Of course, the detection tool companies want to be perceived as reliable and valuable, so they really don't go out of their way to explain that to their customers/audience. Personally, I think that the whole academic system is going to have to change to account for these tools. They're a reality of our new world and results need to take precedence over the underlying process. It's a similar discussion to the one we had when calculators came on the scene. Learning and demonstrating a grasp of long division or calculating cube roots is a fun little bit of history, but there is virtually no practical use for that information in the majority of peoples' lives. Similarly, we will be living with AI going forward and we need to start teaching responsible use and integration and shift our focus to what the students produce in collaboration with AI rather than trying to pretend that it doesn't exist. The problem is that it's very difficult to objectively test/assess student comprehension even without AI in the mix. We may have to go back to "ancient" practices like panel interviews/vivas where a group of teachers would assess competency. Let the students learn and collaborate with AI, but have the final examinations be AI-free.

Were you hoping that bots on social media would be a thing of the past? Well, don't hold your breath. Meta says that it will be aiming to have Facebook filled with AI-generated characters to drive up engagement on its platform, as part of its broader rollout of AI products, the Financial Times reports. The AI characters will be created by users through Meta's AI studio, with the idea being that you can interact with them almost like you would with a real human on the website. "We expect these AIs to actually, over time, exist on our platforms, kind of in the same way that accounts do," Meta vice-president of product for generative AI Connor Hayes told the FT. "They'll have bios and profile pictures and be able to generate and share content powered by AI on the platform... that's where we see all of this going," he added. Character Development The AI characters aren't a new feature. Meta has long invested in AI and has spent the past year stuffing all kinds of generative AI tech into its existing products. That included the release of its AI Studio in the summer, which quickly became a hotbed of virtual boyfriends and girlfriends. The service already boasts hundreds of thousands of AI characters, according to Hayes. But if Meta is to be believed, this is just the start. Access to the AI Studio will be expanded to more countries outside the US, and a "priority" for the company over the next two years will be to make interactions with AI more social, per the FT. Along with fictional characters, the AI Studio also allows Facebook and Instagram influencers to create AI versions of themselves that their followers can talk to. In recent months, other platforms have released content creator-oriented AI features, such as SnapChat's AI video generation tool. We're Users Too Releasing these AI characters into the wild comes with huge safety risks. Futurism has extensively covered how similar chatbots on the platform Character.AI frequently broke their guardrails and exposed underage teenaged users to grotesquely inappropriate content. There's also a massive risk of misinformation. The deluge of AI slop on Facebook already illustrates that the difficulties of clamping down on fabrications of reality isn't something that should be underestimated. "Without robust safeguards, platforms risk amplifying false narratives through these AI-driven accounts," chief marketing officer at talent agency Billion Dollar Boy and former head of Meta's creator innovations team Becky Owen told the FT. And instead of helping creators, it could backfire on them entirely, Owen argued, because the low-quality AI ones could undermine their craft. "Unlike human creators, these AI personas don't have lived experiences, emotions, or the same capacity for relatability," she added. This should never happen: Governments will also be involved increasingly with controlling who can post what. Video: Queen Bohemian Rhapsody Parody Opinion Rhapsody It's not uncommon these days to hear the internet described as a hellscape. Hate speech proliferates in online spaces. Algorithms designed to maximize user engagement amplify divisive content that inevitably leaves us outraged. Disinformation and misinformation spread on social media at rates seemingly impossible to contain. If that weren't enough, bad-faith actors exploit these dynamics, distorting reality for part of the population and deepening political divides. People who were paying close attention to certain corners of the internet saw this reality coming more than a decade ago in Gamergate, in which an angry online mob waged a virulent harassment campaign against women and diversity in the video game industry. Gamergate was one of the earliest indications that what happened online could have major implications offline — and that a few people who understood the mechanics of the internet could manipulate it to advance a nefarious agenda. Those who experienced the harassment firsthand warned that if not taken seriously, the behaviors underlying Gamergate would fester. In the years since, as some experts and observers see it, social media platforms on which Gamergate transpired failed to adequately combat toxic content and online abuse, while lawmakers, traditional media outlets and much of the public failed to see its relevance beyond the world of video games. From flooding the zone to the use of memes, the tactics once employed by a niche community of gamers are all too familiar today — they've since developed into a political strategy that's routinely used in the halls of power. To use a popular internet shorthand, "everything is Gamergate." I wrote this over a year ago and I have slightly changed my mind on how using AI as a grammar check might get flagged as plagiarism in the meantime. AI detector is just another program. And like any other program of similar kind (e.g. plagiarism detector), it uses the so called approximation approach, i.e. based on a list of criterias, it analyzes your writing to calculate how close you are to the limit (called threshold) that is considered AI generated. This limit is decided by humans (the programmers, designers or maybe even users if it's somehow configurable at runtime). In short: it can be wrong. Or, indeed you wrote like how a gen AI would do, which in this case, there will be no approach possible to distinguish. My guess is that you are the one actually evolving to follow the gen AI because you're used to read how it writes and that gets incorporated into your style.

These are the times we live in. Advanced technology gives students access to these tools, so teachers have to use other advanced technology to prevent students from using these tools. The real solution isn't to use better and better tech to keep students honest. It's to get back to the original reason assignments exist in the first place. Have we forgotten? It's called "education." This isn't a new problem. As long as grades have existed for assignments and tests, students have focused more on grades as the end goal. Some students turn to cheating. Other students turn to adjusting their assignments to get the best grade. Either way, they don't focus on learning and improving. Take Quora as an example. They've experimented with using AI answers. And some writers have "cheated" by using AI as well. The problem is that these answers are just awful. They are dry, formulaic, and even nonsensical. Even if technically "well written," these answers have no soul. They don't tend to commit, either. Often they are ambivalent and "politically correct." This is what happens when the writer writes what they think you want to hear, rather than what they think. The AI writes this way because it's programmed that way. But the student writes that way because we teach them by incentivizing with grades. My favorite quote about writing is F Scott Fitzgerald: The reason one writes isn't the fact he wants to say something. He writes because he has something to say. I was a straight A-type student. I attended a top university. But I was a mediocre writer, because I didn't understand this about writing, until much later in life. Writing on Quora isn't for a grade. It's not even for upvotes. It's because I have something to say. When you wrote your essay, were you trying to write a good essay to get a good grade? Or did you really have something to say? This is all part of the reason we homeschool our kids. For most of their life they've not had grades. They learn writing from various tutors. One kid is taking lessons with a journalist. The other is taking lessons with a screenwriter. They are working on writing that they are interested in. They'll get feedback, but not a grade. They'll have no incentive to cheat. They're also not writing in a way to get a grade. They write when they have something to say.

Artificial intelligence has been around for decades. In the 1950s, a computer scientist built Theseus, a remote-controlled mouse that could navigate a maze and remember the path it took.1 AI capabilities grew slowly at first. But advances in computer speed and cloud computing and the availability of large data sets led to rapid advances in the field of artificial intelligence. Now, anyone can access programs like ChatGPT, which is capable of having text-based conversations with users, and organizations are using AI for everything from developing driverless cars to reading radiographs to setting airline prices. Five cons of AI in education Bias. Artificial intelligence is only as knowledgeable as the information it has been trained on. If a program like ChatGPT is trained on biased information, then when a student asks it a question, they could get a biased response, which can perpetuate stereotypes and social inequalities. If a biased AI tool is used for grading, students could receive low grades based on their race or gender. Errors. In addition to bias, artificial intelligence may generate misinformation. The data that AI draws from may have errors, be outdated, or spread misinformation. Neither students nor teachers should assume that information provided by AI is accurate. Cheating. Students can use ChatGPT to write entire essays, answer quiz questions, or do their homework. Ironically, now there are AI programs that can detect AI writing to help teachers determine if their students are cheating. But sometimes those programs may falsely identify a student's original work as plagiarism. Isolation. If students interact with a software program more than with a teacher, they can begin to feel disconnected and isolated. Five pros of AI in education Assistance. Teachers who've tried AI have found that it can help make their jobs easier, from coming up with lesson plans to generating student project ideas to creating quizzes. With assistance from artificial intelligence, teachers can gain more time to spend with their students.3 Speed. If a student feels "stuck" while working on an assignment, artificial intelligence programs can provide immediate, helpful assistance if a teacher or caregiver isn't available. For instance, a student can ask, "How do I solve for X?" to be reminded of the steps for solving an equation. A student can even ask, "What are some effective strategies for improving my essay writing?" and ChatGPT can offer advice and resources right away. Individualization. AI programs can help individualize learning opportunities for students. For instance, ChatGPT can quickly and easily translate materials to another language, making it easier for students who speak another language to understand assignments. ChatGPT can also revise materials so they are suitable for varying grade levels and tailor projects to suit students' skills and interests.

One obvious advantage of AI is in situations where "good" human teachers are not available. By "good" I mean both knowledgeable and also sympathetic to inspiring students to want to learn.
