On the other side, a lot of school ass—-ignments do not inspire the student, do they? Given a free choice, the student would choose to not do them. Then they can USE AI like they used to use encyclopedias: as resources for their own original activity. Teach students skill in using AI. I was taught to learn facts. AI is not intelligent; it just computes. I asked the Bing AI why the mountain K2 is called "K2" and it outputted what looks to me like the correct answer. Here is an example of motivating students. Some education researchers once collected a number of teenage boys who had zero interest in school and were far behind grade in reading level. So they got broken down cars for them to repair and gave them repair manuals to read. They quickly learned to read the repair manuals to fix the cars. We can guess what would have happened if the researchers had tried to improve their reading skill by ass—-igning them to read a Charles Dickens novel. Or here's another one: In physics class have the students do the experiments and TELL THEM THE ANSWERS. I have been doing this for many years. Over that time I have had mixed results. Sometimes I'll run a campaign sure that I'll produce an amazing ROI, only to have it fall completely flat. Other times I'll whip up a simple regression model in about five minutes, only to have the target group massively outperform the control. I have not found a magic bullet - a simple test that tells me reliably how much difference my work is going to make. Instead I'm going to list below some of the things I've done which have worked, and some that were not so successful: Simple profile based targeting. Take the customers that bought a product and a random sample of those that bought something else. Grab a bunch of customer variables about them from prior to the purchase and run it through a simple binary classifier. Take those given a high probability of purchase who haven't purchased, and send them an offer. Help out the creative team. Take an approach similar to the previous one, but instead of building a list instead craft a persona of the kind of people that buy. Take this to creative and they'll write a far better email. Improve your profile using segmentation. One issue I have with the profile is it tends to be very 'middle-of-the-road'. Chuck it through k-means or something more sophisticated and you'll get as many subgroups of customers as you feel like. The more you get, the better profile you'll be able to create but the more effort creative will need to put in writing different copy. Most of the time I go with 4–6 but I've done as few as 2. Churn modelling. Find people likely to churn and make sure you look after them That's probably the bread 'n' butter work that I'd do every day. Beyond that and it starts to get a bit more interesting. Collaborative filtering is really starting to come of age. Product recommendation is the obvious example, but a different one is having a bank of different brand messages and selecting the best one using UBCF. One of the things that upsets me the most with email marketing is people view it as a soapbox rather than a communication tool. Text mining enables you to glean, at scale, what your customers are thinking and start responding based on what they actually say rather than quietly watching from the shadows like big brother. Continuing on from text mining is NLG. Chatbots are years ahead of email marketing in this regard which provides a nice opportunity to steal all their good results. This means you really are having proper conversations with the customer and listening to what they say. Journey planning is a relatively new field. You can speed things up or slow them down to match what the customer wants. I did this for house buying and it worked pretty well. Sometimes people can be in the market for years and then suddenly take off, so standard journey planning tools struggle. You can flip it around and start applying pressure to the business. These products are not effective, those ones are sabotaging your revenue. Don't just take the products that marketing gives you - instead start saying 'we have this segment that you're not serving effectively - give me something that works for them'. Custom pricing is popular but I've never had much luck with it. Estimate an individual's elasticity and you could send the same product at different price-points. I know other people that have succeeded with it - mainly in the charity space - but I personally have had more luck offering different products based on my guess of the customer's disposable income. I'm a big fan of looking after the customer base rather than maximising the revenue per email. I like to measure everyone's engagement and then find the best offer for them rather than taking the offers and finding the people that will give the biggest ROI. In the short term this is worse but in the long term the large customer base is really powerful. Those would be the main ones. One point is that very little of this ties back to email. That just happens to be the chanel that I've used most often. I haven't talked about the future at all. One of my big bugbears is that you can work using intuition, or using AI, but there is no effective way to combine the two. I have a vision of marketers wielding AI like an ironman suit, where the AI helps the marketer execute their ideas far more effectively rather than trying to take over from the marketer. Too often I have been disappointed by the results of the AI system. I produce an AI profile and then it turns out the profile is not predictive as the market conditions have changed too much. Marketers don't make that mistake, they're in tune with the market and adapt before changes are present in the data. That's why I think AI assisted is the way to go rather than AI driven.

Artificial Intelligence is about algorithms. Therefore the limitations of A.I. is similar to those of algorithms. Undecidable Problems. These problems are the theoretically impossible to solve — by any algorithm. The halting problem is a decision problem (with a yes or no answer) that is undecidable. A computer cannot tell if it is in an infinite loop or it will at some point stop! Intractable Problems. These problems are theoretically impossible to solve in a reasonable time — i.e., there are known algorithmic solutions, but the algorithms are too inefficient/slow to solve the problem when the number of inputs grows large. IMO, the limitations of A.I. are the available resources and computation performance for the algorithm.

Since we can't look into a crystal ball and see the future of artificial neural networks, it is difficult to conclude that there are any limitations. I suspect that this question could be reworded and made to ask about specific types of implementations. If you were talking about specifics, I could perhaps offer more insight into the potential of AI. Certainly, if an intelligence or consciousness is indeed artificial, it cannot experience objectively what humans experience. An AI won't be able to truly empathize or relate to the lives and dreams of humans. One limitation would be the ability to discern and appreciate meaning as humans do. An AI could estimate what you may find meaningful in your own life, it could even take actions based on that estimate. It couldn't however understand or compare the meaning or source of the meaning with its own experience. The state of AI today relating to capability is the following: AI can at best reflect on data which represents the activities of humans and the properties of the universe and make educated and useful predictions. Although data scientists have achieved amazing results based on AIs ability to predict and classify information, currently AI can't derive meaning from it's predictions and invent novel new systems of prediction. In other words, in general and without assistance from humans, AI can't decide what to work on and build and refine its own specialized subsystems. This is a grey area and probably one of the keys to developing a successful AGI. In general, the activity that makes our brains special is the ability to choose what to work on. We can also reinforce the various systems within out minds to specialize and we can train our brains to perform other tasks that we believe are important. Let's say that scientists invented an AI that was able to predict the revenue potential of all of the existing markets and estimate the markets that are likely to grow and offer the best profitability in the future. This would be a powerful tool for startups and investors but the AI would be unable to respond in a useful way to the conclusion or output. Let's assume that the AI discovers that the most profitable market is renewable energy. Data scientists might be inclined to create a new AI that can analyze the energy market however the AI would be unable to perform this analysis without the intervention of humans. This thought experiment is a useful analogy that roughly explains the current state of AI in generic terms. In the future we may find that we can build systems that can analyze the markets and respond more intuitively. What if the AI decided that the best profit would be achieved by eradicating all life an earth and turning it into a giant solar farm? How would we build a value system into the AI so that it best represents human interests. How would we teach the AI to create a harmonious balance between our interests as entrepreneurs and the interests of other humans and other natural life? How would we motivate AI to avoid destroying our other AIs and other technologies that exist? Humans have biases because they are advantageous. You have a bias toward survival and therefore you have a preference to not be locked in a cage with wild jungle cat. You recognize that other humans probably also have this bias and thus you have a bias (or preference) that dissuades you from locking your friend in a cage with a wild jungle cat. AI, similarly must have biases or it cannot make useful predictions. How do we impart biases into AI systems that allow it to make predictions while simultaneously avoiding damaging and/or harmful biases? We can only speculate and make educated guesses about the limitations of AI in the future. There are some obvious limitations and there are also potential limitations that we find hard to define until we prove that they exist. When we achieve an AI that is capable of creating other AI's or implementing its own subsystems, we will be one step closer to mimicking the human brain. I believe this will happen and to some extent, already is. The point is that unless an AI can assume a state of consciousness that approximates human consciousness, there will be a vast number of limitations. Particularly if we consider all of the aspects of the human experience and subsequently consider the value system of humans.

Because of it, email teams can now use AI to personalize content, predict engagement, choose optimal send times, and ultimately, drive conversions. Many industries, including banking, healthcare, and retail, have already started to integrate AI into their marketing initiatives. Email marketing is one such sector in which AI has had a particularly revolutionary impact. Regardless of sector, AI can assist email marketers in better understanding their customers. As a result, they'll send more targeted emails that are more inclined to be opened and clicked on. It is an essential, helpful, and profitable tool in the competitive email marketing sector. Email marketing has improved significantly with the use of artificial intelligence. Automation, personalization, and data analysis have been applied to generate benefits such as higher conversions. AI has effectively bridged the gap between conventional and online buying by personalizing 1:1 interactions. Artificial Intelligence can help email teams understand deep insights, such as: i. Which subject lines perform better. ii. What times recipients tend to open emails. iii. What the optimal frequency for sending emails is. iv. When to send an abandoned cart email. v. Which new segments to include in your email list. vi. How recipients interact with your emails. vii. How to personalize future content to better engage subscribers. In a nutshell, AI can help email teams know what kind of email to send, to whom, when, and how often. However, AI doesn't replace marketers or developers but rather helps streamline their processes and analyze (and use) big data in real time. 4 BENEFITS OF AI FOR EMAIL MARKETING AI is a game-changer for email marketing teams, whether you're a team of 1 or 100. Here are a few of the benefits of using AI in your email program: 1. PERSONALIZATION AI learns about each of your customers: their history, engagement behaviors, interests, and habits. It then uses this information to determine what product recommendations, discounts, or freebies will best resonate with each recipient on your list. So when someone visits your website and browses a certain selection of items, AI can retarget that potential customer via email if they abandon their cart before checkout. It'll know what items they were looking at and let them know if there's been a price change, discount, or it's close to selling out. This level of personalization goes beyond adding someone's first name to an email. It engages with them based on their behaviors, increasing their chance of converting to a customer. 2. EFFICIENCY. Humans can do practically anything you ask your email marketing AI to do—it just takes a money-wasting amount of time. Email automations and AI optimizations let your email marketing teams focus on big-picture problem-solving and solutions rather than choosing individual send times or sifting through product recommendations to send to a single customer on your list of 200,000. AI also analyzes your email programs' performance and customer behavior in real time, which means you can use the data from yesterday's email campaign to inform tomorrow's send. And you can accomplish this without having a team of dedicated data scientists constantly sifting through your analytics to pull out email optimization insights. 3. OPTIMIZATION AI can also help your email program, even if your program is already crushing it. When optimizing an email, AI makes teeny-weeny tweaks that add up to a big difference. For example, AI can help optimize your email program by: i. Adjusting the send time to better align with customer behavior ii. Recommending a different subject line that'll likely get a higher open rate iii. Segmenting your audience to increase the likelihood of engagement and conversion 4. AUTOMATION You and your marketing team won't always be at your computer when a customer needs help or an email recipient joins your email list. Nor should you manually route customers through a journey that AI can guide them. AI-powered email automation can help you send the right content, to the right recipient, at the right time. It can use prewritten copy, promotions, links, and blog post snippets to engage your audience without any human intervention. Plus, your AI-powered email campaigns continue to improve themselves by consuming data and making changes. So if an automated email doesn't perform well, AI will adjust future campaigns to improve the content, timing, or promotion. AI EMAIL MARKETING TOOLS There are several AI-based email marketing tools to help improve the email experience for senders and recipients alike. Here are a few of those tools: 1. EMAIL VALIDATION: The email validation tool scans your list to detect and prevent invalid email addresses, creating a clean list that reduces your bounce rates and improves your deliverability. The email validation tool also helps users submit their correct email address the first time, eliminating typos and lost leads. 2. NEURAL PROTECTION: The neural protection increases your deliverability rates by identifying risky sending patterns. The AI monitors your sending behaviors and inspects outgoing messages to spot ways you might be hurting your reputation with inbox service providers (ISPs). It also intelligently paces your sending and schedules retries for each ISP (as needed) to ensure maximum inboxing with each provider. 3. DELIVERABLITY INSIGHT: The AI scans your real-time metrics and email performance to provide recommendations and actionable insights. This dynamic email advice helps improve your email deliverability and boost the overall success of your email program.

AI can be a powerful tool for small businesses, but it also poses some risks. According to the World Economic Forum, some of the biggest risks of AI include privacy concerns, potential bias, and the spread of misinformation. Additionally, AI solutions may be unable to provide the same level of empathy as human representatives, which can lead to minor customer dissatisfaction. To address these risks, organizations need to weigh the pros against the cons of adopting AI solutions, such as the cost of implementation and maintenance, privacy concerns, and potential bias. Here are some ways to address the challenges of adopting AI in small businesses: Invest in relevant training for your team: Understanding AI's complexities is crucial. A practical step to improve AI adoption is investing in relevant training for your team. This empowers your staff, accelerates AI implementation, and drives business innovation. Foster a culture of collaboration and communication: Leaders can foster a culture of collaboration and communication, emphasizing that AI augments human capabilities. This approach promotes employee involvement, addresses concerns, and highlights the benefits of AI, which improves adoption and creates support. Start small: Start with low-risk, low-cost projects and use a small dataset for your first project. If you need to, adjust along the way. Look into low-cost or even free software solutions. Then, expand to bigger initiatives after you get some measurable results that prove AI's value to your business (and confirm your strategy is working). It's important to note that the path to successfully implementing AI in an organization is not insurmountable. With careful consideration and planning, small businesses can reap the benefits of AI while minimizing the risks.

As the Senior AI Advisor at a university, that is exactly what I was hired to find out. In this newly created position, my role is to advise college management and academic officials on how we can best use AI to advance the goals of higher education, integrate the new technology into our classrooms, and improve the overall educational experience for faculty and students. Universities, however, are so much more than just classrooms, libraries and computer labs. They are major centres for research, technology, innovation, development, and collaboration. They are incubators for business and social enterprise startups. They are think tanks that look to solve some of society's toughest problems, from pollution to disease to poverty to global warming and climate change. In short, universities are knowledge hubs that bring together the best and brightest to advance society and figure out ways to improve lives for people. Universities are about finding solutions to problems and thrive on knowledge and innovation. They look for ways to leverage and integrate the latest technology into their education and learning environment. This includes Artificial Intelligence and generative AI tools such as chatbots and voice and image generators. While many may view AI as a threat to education or institutions of higher learning, one has to remember that universities have had to navigate similar issues around technology in the past. There was a time when calculators, as a new technology, were viewed as a threat to higher learning. The same thing happened when personal computers first emerged in the early 1980s, and then came along the Internet, Yahoo, Google, and Wikipedia. Similarly, laptop computers, tablets, cell phones and texting all changed the way students worked in the classroom and interacted with each other, their professors, and learning environment. What do all these technologies have in common? They were all viewed at one time to be a disrupter to higher education, or a threat to learning and society. Yet far from destroying education, it proved to be a useful tool for students to support their learning experience. There's no doubt that these new technologies, or advances in technology, changed how professors went about teaching or the way students learned, but they were hardly a threat to universities or education in general. The result was a better educational experience for students, as universities and professors found ways to not only incorporate the technology into the academic environment but enhance the overall learning experience. Let's take the Internet, search engines and Wikipedia as the closest example to the impact AI is having on higher education. There was a time when students may have had to spend hours in the campus library just researching a topic and gathering information from different sources, before actually doing the real work of studying and writing about it. Then with the new Internet tools what used to take hours took only minutes. What changed? Expectations. While students could do their research far faster than ever before, remember that they also had to sift through a lot more information, not all of which was relevant or useful for their research, and some of it may have been from dubious or unreliable sources. Remember also that now all students now had that ability to use the same tools from the campus library, if not their own home. (However, keep in mind in early days of the Internet few students had high-speed Internet, or even any Internet access, or even the right computers or expertise to use the technology). Faculty now had far higher expectations from students in terms of research, as the the focus shifted more to how students used the information they compiled for their work. The result was it actually enhanced the learning experience, but academic standards were raised to reflect the new research tools and meet those higher expectations. This changed the way students worked, learned and, in some cases, even how they were evaluated and graded for their work. In these situations, the new technology actually contributed to the development of knowledge rather than threaten or destroy it. One of the key aims of higher education is to always use the very latest in technology and computers, and figure out how to work with new technology to improve the classroom and learning experience. Finding new ways to use technology supports another strategic area of higher education. Innovation. Colleges constantly rely on innovation to improve education and accessibility for all students. The use of new technology has not only helped to improve access to education for remote or distance learning, which proved useful during the pandemic, but it has also improved accessibility for students with physical and learning disabilities. In terms of exactly how AI will improve the learning experience at universities and education in general is still too early to tell. The technology is new and there is a whole range of legal, ethical and regulatory issues that have to be resolved before universities develop official polices on the use of AI in higher education. Universities had to manage similar issues in the early days of the Internet, search engines and Wikipedia. What is true is that the technology is here to stay, students are and will continue to use it, and as with other technology, colleges will have to learn how to adapt, innovate and improve the learning experience for students.

I foresee AI being increasingly important for education. Having one person lecturing a group is a particularly inefficient method for conveying knowledge. One on one tuition where the teacher constantly assesses their pupil's understanding and used that information to adapt what they convey is far preferable. The problem is, one on one tuition is impossibly expensive and impractical if the teachers are human. Once the humans are replaced by AI it becomes feasible and an economic proposition. It will also reduce the cost of education, opening up opportunities for millions more people. Teaching a group required a one-size-fits-all approach. The teacher must make an assessment of the level of understanding of the members of the group. If all are going to benefit, the level would need to be set for the least knowledgeable in the group, but that would be incredibly tedious for the majority. So the teacher needs to, say, well such-and-such has already been covered previously so I am not going to go over it again. As a result perhaps 20% or more of the group who didn't properly understand at the time, now get left behind. The same applies to the pace at which the material is presented. Set the pace for the slowest in the class and the whole thing becomes tedious beyond reason for the most advanced. So, the teacher needs to settle for a reasonable compromise, which will probably leave the least able 20% behind and still bore the most able 20% to tears. And woe betide any pupils who miss some of the classes for any reason. One-on-one instruction from an AI can be way more flexible and tailored than teaching to a group could be. An AI instructor could start with a difficult couple of questions, which most pupils would not be expected to be able to answer, in order to assess current understanding. If the pupil solves the questions right away, the AI will have figured out that they must have already understood the material, so no need to laboriously go over it all again. Saves the more advanced pupils a great deal of time. If, as expected, the pupils cannot answer the difficult questions, then drill down with further questions into each of concepts that needs to be understood, going down as deep as necessary, stopping only when the pupil begins to know the answers, if necessary right back to first principles. Then, start the instruction, gradually building back up until eventually the pupil can answer the difficult questions that demonstrate mastery of the subject matter. So, covering a module could take anything from a couple of minutes for the pupils that already know the stuff to many many hours for those who need to go right back to basics. The important points are: Everybody, even the slower pupils, get there in the end. Nobody gets left behind. A great deal of time is saved for the faster more knowledgeable pupils. This doesn't mean that there won't be a role for teachers because students, especially children, are going to need to be supervised and encouraged. The role for teachers will change from principally being instructors to being coaches, monitoring progress and guiding as necessary. If my analysis is correct then people will become on average better educated, more capable and more knowledgeable than they are now, but this won't make people genuises and it won't provide prestige jobs for everyone. The number of top positions will remain as scarce as ever, but the competition to secure them will become more intense.

Here is an open lettter which gives a number of reasons: An open letter from educators who refuse the call to adopt GenAI in education We are a global community of education professionals who refuse the call for generative AI (GenAI) adoption in schools and colleges, and reject the narrative of its inevitability. At its heart, education is a project of guiding learners to exercise their own agency in the world. Through education, learners should be empowered to participate meaningfully in society, industry, and the planet. But in its current form, GenAI is corrosive to the agency of students, educators and professionals. Current GenAI technologies represent unacceptable legal, ethical and environmental harms , including exploitative labour, piracy of countless creators' and artists' work, harmful biases, mass production of misinformation, and reversal of the global emissions reduction trajectory. GenAI is a threat to student learning and wellbeing . There is insufficient evidence for student use of GenAI to support genuine learning gains, though there is a massive marketing push to position these products as essential to students' future livelihoods. Young people using anthropomorphised chatbots are vulnerable to psychological and emotional addiction. GenAI "relationships" continue to trigger mental health crises, human relationship breakdowns, and in the worst cases, attempted and completed suicides. Further, GenAI adoption in industry is overwhelmingly aimed at automating and replacing human effort , often with the expectation that future "AGI" will render human intellectual and creative labor obsolete. This is a narrative we will not participate in. We do not support the use of GenAI in education. We pledge to uphold the following commitments in our education work, and call on educational institutions, school leaders and policymakers to honor our right to enact them. 1 — We will not use GenAI to mark or provide feedback on student work, nor to design any part of our courses. 2 — We will not promote institutional GenAI products built on unethically-developed foundation models like ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, Gemini, Grok or Llama. We will not allow corporate-institutional partnerships to compromise our academic freedom. 3 — We will not accept without evidence the sales agenda of people who are not educators, nor will we spread hype at the expense of student learning and vibrant pedagogy. 4 — We will not train our students to use generative AI tools to replace their own intellectual effort and development. We cannot endorse the automation and exploitation of intellectual and creative labor. 5 — We will not ask students or staff to violate the spirit of academic integrity by promoting the use of unethical products. 6 — We will not rewrite curriculum to insert generative AI into it for the purposes of "scaffolding AI literacy". 7 — We will not contribute to the erosion of academic freedom and educator agency by forcing educators into compliance with technology they find unethical. 8 — We honor students' rights to resist and refuse as well. https://openletter.earth/an-open-letter-from-educators-who-refuse-the-call-to-adopt-genai-in-education-cb4aee75

This is truly a wonderful question. I don't have a simple answer, but it is on my mind daily as I work to improve science education. Before jumping into AI, we should all agree on what it means to improve student learning. I will restrict myself to science learning, as that is my expertise, honed over two decades of work in this area after being a university professor of chemistry. In science classes, students spend too much time memorizing and too little time thinking. They leave school having "learned" science without understanding it. AI is machine learning. Feed data and desired results into AI, and it will respond accordingly to new data. AI can help by grading materials if a sufficient number (thousands may not be enough) of graded stuff is available for machine processing. You can see the problem. The best way to learn science is to do science or the best possible emulation of that process. AI will help very little in this today. However, as we accumulate greater quantities of online student interaction with materials, we may begin to use AI to assist. Instead, we must use old-fashioned programming to make progress. This approach resembles "best practices" encoded in software. For science, best practices mean (see America's Lab Report) running real-world science investigations (aka "labs") in which students have to figure things out and propose hypotheses. They have to collect their own data point-by-point. The experiments must be real and not simulated. We can do this, and this is tough enough without having to insert AI today. The NGSS attempts to move in this direction with its three dimensions and all of the other stuff. It can be a bit overwhelming. If you simply focus on understanding science rather than memorizing words, formulas, and procedures, you are most likely to find the right approach to learning science. The approaches to understanding science include the "labs" mentioned above and studying the lives of scientists. These labs should be real scientific investigations, not labs that confirm what you have already been told. They are not exercises in manipulating lab equipment, equipment that most students will never touch again after leaving school, even obsolete equipment. I hope that AI, VR, AR, and all of the other shiny new technology ideas will soon be applied to learning science, but I am not holding my breath. EDIT: I wrote the above five years ago. AI has advanced since then and has even more promise in education. I have yet to see much of this promise realized, though.

Artificial intelligence is the intelligence shown by machines, which can be gained by performing statistical operations on the data rather than being explicitly programmed to do so. Now if your question is how machine learning happens, then the answer is by discovering patterns between the input and output. Lets understand by taking an example of classification. Say we want to classify movies into box office hit or flop. Our problem statement would be, given a collection of marked movies, each with a set of features, can we train a model to predict an upcoming movie's box office success!? Say we have some features about each movie like its budget, its star cast, its director, its genre, its release date, etc. To start, with each example fead into the learning algorithms, it will try to understand which feature or which combination of features lead to the respective classification. It doesnt stop there but try to compute how much does each feature really contributed to the classification. This gives the weighted feature model to the respective output, which lead to non-linear pattern discovery present in the data. To cut the story short, data is the ultimate truth, its the single source of knowlegde about the environment. By discovering patterns in the data and the output we train our machine to learn insights, which is the basis of artificial intelligence. Cheers.

That is the LAST place that needs to have AI. Already teachers and professors are failing students who hand in AI generated work (Chat GPT, etc, etc.) and it doesn't look to abate anytime soon. AI has certainly earned its place in commerce, politics, the national defense industry, and other far reaching installations, as the data centers begin devouing all power and water resources. Yet the education industry, which is vast, cannot see the trees for the forest here., and the damage is already starting to appear among the mentally lazy and lethargic students who frequent AI for all homework assignments. The papers and essays have become carbon copies of each other. It stands to reason that a herd mentality is the logical outcome of AI, and it predicts fairly the same results that Cliffs Notes, Monarch Notes, and all the other predigested aids that prevented students from thinking on their own efforts, ideas, and written assignments.

AI gets its "intelligence" from human input. It crawls across the Internet, grabs from various sources, and compiles this knowledge into a body of data. In a somewhat less involved process than what most human teachers do, it applies this approach to education. I do some online work for an AI company, where I audit the material it assembles for STEM education. The aim is to have this information become a virtual tutor for students to ask about their assignments. They might want to know about the water cycle, or photosynthesis, or how to solve polynomial equations. The AI model does simple Google searches for these topics, leading it to Khan Academy, Byju's, or Britannica, to get whichever answers it needs. It also happens to grab wherever else it might encounter this information, such as a personal blog, or social media posts about the subject. These amateur sources are why I often found casual, unprofessional prose in my auditing, which I then had to rewrite. Rambling text was to be condensed to only the most salient points. In personal blogs there was frequent inappropriate humor, such as tossing babies across a room to explain physics, which then had to be removed. In grabbing its material the company's AI seemed to have little idea of what it did, looking for that which was comparable, but having almost no skills in curation. But as the AI company has strict parameters for their STEM project, this in turn is what gave me a job, the "intelligent" AI's required human intervention to produce adequate results. At times it was too human and off the cuff, while at others too bland, formulaic, and not human enough, with an ideal balance to be struck somewhere between. I asked ChatGPT to write my cover letters. Multiple hiring managers say they would have given me an interview but the letters lacked personality. I gave ChatGPT some real job descriptions and a brief sentence about my made-up experience. I then sent the results to hiring managers. https://www.businessinsider.com/chatgpt-job-applications-hiring-managers-job-interview-candidate-2022-12 AI could write you a "perfect" cover letter, having it check all the boxes a hiring manager would, with all the right buzzwords and catchphrases. You could tell it to synthesize this from thousands of cover letters. But what the above article found is that the end result lacked personality. While impressed by the letter itself, those reading it failed to see just who it was that wrote it. Is a cover letter merely to convey something well written, or a compelling feel for the author? As a person is to be hired and not the letter, this makes what AI was set out to do, seemingly perfect in execution, lacking in the humanity essential to the task. This appears to be why that AI company now throws more humans at their project, perhaps bringing us back to square one, the good ol' days with actual humans producing the material to begin with. This makes me think of all the teachers I've ever had, and what made them good or bad: The bad ones made it clear they weren't too invested in their profession. They lacked motivation, simply regurgitated information, and had no interest in connecting with their students. They simply told themselves that morning, I just gotta get up there and read from this textbook, or off this Powerpoint. Blah blah blah. They failed to make the material engaging, having failed to relate to it personally, and to each of the lives of their students. On the other hand, the good teachers had more of what they were lacking, actual personality and humanity. They had personal, real life experiences to draw upon, related this to the material, and to the lives of their students. Just why are they learning, they asked themselves? What are their interests and goals? To find these they elicited students' opinions and experiences, making these a vital addition to the class. This made the material compelling, while also impressing upon students that each of their lives and success are valued. From what I can see from AI thus far, it's rather good at #1, though #2 requires something beyond the scope of what it's capable of. I see this continuing in the foreseeable future, evidenced by those bland cover letters above, and that AI company's increasing use of human intervention. This might lead them to the eventual realization that the best sort of teacher, the one most capable of impressing upon humans what they find most difficult, is in fact another human themselves.

Is it worth playing chess even though a computers can perform at a super-human level? It depends. Humans play chess against other humans and computers because it is enjoyable and can serve social functions. On the other hand, if your sole goal is to be unbeatable at chess, then you are out of luck — your small squishy brain cannot compete. Is it worth walking to work even if you have access to a car? It depends. Walking may be more convenient for small distances. Walking is also good for your health. If you need to travel a large distance then a car is a good idea. Just like chess and walking, some humans learn languages out of enjoyment and to maintain mental health. Like walking, learning at least some of a language can be more efficient than using technology (no matter how advanced technology is, it will always be moderately frustrating to use). If you just want to be the fastest translator, you are ill-equipped. If you want to translate an entire book, an automatic translator is a good idea.

If you want to study a foreign language, you should study with a teacher or professor. You follow the curriculum, practice the problems or exercises, and talk to people using the foreign language. When you immerse yourself in this class, you should gradually improve and feel comfortable talking in the foreign language. When you do not need to translate back and forth, or clarify meaning or what you do not know in the foreign language, you have built or established a basic competence or fluency. This is very important, because learning new terms, sentences, or meaning in context using only the foreign language, will establish a gradually increasing competence. And you will be able to learn a much greater variety of subjects, talks or discussions, or usage in the foreign language, without any outside intervention. That's actually the goal of language arts in general, where we seek to instill into students an emerging independence and comfort with only the terms in the language. Because the usage in the language as an art first, promotes using it in daily interactions, talking about stuff or referents, and taking it as the actual indicative for what the objects are. The translations offered by natural language processes in artificial intelligence, apply some linguistic principles and techniques, with the work of a computer and programming languages. These will translate words, phrases, or sentence patterns from a source language into a target language. Combine this with a formal instruction or class curriculum in a foreign language, and you will have support and faster translation when you study alone, without specific guidance, or want a quick comparison.

Definitely C++ and Python. Both languages ​​are used together at the same time. C++ to do the work in the framework and Python to command the framework. Yes, Python is not the main language, the main language is C++. Python is the language that sends the requirements to the framework and presents the answers to humans. The real work is done in libraries written in C++. If your work can be done using algorithms that are already written in C++, you will probably only use Python to handle the framework to do well know tasks. However, if you are developing new algorithms, you must implement them in C++. The more you stray from the existing algorithms, the more you need to work in C++. The more you use existing algorithms, the more you will use Python. Therefore, C++ and Python make up a very powerful tool that allows you to be very efficient, thinking at different levels of abstraction. I believe that this powerful tool (C++ - Python) is not unique to AI and could be used in almost all scientific investigations. Finally, I must clarify that of that pair, C++ today is insubtituible, while Python can be replaced by other languages ​​such as Julia or R. However, today, Python is the dominant language. Question: How can artificial intelligence be applied in English teaching. Answer: When I studied English in England (long long ago in the mists of time) before there was such a thing as a PC or laptop, we learnt two seperate subjects: English Language and English Literature. English literature is the study of Shakespeare, Dickens, Milton etc. It covers things such as character studies, plot arcs, literary metaphors and allegories. Great literature and story telling is probably going to be one of the last areas in which AI is actually better than the best humans. I don't think AI has a large role to play in teaching English Literature for a while yet. English Language was where we learnt English grammar, sentence composition, vocabulary, punctuation, verbs, nouns, adjectives etc. It's where we used a dictionary and a thesaurus. It's where we learned conjugation and not ending sentences with a preposition. And artificial intelligence has been doing this and teaching this to us for years now. Every decent word processor, such as Microsoft Word, comes with spell checkers, dictionaries, grammar checkers etc. It highlights errors, corrects spellings, and underlines its, when you meant to write it's. And there are plenty of English learning apps, tools and courses on the Net where you can learn these things by typing into the Q&A boxes and the AI will explain the lesson to you, grade your input answers, tell you what got right and wrong, and why. On-line learning is a huge industry. Just type "Learn English Online" into Google and you'll more AI driven Online courses offered than you know what to do with. And there are already AI apps to help you with your English reading and pronunciation too. If your school isn't already using some of these, at least for homework, you are behind the learning curve. So - English literature is still probably best covered by a human teacher, but AI has been helping people to learn English Language for years now, and that influence will increase. Hope this helps.

When it comes to skills, more practice means higher proficiency. With the proliferation of texting, emails, word processing on computers, etc., the number of times that one is required to actually write with a pen / pencil has drastically reduced to almost zero. Before this proliferation, we actually did write quite a lot. All our homework in school was hand-written, we wrote letters to friends and family, we submitted handwritten letters to the bank to transfer funds or open/close accounts, we hand-wrote invitations to events, and many other things. But today, we type into a computer when we want to create a document. So, we have lost practice with handwriting. And now with AI, the same phenomenon is taking place where people are now outsourcing even the content of the document to a computer. And the content of the document is indeed an output of your thinking process. So, with a reduced need for thinking clearly, organizing thoughts, and framing sentences, it is very possible that soon we will see a generation of people who will struggle with basic communication as well. This is one of the reasons that I am writing answers on Quora - to help me retain the ability to think and communicate in a lucid way.

Teaching is a great way to deepen and broaden your knowledge of something you already understand. I wouldn't recommend it as a way of learning about something for the first time. Why is it useful for improving your knowledge? Part of teaching is processing your knowledge into a form appropriate for someone else. That processing helps you gain a deeper knowledge. Your student will have questions, some will be obvious, some will be things you never thought of, and need to expand your knowledge to answer. So, don't learn something the first time by teaching it, but certainly improve your knowledge of the area by doing so.

Experience. The more you teach, the better you get. But practice alone is insufficient, You need to reflect on your teaching daily so that you can figure out what worked and what didn't, and then what you might do so that you can make a lesson better. You also need to continue to study and learn once you are out of college. Take courses. Go to conferences. Read a lot. Work with colleagues--feel free to ask for help from other teachers you admire. Ask your department chair or another administrator to observe your classes and to give you feedback on what worked and what needs to be improved. Ask for help if you feel that you need assistance. I have been in the classroom for 40 years. I think I have learned to teach better every year. Quite often it is my students who teach me. So my last bit of advice is to listen to your students. They want to learn, and they know what they need in order to learn.

For all of us, learning is a lifelong process. Every day, in an ideal world, we would all learn something new. There are many other methods to learn, even though we often view formal schooling as being necessary. Giving lessons with learning as the goal is simply done in a more formal manner while teaching. Various styles of learning Students acquire information through structured lessons, curricula, and courses using the formal method of learning. Given that all students must go through the identical procedures, one may claim that this strategy is passive. This may be the case, however some students may feel more at ease with a particular learning method. The list of these learning preferences follows. Different Approaches of Teaching "A thousand professors, a thousand approaches," says a Chinese adage. This saying rings true since each instructor is different and will approach education in a slightly different way. The two approaches of teaching that are most frequently used are learner- and teacher-centered. The teacher is viewed as the only trustworthy and exclusive source of knowledge in the learner-centered method, which is in some ways dominated by the instructor. The source of the information is a little less important in the learner-centered approach. The students have seen a trustworthy source of knowledge in this method as well. There are numerous ways to teach, and each one has a different discipline. In the graph below, we have described these various learning strategies. What Distinguishes Learning from Teaching? Differentiating instruction and learning is a simple process. Giving lessons to a group of students on a certain subject is the simple definition of teaching. While learning is the process of acquiring information via study, instruction, and experience. Many people would mistakenly believe that teaching is more significant than learning, but in reality, learning is more significant since it serves as the purpose of teaching. Although instructors cannot teach without students, students can learn without them.

If the stress is caused by existential issues, the students are not going to be fully present in the moment nor are they going to be focused on subject matter that is not immediately useful for their survival. For these students, any criticism of their work is like throwing a non-swimmer into the ocean and then criticizing them on their lack of technique while trying to stay afloat. Fear of fellow students is a huge source of stress. If there are students who dominate the classroom with fear by telling others not to answer questions or do the work that is assigned, the whole class will suffer for what amuses the bullies who dominate it. In cases like these, the teacher is seen as the outsider, attempting to impose order on the established group and doling out punishment in the form of negative criticism and low grades on the members of that group. Students will learn to resent the teachers for doing these things. Fear of what others will do to a student outside of the classroom or beyond the school fence creates a tremendous amount of stress, especially for students who fear for the safety of their family and friends within the community. Rather than being on the receiving end of the wrath of the bullies, students will fall into line and conform to expectations. When community members hold a negative view of education, students will work hard to appear as if they don't care or don't want to succeed academically. Gangs love dropouts because they have fewer options than to join the gang for protection from the gang and no other place to go to avoid contact with the gangsters whose ranks are filled with such people. When friends argue that a student is too focused on their work and doesn't have time or energy for their friends, it becomes a challenge for that student who will be told, "What makes you think you're better than we are? Why are you abandoning us for them?" Students under significant amounts of stress begin to question everything they say and do in order to avoid conflict with their critics. Eventually, the schism grows wider between them and they are forced to choose a path in which they retain their relationships or lose them to become independent of the support structure they once had. The same thing applies to parents who fear that their child will outgrow them, despite usually wanting their children to do better in life than they themselves have done. Learning requires a willingness to expand the sense of one's self in order to absorb and utilize new information. People under stress tend to become defensive and desire to retain their sense of self rather than taking chances on expanding it and becoming more vulnerable for having done so.

The OP speaks specifically of teaching and methodologies, so, I'll try to focus on those. As much as we try to deny it, much of the teaching/learning cycle is based upon relationships. While not true for all learners, it is the case for many. If you build a good relationship with the teacher, you will be more successful. In an online environment, it will be important for instructors to reach through the wires and make the student feel valued- and feel as if they have a real connection with a virtual teacher. There is also a great deal of discussion about the efficacy of study cohorts. In a traditional brick and mortar classroom, these are not difficult to facilitate. Online communities, while more common than a decade ago, still benefit from synchronous discussion. This isn't such a big deal with Skype/Hangouts, etc., but it will make teachers think about groups differently. Formative assessment (ongoing progress monitoring) can be more readily achieved through online work, BUT, things like expression/body language during learning is almost impossible. Teachers use visual clues frequently during the learning process to adjust instruction based upon nods or dazed and confused expressions. In an asynchronous environment, teachers need to be specific on how and when they are available for "live" help. Questions/requests can come literally any time and from any where. The teacher/facilitator cannot be available and online 24/7. My experience tells me that universities are increasingly offering online courses. I think the OP offers a great question as students (who generally leave high school with very little, if any, online learning experience), start interacting with their instructors in this virtual world.

"Grades" as we have known them will cease to exist, much as in the rest of the world. We may finally ditch the antiquated factory system and agrarian calendar that was established over 100 years ago.

That miscommunications are one of the leading causes of many, many, many problems in life and learning how to get better at navigating our interactions can improve everyone's life exponentially. Maybe they used the wrong tone by accident or something was misunderstood or lost in translation. Not everyone is a world class speaker who can convey their thoughts succintly and exhaustively so working through their (or our) thoughts may take some extra effort to get the full picture but OH MY LORD CAN IT CHANGE THINGS. I'll list off some examples. Me and an ex-girlfriend were at a concert in a party city of the eastern US. We had planned a weekend around it to go, party and gamble and just have some fun as we both were working high stress jobs in staffing and telecom (I was losing hair at age 24 because of it). After the concert, she goes to the bathroom and I'm outside waiting while chewing on a straw (didn't have gum and the molly was still kicking in 😂). Out of nowhere, a girl approaches me, takes the straw out of my mouth and puts it into hers. I didn't even have a chance to react. I laughed and started to turn away when I look up and see my ex giving me the death stare. She had seen the end of this interaction but not the beginning so she didn't know that I hadn't been the one to initiate this whole thing with the straw. She stormed off. We had been fighting at the beginning of the night so I was not feeling 100% supportive at the time but I knew that I had a small window to avoud an unecessary disaster. I had to explain what actually happened (I was on my phone and she approached me), calm her down, let her know that I wouldn't do that and that it was harmless because I walked away from the situation anyways. I let her know I wasn't hitting on anyone and I know what it looked like but that wasn't the case. The extra few minutes we took to clear up the misunderstanding saved our trip and weekend. 2) This is where a miscommunication didn't get resolved and caused problems. A good friend of mine was living in a different part of the country but would travel for work in my city. We would meet up when he would come out and eventually we started going out with some women we met. It was like long distance double dating. My date was from out of state. My friend was seeing my date's friend who was living in my city at the time. All was well. Eventually things calmed down with my date and I would tag along with my friend and his date and her friends sometimes. My friend was sometimes a bit of a hermit and I was new to the city so I was keen on making friends and creating a good social life. One night he tells me that he is making plans with family. We had spent some time with his date's female friends so I was comfortable asking her what her plans were to see if her friends were interested in doing something. Turns out that he was with his date at the this time, saw my message and took it as me going behind his back to approach his date. Wasn't the case at all but I also did not take the time needed to explain this clearly. I figured it would be easy to explain once but the message was not being received and my patience wore thin. So we had a fallout. Over a miscommunication. Haven't spoken to him since and this was someone I considered a very close friend at the time. Long story long, miscommunications and impatience can create angst, anger, sadness and impact our lives but the lesson learned is to take a breath, not assume the worst and try to get the full story before deleting someone out of your life.

Students graduating from high school should leave with frameworks for real-world problem solving and with soft skills (like collaboration), so they can tackle the open-ended challenges and opportunities of the future technology-immersed economies. The 'how' should first be assumed as a complex and long-term process. Next, the solutions will vary according to context and geography of the classrooms. But irrespective of these differences, teachers should consider engaged learning (including differentiated instruction) as the means for this development process.

In my opinion, you are very clever to ask this question, because this for me is THE question. There are many books on learning and teaching. But which to read? Which to believe? How to implement all their fine and well-meaning advice? There are also many volumes for people who do 'professional development' for teaching: people who run teaching/learning centers, for instance. And these volumes tell you how to arrange events to support faculty professional development around instruction and how to measure the results. My own approach has been a little different: I'm interested in how people become good (or at least highly-admired) teachers without formal training. That is: how do people 'naturally' develop into good teachers. For the last four years, Doug McKee and I have done a podcast (at first bi-monthly and now monthly) in which we interview highly-admired teachers at Yale, Cornell, and elsewhere about teaching: The Teach Better Podcast. Most of these interviews are with faculty who sought out no special professional development but just applied their own insight and hard work to growing as a teacher. (More recently, we've interviewed more specialists.) This is essentially ethnographic interviewing on this topic, and I'm in the process of collating some 'results' (which I'll summarize briefly below). In the mean time, there is some prior research which is highly suggestive. Hunzicker (2004) argues that teachers may not change because they lack motivation due to negative past experiences, environmental distractions, and negative beliefs about their abilities. This suggests that the converse might promote changes in teaching practice: identify past positive experiences you can draw upon, screen out distractions (like publishing and committee work), and identify rational foundations for positive beliefs about your ability to change and improve. This could be done by identifying your past successes: McCarty (1993) argues that change is supported by a record of success. Before behavior changes, beliefs must change (or the two must happen in tandem): you can't change the way you teach until you examine your beliefs about learning. Indeed, Borko and Putnam (1995) argue that change in teaching is supported by acquiring or developing new ways of thinking about learning, learners, and subject matter. And Jensen (1998) and Nuthall and Alton-Lee (1993) point to a disequilibrium between current beliefs and new information as a force impelling change. So this suggests asking yourself: How do I think learning works? What evidence do I have for that? Where might I find valid information about how learning works? Am I doing that in my teaching? What do I think is good teaching? Might I be wrong about that? How might I rationally change my beliefs about teaching? In terms of where you are headed as an expert teacher, Hattie has done an interesting study on the difference between experienced teachers and expert teacher. Doug McKee and I discuss that (plus another article) here: Podcast #18 - Teach Better For Hattie, expert teachers (1) identify 'essential representations of their subject,' (2) plan but then improvise fluidly to meet students' needs, (3) monitor learning and give feedback , (4) attend to students' emotions as well as their minds, and (5) help students 'learn to learn' and give them appropriately challenging tasks. From the evidence of our podcast, I have identified beliefs shared by highly-admired teachers: beliefs about teaching, learning, and the subject matter. Teaching: is human interaction—authenticity and caring count (yours and theirs, about them and each other and the subject matter)— must be fair: all students must learn; is time-consuming and difficult—it requires organization— is supporting others' learning; must make both knowledge and learning visible and palpable. Teaching and learning are social interactions, not a 'delivery system.' Learning: is active and requires agency; is experiential, not just cognitive; is challenging but achievable; means making mistakes; must be open to inspection so the teacher can monitor it and help students make progress. The subject matter: is principles and ideas, not just a heap of facts; must be focused around unities more than minutia; can be made into a big story about the discipline and what it does. So that's a lot to chew on. One shortcut is: ask your students who the 'good' teachers are and why. Then buy lunch for those teachers and ask them 'what makes students learn better?' and 'what is better and worse teaching?' But if you ask yourself what knowledge and beliefs you need about teaching, learning, and your students, and you ask yourself how you might apply those beliefs in a rational, I think you will get far. You may not get there fast, but you will be happy with where you get to.

I have answered this elsewhere on Quora Robert Zenhausern · Dec 9 How can AI-based tools transform the education system in the next 5 years? **My answer to the submitted question: Today Education begins in the home as parents taught their child to understand and speak and civility as the child matured. Increasingly, preschool is common and children are ready to learn to read in Grade 1. Testing becomes more important as the student approaches career choices. Five years from now Education still begins in the home. Parents contact an AI model and discuss the child and what the parents want for the child. In the last century, the Busy Box was a toy for pre toddlers teaching them manual manipulation of knobs and cranks. AI becomes the New Busy Box. It can play music or show images at the press of a button. Probably its most important function is to teach listening and and speaking, reading and writing. AI can show a word and speak it. That is what we do for speech and we just add text and the child learns both. If a child understands when you speak, the child will understand the same text. As a demonstration, I created a smartphone app about 7 years ago in Kenya. It shows a word and when a button is pressed the word is spoken. Another button gives a new word and repeat. Here is a video The next step is to teach the alphabet and the sounds of letters as a tool to use with new words, and as the start of writing. When a child starts Grade 1 they can communicate orally or in written and are ready to use those skills to learn. Today, that usually means Grade 3. Audio-Visual equipment, maintenance, and resources take a substantial resources that can be freed by using AI as the A-V center. No equipment to buy and maintain, access to all free resources, and the ability to create custom sources. AI also provides an alternative to the study, memorize, test procedures we use today. AI makes project based learning and authentic assessment practical. Teacher and AI work together to create a project that covers the goals of the teacher. The student works on line with an AI guide and goad and together they work on the project. The teacher can evaluate the final product and get feedback from AI on process, strengths and weaknesses. I taught graduate neuropsychology and research for 35 years and know the value of good research. I use NotebookLM is do the search and focuses on what I need to evaluate and integrate. This is still preliminary since it is based only on the sources NotebookLM found and it must be evaluated in the light of other sources. I use a custom GPT deeper evaluation. I ask AI to act as Devil's Advocate and find flaws in my ideas to be prepared. I have a NotebookLM based the latest information on employment. The stress is on AI as augmentation of humans, not just a tool that we use. The stress is on collaboration and the whole Education system proposed here is leading to that goal. I have been a been a teacher (Maths) - Bright youngsters are relatively easy to teach it is a the large number of pupils surrounding the mean capability that need teaching. Those in the lowest quartile need the most support but the majority need the most help and can push up the monitoring statistics if taught effectively. You cannot teach beyond the capabilities of the pupil - something that governments tend to ignore. The best possible teaching is achieved by teachers that are also outstanding communicators (highly knowledgeable teachers are actually not that important) - of course high knowledge and brilliant communications (+ empathy) make for the best teachers. For optimal communications with a pupil you need a deep understanding of how we understand. The pupils can get stuff right and still not know - and testing them will not necessarily show you this. Spotting a small inconsistency when talking to a pupil can lead to a diagnosis of internal confusion. This is real skill of teaching - I confess I was not the best but I knew what I needed to and tried to do it in this tough multi-tasking environment (classroom). Given that the primary skills are a deep understanding of people I think it is fairly obvious that people make the best teachers … not computers and not AIs (assuming they will happen). Can you imagine an AI which knows what it is like to be a confused pupil?

For me I think AI can do two things very well: Give Fuzzy answers to fuzzy questions. And respond with the most average text. This looks like two things. If I need to put a messy unstructured data into bucket for later processing, then asking the LLM to give it a broad category, (and I give it a simple structure to follow), then this works remarkably well. Don't try and tell me a customer wants 'Dutch Oven Spinach Artichoke Lasagna' vs 'Polenta Lasagna with Smoky Red Pepper Sauce'. It doesn't know. The customer doesn't know. I need it to simply say that perhaps the customer is interested in 'dinner.' And: Take these rando legalese and make it readable to the average person. Take this HR policy and make it very average so everyone in the office can understand. Nothing creative, but taking complicated text and boiling it DOWN. It of course can boil it 'up' making it more obtuse, but that's not helping anyone.

AI isn't bad at knowing what it knows, if that's what it was designed for. Expert systems have existed since the 1970s and in their domain of knowledge they are very very very good at knowing things. Modern LLMs are not good at knowing things because they are not designed or intended to be good at knowing things. I will say that again because it's very very important. Modern LLMs are not good at knowing things because they are not designed or intended to be good at knowing things. Modern LLMs are designed to produce fluent, reasonable-sounding sentences. Given these sets of words, what words should plausibly come next? That's it. That's what they're intended to do. They are NOT databases of information. They are NOT storage spaces for facts. They are trained on trillions of words to do one thing: Given these sets of tokens, what words might plausibly be associated with them? What output streams of words statistically correlate with this input stream of words? They are not good at knowing things because on a fundamental level they do not know that "things" even exist. If you ask Google Gemini "what materials are airplanes made of," it says: > Airplanes are typically made from a combination of materials, including aluminum alloys, steel, titanium, and composite materials. The primary materials used in aircraft construction are aluminum alloys due to their lightweight and strong properties, but steel is used for high-strength components, titanium for engines and fasteners, and composite materials for lighter, more durable structures. You have to understand that it does not know what airplanes are. You have to understand that it does not know what aluminum is. You have to underst...

The problem is, AIs are not knowledge machines. They are language machines. An LLM is just meant to string together words in a way that people will want to read. Think about this sentence: JFK died on November 21st, 1963. This statement is false. In case you don't know, JFK died on November 22nd. The thing about this sentence is, to many people, it'll be obvious that this statement is false because they remember the date or they've seen it in a number of places etc. This is a well known date. Since ChatGPT's goal is to produce text that people want to read, it has a clear interest in not screwing up this date. That is why ChatGPT will mostly get facts correct most of the time. But think about this sentence: JFK was born on May 28th, 1917 This is also false but most people wouldn't catch it if they didn't know better. JFK's birth date is far less known than the day he died. The correct date and the wrong date, simply don't look all that different in this case. It's simply swapping out one arbitrary number in a long string of text for another. This is why ChatGPT will very often get things wrong. Because correct statements look pretty much the same as false statements. It has been trained to try to get things right because people will often notice if it doesn't, but that isn't really the same as being a truth seeking machine. It is only producing facts for aesthetic reasons, not because that is the goal it's oriented around.

There are really three sorts of AI. There are AIs which do one specialist task, such as identifying cancer cells on slides. They're very bright at the thing they do, but can do nothing else. There are AIs which perform complex tasks, such as driving a self-driving car or a dog-bot. They have genuine intelligence, of a sort - but currently it's at about the level of a beetle. There are LLMs - chatbots - which have genuine understanding of the rules of grammar, but that's as far as it goes. The rest of it is just a glorified autocomplete: ask them a question and they reply with the words which their training set tells them are most likely to turn up in a response made by a human. They don't have any concept of what those words actually mean.

So surely we should utilise that addiction to our benefit @Cell Phone Addiction Similar to Compulsive Buying and Credit Card Misuse, According to Baylor Study

AI - the shortest acronym but the biggest game changer today! The point has come where we begin to think if there is any area where artificial intelligence does not play a role! Artificial intelligence is no doubt changing our world tremendously with its endless benefits, not compromising on aspects of safety and security. While online learning created the first ripple of transformation in the education sector, the concept of automation through artificial intelligence slowly follows. There are many wonders that AI can bring about in schooling and in classrooms. AUTOMATIC MARKING AND GRADING SYSTEM Artificial intelligence helps teachers to get rid of strenuous manual corrections of assessments. AI makes it possible for correction automation for objective type questions such as multiple choice answers and one word answers. AI does the ranking and grading based on the marks obtained by the students in a class. AUTOMATIC FEEDBACK GENERATION This feature is available in many apps where, after the user takes up a challenge (such as a math quiz), an instant report is generated that hints the areas of excellence and areas of improvement of the user. Schools, with the help of AI, have started using the same technology in order to generate automatic feedback for the students based on their performance. This will also alert the teacher who will also come to know where the student needs more attention. ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE PROCTORING Artificial intelligence proctoring is the technique of eliminating the chances of malpractices that may be adopted by the examinees during online assessments. AI proctoring makes the candidate take up an online assessment through safe browsing mode from where other windows cannot be opened. The screen gets locked and the identity of the Student is verified through facial recognition. If the student moves away from the system or his/her eyes are not fixed within the frame, AI will detect and report this. This will ensure that cheating on the web becomes impossible. It also saves the effort of teacher supervision. From curriculum designing to content designing (learning material), AI is soon to become the new tutor to help students learn better. The education sector will soon be revolutionized by AI powered solutions with personalized learning programs adapted to each student's ability and goals. Artificial intelligence will continue to grow more and more in the future and give students the finest learning experience ever.
