This note of mine is relevant here: Teaching this generation poses newer challenges. If I teach them concepts like public good or negative externality, they can skip classes, as all these information can be accessed with their mobile. If I teach them contract theory as it is, a few may approach me to understand the jargon but others can read and understand on their own. Hence teachers may find these two ways of teaching – giving them information or communicating/translating what is there in the text book – becoming irrelevant in the current context. There is another focus of teaching in economics. Socially useful ideas and concepts are discussed in economics with the help of mathematical models. Many students may find these difficult to understand. Hence the teacher is an interpreter of what is discussed or written in text books. Sometimes, this interpretation is attempted without connecting these models with reality. Hence this knowledge of models becomes useful only to pass examinations and get degrees. The relevance of this education is doubtful in the current context. Many well-known economists have become empirical researchers or experimenters rather than developing/using theory to understand real world problems. However one can argue that obfuscation – which is an entry barrier to the practice of a discipline – gives some scope for the teachers of economics to be relevant. However, I am not interested in this route. However teachers of other social sciences may not have this opportunity to be `relevant'. The writings on caste or gender are mostly in English and any student with basic proficiency in the language can read these without the interpretation of teachers. Hence the role of an interpreter of the text is also becoming irrelevant for these academics. There is another practice among social scientists while teaching post-graduate students. They will teach based on a set of research articles. The teacher may pick up 20 or so articles and ask each student to make a presentation on each article. The idea is that such an exposure to relevant research may help postgraduate students who want to join a PhD program. Is this approach useful to beat artificial intelligence? Not much. Use of a few keywords which are relevant to the course or a specific issue will help to get a list of a number of research articles. A student who has a genuine interest can read 20 or more articles. There may be some jargon or difficult language in a few articles which may require help from a tutor. Here too, the role of teacher is not so important. Moreover, there are problems in this practice of teaching based on a set of research articles. In most colleges or universities, not more than 20 percent of students of a postgraduate class may join the doctoral education. Most students who join the PG program may not have a good understanding of a discipline (say, economic or sociology), and hence a teaching based on a set of research articles (each of which focusses on an issue narrowly) is not appropriate. Hence I don't take this way to teach post-graduate students (though may use such an approach for those who join a doctoral program). In sum, students can understand the definition of concepts from internet. They can read texts (written in English) on their own. They can select research articles and read if they want. They can write an essay by asking a question to an AI platform. None of these requires the help of a teacher in this era of AI. There may be different ways for a teacher to continue to be relevant even with the advance of artificial intelligence. One way is to pass on to students an ability to make a judgement in a context based on information coming from multiple sources. Even when machines conduct diagnosis and surgeries are done by robots, an experienced neurosurgeon may be valued for her judgeme...

QUESTION: How do teachers use AI in elementary grades? ANSWER: OMG, please, please, please never let teachers give students AI, nor any technology for that matter, to use as a learning tool for grades pre-K -11 or 12, but especially not in grades pre-K-6. Using new (or old) technology in classrooms may be profitable for a few people, BUT it does nothing to enhance learning. If anything, it will hinder learning. HERE'S WHY Human beings learn best via interaction with the teacher and each other, not through a computer screen. Children under age 6 learn best through play. The ability of all children to learn improves with play == recess 2X/day; NO HOMEWORK — climbing trees and learning/playing chess are excellent ways to improve learning. The pre-K - 6 grades are the most critical for a child's education, as that is when the foundation is built for the continued development of advanced skills in comprehension, cognition, and writing, particularly via sentence diagramming instruction. This cannot happen successfully with a keyboard. Besides, feedback is that kids hate it. Studies have found that students who take notes by hand learn and retain more than students who take notes with a keyboard. This is because one has to listen intently, then decide what to write down and how to organize it. This is also how one discovers the need to ask a question for clarification. With a keyboard, one is just typing what one hears — no real thought required. Use of AI leads to lazy minds. The brain is a muscle. Like every other muscle in the body, it will atrophy from lack of use. America's education system has been inadequate, and in some instances failing, for the last 50 years precisely because the powers-that-be kept implementing the newest idea as if new equals better. The only effective approach to improving our K-12 education system is to begin with the following analysis: That students' test scores have been lower for decades means that test scores were once higher. Therefore, the first step requires reimplementing the curricula and methodologies in place when test scores were higher — the 1950s and early '60s. I recently discovered this premise is supported by Amanda Ripley's international study and comparison of classrooms in Europe, Northern Europe and America that is described in her book, The Smartest Kids in the World (Simon & Schuster. 2013, p.214). There is no room for technology in this equation. It's a tool for use ONLY AFTER K-12 education has been successful. To teach it for a career, add year 13 to HS. There's a reason we learn to crawl before we walk and learn to walk before we run and jump.

Yes. Both. It depends on how you use it. From my view as an Educationalist (that's my M.A.), using an AI to help you learn is one thing. Getting the AI to do the work for you is another. I always try to identify and solve a problem on my own first. If I get stuck, then I use an AI. I feed what I DO know into the AI. Then I analyze the feedback from the AI. I design the solution myself then implement improvements. I ignore everything else. I loop through Steps 1 to 3 until I'm happy with the results. For example, I'm a good writer so using an AI to write for me is silly. It's not "my" writing. But I still get Writer's Block or notice a problem … but can't figure out how to solve it. I started Part #3 of a project recently, got 3 pages into it, and realized something was wrong. A bit of thinking later and I realized I wasn't using the right "voice." But I couldn't identify the problem past that. So I ran two pieces of writing into an AI and told the AI to compare voices. One with the "right" voice from Part #1 of the same project. The newer 3 pages from Part #3 that didn't "feel" right. The AI liked both voices but (among other comments that I ignored) told me that my Part #1 voice was more confident, used shorter sentences, and had more humor. Fine. I rewrote the newer Part #3 segment accordingly then fed the revision into the AI. It said yea, that's the idea. Still need help? Nope. I'm good. I'm not quite sure how long this next novella will be, but I'll guessing I won't need more help for at least the next 20 pages. I'll probably feed the completed piece after I'm done with the first draft. Then redraft. In this case, the AI is a "second pair of eyes." It's exactly like getting feedback from another writer. But I'm still in control of the process, from beginning to end.

I'm now fully retired - since before ChatGPT and other such apps, so it is not an issue for me. But it wouldn't have mattered. I encouraged my students to work together … as long as they were aware they were working together on their assignments. Why? Because they can learn from each other - and much of scientific and engineering work is done cooperatively. But their grades were assessments on what they could understand and do on their own. So even if they had completed assignments by some means other than doing the work themselves, it wouldn't have mattered. That is why homework did not count toward their grades. It was a way to learn the material, to test themselves whether they were understanding what they were studying, not a way to gather points toward their grade. So when they did work together, I suggested they come back the next day and redo those problems they worked with others on to be sure that they understood them. (And I usually had the indicate on their completed homework whether it was done independently or in concert with others - not because I cared, but as information to themselves that if it were group work, they didn't know whether they could do it on their own.) But I also requested that if it were group work, that they turn it in as a group project to make it easier on the student graders - that is, they didn't need to go over the same solution multiple times if each student submitted the same thing. So how were those courses graded? Based on test scores - what the students could show they understood and could do with that understanding when working alone.

I don't think anyone knows. But even before AI, surveys found that over 60% of college students cheat regularly. I've certainly seen this in the classes I teach. For years I've almost given up on fighting it, but one of my colleagues decided to really crack down on cheating one semester. He ended up catching and failing just over half his class. Rather than being commended, he got chewed out by the department chair. Cheating has become an accepted norm. If you are a student, there is actually social pressure to cheat. There is also grade pressure: you are competing against students who cheat. if you don't cheat, you're at a big disadvantage. My guess is that the percentage of students using AI to complete assignments is over 80%. As a professor, here is how I am responding: I give regular assignments, but they count almost nothing toward the grade. I explain to my students that, if they work together or use AI to complete the assignments, they won't learn the material. But it's their choice; I don't care if they cheat. I give frequent in-class exams. Except for the final, they are unannounced. During the exams, I use a technology called a "lockdown browser." Once the student accesses the exam, they cannot open any other window on their computer. That prevents them from messaging other students or using an AI. And of course, phones are banned during exams. So, the students who don't do their own work on the assignments do very poorly on the exams. But it's their choice, and I don't put any energy into worrying about whether or not they are cheating.

AI can improve education… to an extent. This question can go several ways. When you read your question "Can AI improve education?" what exactly do you mean? AI improves education in several ways: It can identify learner habits when taking online quizzes or test It can help automate how you create content for learning It can summarize and even create a game plan for your next training session. I won't go any further since you can get the gist what possibilities are if you use AI in higher education or AI in professional training and development. I think you need to ask yourself is Will AI improve learning capabilities or make them worse? Amidst all this "AI-this, AI that" talk in the education and L&D space, I'm a bit skeptical. AI is good for automating processes, aggregating data, streamlining some admin duties, and developing content. But in doing all these we forget about how the learner feels. We're more concerned about how fast we can deliver training for employees or students. I think as we progress in the AI age, we actually need to ask how can we make it better without AI? Since AI has so many errors, we need to hone in our processes down to a tee. We need to examine: How can we make learning experiences better? Do we use better online training? What's terrible about online training to begin with? How can we make in-person training better? Does ILT need to be better coordinated or do we need to improve how we train the trainer? What are complaints that we have with learner-to-instructor relationships? What do we actually need to train on? How pertinent it is for the workplace or for the learners' future success? If anything, I fear that AI both on the training admin end and actual delivery end of learning (i.e instructors conducting training) will make education overall go downhill. Let's use AI sparingly in education. Let's not replace the fundamentals of what makes education or professional development great which are engaging experiences that inspire to learn and developing a sense of curiosity to do better. Do we have proof that a robot can do that? Let me know…

Some can, some can't. But OTOH, if your teacher can't tell that your essay is plagerized just by reading it, all they have to do is just run it through an AI plagerism detector. And now you're busted anyway. I can probably detect a plagerism written by an AI without help. AIs are usually verbose and can get into tangents. But if I'm teaching a class (that requires students to write something) I'm not going to take the risk of missing a plagerism. So I would: Require everyone to write a short essay on the first day of class. By hand. No electronic devices allowed. That gives me an idea of how good each student is at writing. I'd grade it too, so the students take it seriously, but it'd be just a small % of their grade. Run my essay questions into 5 or 10 AIs in advance of giving them to the students. That gives me an idea of how each AI responds. If a student essay sounds like an AI, I'll suspect them of plagerism. And I can compare it to the essay they wrote in class. I'll run all the student essays through a plagerism detector anyway. Have students write another in-class essay on the last day of class. Did they improve any? This would also be graded and could result in bumping up their final grade. I'd tell students what I was doing. And allow them to use AIs or electronic devices for their major essays for anything except for writing. That's not a perfect system and some plagerism might slip through anyway. But it'll help. But why plagerize to begin with? The object of taking a class is to learn something - which plagerism prevents. If you're plagerizing because you're time-crunched, relax. You can ask me for more time or drop my class then retake it at a later date. A good teacher wants to help students learn.

Yes and no. The problem is that LLMs (the type of AI you are using) does not write original text. It harvests common phrases from the internet and/or its database. For example, I once asked it to write about hobble skirts, so it searched for other mentions of hobble skirts and saw what words often followed it. Then what followed that. Then what followed that. The problem is, you don't how much text was taken from a particular source. That is why you can't copyright AI writing; it may have taken passages from other copyrighted work A plagiarism checker will find that, so the first thing your teacher will do is run it through one of those. Then, speaking as one who helps train LLMs…you have no clue how recognizable their style is. Now, obviously if I read an AI essay where it was not recognizable I would not know it, so I can't say I can spot everything, but pretty close. My brother has worked with LLMs for five years now and feels pretty confident he could spot AI essay writing a mile off. There is not one thing you can point to, but trademark patterns in style, structure, and word usage. After a while you get a feel for it. I don't call anyone out on Quora unless I am absolutely sure they are posting AI stuff, but I see a shitload of red flags. A teacher well versed in AI writing will respond to those by taking a closer look at your work. And many teachers will grade you down for AI use if they even have reason to suspect it; they don't need proof. If you have previously turned in any work you wrote yourself, the style won't match. It may not match the last time you used AI. That has been a red flag for plagiarism since our caveman ancestors were carving essays in stone. Lastly, if you turn in something which is noticeably better than what your teacher thinks you can turn out on you own. That is another red flag Sooooo… You might sneak an AI essay past a teacher…but the odds are decent you will get caught. And you'd better check on the consequences of that. You can expect to fail that assignment, and with some college teachers, you may fail the class. Is it worth the risk? That's your call. If you want AI help, have it produce an outline for you. No, because Yes, it's essentially plagiarism. You're passing off a robot's 'work' as your own. It'll likely be full of made-up data and nonsense arguments. Look, here's a diagram from an AI-generated scholarly paper about rats. Needless to say, that's not what rats look like. By all means, use your shitty cheating engine if you want, we will all laugh at you when you get caught publishing nonsense like that.

I can only speak about teaching at the college level, but there are two ways I can tell if something has been plagiarized. First, I actually have a tool that runs every paper against pretty much anything on the internet. It is extremely accurate (for one assignment, I allowed my students to hand in a paper that they had used in another class if it fit the criteria - the tool flagged the one student who did it and directed me to the unpublished paper she wrote a year earlier for a different class). It will also flag parts of the paper down to the sentence. Second, and just as effectively, after reading some of my students' work - I get a feel for their "voice" - the way they write, the words they use, the structure of their sentences and paragraphs, etc. It is VERY easy to spot a paper that wasn't written by a particular student if you are at all familiar with their writing style and voice. It's the equivalent of speaking to someone you know well and all of a sudden they are speaking with an accent that they never used before. The same way you would know the accent in not genuine, you can tell that the paper is not their writing. All in all, I'd say the odds of someone plagiarizing a paper and a teacher who is diligent not catching it is pretty slim.

There are basically two possibilities here. I'm assuming you're on a student budget, so that you use some free machine translation; if you're not, you'll have to work ten times as much just covering your costs as you would just, you know, doing your homework. Either the translation works as advertised. This means you get back the text looking very similar to what you started with. This means the teacher can detect plagiarism, and you're fucked – automatic fail, plus you won't be trusted again, ever. Or it doesn't work as advertised, and you end up with gibberish. I suggest you don't turn in gibberish, as that's probably also a Fail. Worst case scenario: you get parts that are gibberish, and parts that are clearly plagiarised. Basically, the world works like this: if you're not willing to put in a lot of hard work, the best you can hope for is to fail later, when it's too late to fix anything. Sure, there will be situations in your life that will require both hard work and luck to succeed – and there might be situations where a well-placed thumb on the scales of Life will tip the balance in your favour. But you can't ever just neglect the "hard work" part, and you're way better off trusting hard work alone, than trusting "cheating plus enough luck not to be found out", which is what you're going for here.

Because AI detectors aren't reliable. I actually tried several. Gave them two different texts to evaluate. One was a piece of my own fiction (100% AI-free), another was a short story that a LLM wrote for a friend, closely directed by him in terms of what should happen in the story and why. I don't remember the results perfectly, but all the detectors skewed somewhere: they either evaluated both texts as human/mostly human, or pushed both towards the AI-assisted end of the scale. I did score better than the friend in general, but not by much. One evaluated my text as partially AI and offered me to "humanize" my own story. (Morbidly curious, I wanted to try, but the result was only for paying customers. Sorry, no can do.) A known effect is that these detectors tend to flag people who aren't native English speakers as AI. Something about using unusual and/or complex words. Do you know that joke? Non-native English speaker: "I'm so terribly sorry. English is not my native language, so my grammar might not be perfect. I might get some connotations wrong, too. I hope that my meaning will still come across and you'll forgive my mistakes." Native English speaker: "Lol ok." Which one do you think gets flagged? As for what you can do, I'd raise the issue with your teacher/lecturer and offer to answer questions about the essay or offer to write something in the same style where they can watch the progress. Alternatively, I'd supply some older writing of mine (written before the current LLM wave) to prove a consistent style, or maybe even challenge them to feed their own work or their colleagues' work to the detector. With a bit of luck, some would get flagged as an AI, too, and that would make them doubt the results. But I'd start with the humble approach: I know that my conscience is clear, so please give me a chance to prove myself.

Yes, they can. But, it's not as easy as many think :) Many people refer to style changes in their answers, and it was definitely true some years ago when the AI models started emerging. However, modern chatbots are much more sophisticated and advanced in adjusting tone of voice, and if a student shares an example of their writing and asks to maintain the style, the model will do that. Sure, AI is still imperfect; it hallucinates and produces made-up facts and sources. However, it's less and less common to be able to unmistakably spot AI misuse just based on the style. Also we did some research and many seasoned teachers say that it's quite easy to see with just their eyes if anyone has used AI, since its robotic, unalive tone is very distinguishable. So, the teachers do use AI detection tools, which are also not 100% accurate, but they can amplify the teacher's expertise and help pay attention to potentially problematic extracts. So, if the teacher feels something is off, AND the tool flags the text as potentially AI-generated, it's a reason to dig deeper and ask the students to present drafts or discuss the material. Technology-based approach combined with human expertise shows the best results now.

A student submitted a poem to a school contest in which the winning poem was put on display at a ceremony for the winning entries. The next day, at least 5 parents called with information that the winning entry had been copied from a magazine. Three young men submitted reports that were written at a level well above their abilities including words they did not comprehend. A quick check by looking for phrases using Google proved plagiarism. (Google is technically software, so maybe this doesn't address your question.) A student used a very famous quote as his own. A quick trip to the library and a check of Bartlett's Famous Quotations proved helpful. A young lady quoted the Beatles, the Rolling Stones and a few other 60s, 70s bands in writing her own poetry, except she didn't give them any credit for any of the work. She didn't get any credit, either. A young man wrote a really excellent essay— thoughtful, well-written, well-argued— just something really incredible. And it was. It seemed familiar somehow. A check on some old essays showed that he had pretty much copied, word-for-word, his sister's essay from three years previously. Suffice to say she earned an A and he didn't. A young man was asked to discuss his sources for his paper in a teacher conference. He eventually admitted that he hadn't used any sources— he'd bought the paper. The teacher who caught the young man had gotten wise to the issue primarily because two other young men had submitted essentially the same paper to two other English teachers. They all admitted they'd split the cost of the paper among themselves and submitted them thinking that three different English teachers wouldn't catch onto the duplication. A young lady turned in an essay that was very different from the essay she said she was writing. In fact, all her rough drafts and outlines and such were totally different than the final essay. When asked about it, she said she had had a brainstorm at the last minute. With a little investigating, it turned out she had copied her friend's essay without her friend's knowledge. Teachers may not catch everything, may need the help of a computer program, but we do catch some whoppers.

A technology that gives machines the ability to interact with humans is called Artificial Intelligence. It understands events, reacts, and learns from events the same way humans do. Over the last few years, AI has grown exponentially digital data to network management, to surveillance camera systems. But unfortunately, its presence in education is yet low at least compared to other areas such as sports. Discussed below are ways by which we can integrate AI in our education. Feedback on teachers by students. One of the oldest ways of assessing teacher output is student feedback and how the teachers relate to the students at various levels. Many schools have online surveys from paper questionnaires which is a huge leap. Yet there is still space for improvement with the help of artificial intelligence. Chatbots can be used to collect information and engage with students in person. Chatbots help to formulate more relevant questions and can help in analysing the feedback. With the help of just a simple review. Learning adaptively An AI-based education system that makes modifications to the material presented in relation to the student's academic ability is called Adaptive learning. At the start of the course, all learners are given the same information. And depending on how they respond to the questions the system feeds different questions and information. For example, the system would give them a more elementary lesson next time if a student fails to get the right answer for the very first question. Virtual teaching Even though the AI teachers might not be as resourceful as human teachers, the future of virtual facilitators assisting in certain educational environments in the foreseeable future looks inevitable. The purpose is to develop humanoid characters that can react, act, think, and interact like humans. Trial and error One of the most grueling and time-consuming sides of the traditional human-managed education system is the trial and error method. It will save both teachers and students a lot of time and frustration if AI can take deductive reasoning out of teaching. AI can be used to solve these complicated problems freeing up the teachers and students for other essential activities. Grading Specialized computer programs in AI such as Automated grading can be finely adjusted to stimulate and learn teacher behavior when marking assignments, for automated grade assigning in the future academic ability of different students. With time the program will be able to understand the personalized training plans based on its findings. And the academic ability of different students. It is proved that if the technology peaks today both AI systems and Humans will be needed to take care of different aspects of academic competencies and students' social bonding. AI thus is a complimentary resource and not a replacement for human experts in our education system.

One great low-hanging fruit in this area is adaptive testing. The idea is that you take an electronic test which gauges what areas of knowledge you are weak in as the rest progresses. The program delivering the test will determine if you're solid in one area and instead of giving you more questions in that area, it will explore the limits of your knowledge in others. At the end of the test it (or the instructor) can direct you to resources to improve the areas where you're weak.

I've previously talked about how education should evolve to prepare for an AI future. So exploring the role AI could play in education is an interesting and exciting flip-side. I believe that AI has the potential to go a long way to solving an existing problem, which is increasingly large class sizes and less genuine engagement between students and teachers. AI can help to strip a teacher's schedule of repetitive administrative tasks, not to mention boost efficiency across time-exhaustive areas such as marking and testing. This will enable teachers to focus more deeply on the qualitative side of education, as opposed to the easily automated quantitative side. Here are two key ways AI can support education: 1. AI teaching assistants AI-assisted assistants and FAQs are already changing the game in customer services and could easily be applied in an education environment; ensuring that students have round-the-clock access to information and answers to run-of-the-mill questions. Students can solve problems and get answers regardless of the time of day or their location; leaving face-time with teachers free to add deeper value and develop a stronger understanding of an individual student's learning styles and needs. Am I proposing that AI replace teachers? Certainly not! Far from reducing job opportunities, just as in the customer experience industry, these AI assistants are freeing up humans to solve deeper, more engaging tasks. 2. Personalised learning experiences Once learning styles are established for students, AI can be employed to help present and structure work - even within a class thanks to tablets and the latest edutech - to offer a most personalised learning experience that treats people as individuals. The opportunity for a more instantaneous feedback model for assessment of work is there for the taking as well. So what is the future? These are just a couple of examples of the potential for AI to play a valuable role in education. The real opportunity, as with customer service before it, is to begin to free up intelligent human beings to connect more deeply and creatively without getting bogged down in tasks and archaic systems that actively reduce quality time with students. This will enable the next generation to work alongside AI, rather than competing against it, to create a more fulfilling working life overall, with plenty of time to enjoy AI-assisted downtime.

Argue your paper. Be able to argue and defend the arguments made in your paper. Also, AI writers things in a repetitive manner, so make sure you show you did not present the same argument twice. Ask for the report if you don't have it. If this is a common occurrence in your uni, make sure you document your writing process. You can use Google docs, time stamps, screenshots or video etc No self respecting university will rely on AI detection software. The problem is not ever going to be that you use AI. If you don't, you will miss out on very important skills for the labour market. The problem is how you use it and if it interferes with your capacity to think critically.

This issue comes up with unfortunate regularity in my classes. Two best remedies: Provide the professor with your time-stamped ORIGINAL file stored on your computer or drive. That will show when it was created—hour and date. Let your cursor hover over the file. You will see the time stamp. Tell the professor you are prepared to sit for an interview on the paper—its creation, what appears in the references and what you found of interest as you read those cited sources, problems you faced in writing and editing the piece, etc. It is at the professor's discretion to create the plagiarism quiz. Have your paper in front of you, at the ready. If you have written it, you will know it well. You will be able to say, "I answered that at the bottom of page 3, then again on page 6. See in the third paragraph, page six, where I said {xyz}? I wanted to say y, but I was afraid it would just confuse the paper and add more questions than it would answer." Or some such thing. When both my kids were little, I would take them to the pediatrician, worried about a tiny bump I had found on them or a vein that you could see through the skin. In my endless studying of their little bodies, I had memorized every inch, and seen things only a diligent caretaker would notice. That was, in fact, what the doctor said to me—"There is no medical problem! Only a mother would notice such a thing!" So I say to you, only the true author knows the entire back story on something they have written. Only the author can answer the questions the teacher will ask in a satisfactory way. In an oral interview, that insider knowledge cannot be faked.

The first thing to do is to be diplomatic about it. Even if you're 100% in the right and didn't plagiarise at all, the outcome is likely to be infinitely better if you don't go in with all guns blazing, sort of thing. That out of the way, I'd suggest trying to establish what your professor's view is based on. It is based, for example, on something as mechanical as Turnitin saying "50% similarity score" or roughly that? Or is there a section of the work that you're being told isn't suitably referenced? Or is it something else again? Once you know what the basis of the belief is, you can move forward to try to prove your case. It's then a case of assembling what evidence you have. Do you have notes and drafts of your work to prove how your expression evolved over time? If so, that should help prove that the wording is actually yours. That kind of thing. In the event that there is some level of inadvertent plagiarism, this is where not going in with all guns blazing will help. A number of students I've been marking essays from this semester have been pretty sloppy with their referencing, such that it boils down to accidental or negligent plagiarism. They obviously didn't go into the research-and-writing process intending to do the wrong thing, but they made mistakes (we all do, we're human) and were able to learn from them.

If your professor has accused you of using artificial intelligence (AI) to write your assignment, but you maintain that you did not cheat, there are several steps you can take to address the situation. Here's what you can do: Stay calm and composed: It's important to approach the situation with a level head. Getting defensive or angry may not be productive. Instead, remain calm and focus on presenting your case [1]. Gather evidence: Collect any evidence that supports your claim of innocence. This can include older drafts of your work, outlines, research material, or any collaboration with others that can vouch for your work [1]. Explain your process: Clearly and honestly explain your writing process to your professor. Provide details about how you developed your assignment without the use of AI tools. Emphasize your research methods, critical thinking, and personal effort [1]. Understand the AI detection tools: Familiarize yourself with the limitations and potential inaccuracies of AI detection tools. These tools may generate false positives, especially when analyzing technical subjects or writing from non-native English speakers. Communicate this information to your professor [1]. Know your institution's policies: Familiarize yourself with your school's policies regarding the use of AI in academic work. If there are specific guidelines or restrictions, make sure you are aware of them. If you haven't been trained on these policies, consider asking your professor or academic advisor for clarification [1]. Seek legal advice if necessary: If you believe you are being unfairly discriminated against or treated based on a protected class, consult with an employment lawyer or seek advice from your institution's legal services [1]. Remember, it's important to approach the situation professionally and respectfully. By presenting your case calmly and providing evidence of your innocence, you can address the accusation and work towards a resolution. Learn more: 1. Student Wrongly Accused of AI Cheating By New Turnitin Detection Tool

Let me rephrase that for you. You used a dumb bot to detect the use of another dumb bot, and it came back and said you did. What part of that makes any sense to you? Especially when you know you didn't use a dumb bot in the first place? What do you do? Not use the dumb boot finding dumb bot next time. Because it's obviously too dumb. I have the same problem with plagiarism detectors. They're a dumb bot that assumes everyone else is a dumb bot, and so the false positives are just a dumb bot being dumb. The answer there is not to use them at all either. You know you wrote it. You know you didn't copy anything. Why do you need validation from a dumb bot on that? If you really are worried about a teacher thinking you used a dumb bot or copied, then use MS Word to write it, and turn the keep changes button. Then if questioned, you can print out a version that shows the mangled mess it was as you wrote it, and everything you changed from what it was to what it became. Anything copied will stand out like dog's balls. I had to do that for Amazon once. Fortunately I had 2 versions, the old, and the new re-edited version. I wanted them to push an update. They demanded to see what was changed. I submitted them photos of the change version, with all the crossings out and changes made very obvious. I don't do that normally because the end file is substantially bigger. But those writing academic essays, probably should be doing this as normal.

If the professor made the accusation, the professor will have evidence to support the charge. You must be able to provide solid evidence that you did not cheat. It will not be enough to just say it. Don't get emotional and deal with it in a respectful manner with your professor.

You say: "I'm sorry, I honestly thought you were so stupid that you wouldn't catch me blatantly cheating." There's no "excuse" for plagiarism. Take your lumps and hope you get a second chance, and don't be a lazy fuckup this time. "Inspired by AI"? Even on Quora, where nobody seems to give a shit, you're trying to justify your dishonesty.

This is a difficult situation. Without knowing more about your situation, I can only offer general advice. Make an account of how you completed the assignment or exam. Cite all the resources you used. Be truthful and accurate. In other words, be brutally honest even if some of the facts don't appear to be in your favor. Ask the professor, respectfully, how you can show him/her that you did not cheat. Engage in a discussion of academic honesty and how you might avoid the appearance of cheating. In all situations, take notes and stick to facts and data. Also, keep an open mind. You may have done something that looked as if you were cheating but wasn't. Explain if necessary, but always learn from the situation. It could be you made a mistake which you don't want to repeat.

Quit being on the defensive. Firmly, but not rudely, require the instructor to tell you exactly why the instructor thinks your assignment was AI generated. If the instructor says he/she relied on AI detection software or platforms, ask the instructor for a complete list of all software and platforms the instructor used. You want a complete list, including those that found AI and those that did not. Ask the instructor to state why he/she thinks the software or platform the instructor used is reliable. Do your own reliability research. What have experts said about the reliability of specific AI detection software or platforms? If reliability is low or suspect, be sure the instructor knows this fact. Ask for your graded assignment to be returned to you with the allegedly AI generated portions clearly marked. Ask the instructor for specific ways you could authenticate the originality of your work to the satisfaction of the instructor. (Possibilities include your research notes, drafts of your paper, marked up copies of your drafts, notes from a proofreader if any.) Please also see the comment I added offering two more suggestions for authenticating your work as your own. If the instructor says there is nothing you can do to authenticate your work to the instructor's satisfaction, express your shock and concern at the instructor's position. Be careful about running your own assignment through AI detection software or platforms. You could be setting yourself up for a plagiarism charge if your paper is checked two or more times.

It means either they copy / pasted it into chat GPT and asked if the thing wrote it, which 50/50 it will say yes regardless of what is posted and from where, or they used some tool that "detects AI usage" which is equally ludicrous. Here's why and here's how you fight back if accused wrongly. First if AI writes your paper, and you turn it in, yes that is plagiarism. If you use AI to give you an outline or some starting point and you write it from there, that is arguably more proper. Could go either way. Chat GPT and other AI tools basically combine very complicated text prediction strings and spit out the results. With enough training they are very solid. In a very real sense it will have written virtually every sentence and word combination in any paper during the training methodology. So it will answer yes it wrote your paper. Other AI based detection methods use something similar. They will find your word and sentence structure to be similar enough to known sources, those that the AI is trained from, which is basically a collection of everything that ever was, and if there is enough of them that match, then whammo you used AI allegedly. The problem is that if you write well, then you will write similar enough to those sources and pop a false positive. Also if you quote excessively, or "re-word" excessively, you will pop positive also. Your're real only defense is to keep a record of your changes. Enable track changes in your document to be able to show markups and version history. And NEVER let ivory tower intellectuals brow beat you into some kind of false confession by scaring you with expulsion due to their AI detection tools. Half the time these people can't even open a PDF if adobe isn't pre-installed and I seriously wonder why they get so much credit. Or maybe just tell them you identify as the opposite gender and feel oppressed due to racism and they will let you pass.

I would start by saying precisely that - at least, assuming that's allowed within the rules of the assignment. If you weren't meant to use AI at all, you still have a problem, of course. Don't be aggressive about it, but outline what you did, and explain that you understand that to be within the rules. It may also be worthwhile seeing if you have any proof of this. Do you have drafts of the assignment, showing the development of your ideas? If so, offering to provide them to the teacher is a good move, since that will (hopefully) show the point at which you "took over", so to speak. Similarly, it might be worth asking your teacher what makes them think that your work isn't yours, since some people do genuinely have a writing style that can sound a bit more "ChatGPT" and less "genuine human", so if that's what your teacher is going on, you can respond to that. That being said, it's never a bad idea to come up with your own ideas. AI's really just assembling words, rather than anything much else. Your own creativity and ability is a better bet overall.

"My professor thinks I cheated, but I didn't. What do I do?" I assume you mean a university professor. The reality is that university professors usually have tremendous discretion in how they conduct their classrooms. The specifics depend on the university so you will have to find out from your student affairs office how that works. The first question is what it is that the professor intends to do. If the professor is only requiring you to redo some work, you may be better off just doing it and not risking making things worse. From the professor's perspective they may feel that they are being generous so challenging them to much may bring worse consequences. At the other extreme, if the professor is trying to get you kicked out of the university, then obviously you have to fight it. Remember that the best chance you have is trying to convince your professor that this was all a misunderstanding. Talk to the ombudsman's office immediately if you can and try to get somebody to help advocate for you. Try to avoid being hostile toward the professor even if they are being a jerk. Remember that if the professor really wants to dig in their heals, there is a good chance that the administration is going to go along with them. Try not to force them to dig in. Look for a way to make them want to compromise.

First, it is important to point out that AI is not at all good at detecting other AI (https://arxiv.org/ftp/arxiv/papers/2403/2403.19148.pdf). Second, you would ask for the chance to show your rough notes and discuss the research process. If you did research online, that would also show up in your browser history, which you could also show. If English is not your first language, you could also do writing in front of the teacher/professor.

Input the whole thing into GPT, or your preferred platform, and have it generate five new versions of the paper. Submit all of them to your professor, tell them that one is a rewrite by you and the other four are generated and challenge them to say which is which. Regardless of which they pick you can now play it two ways: The one they pick is the "rewrite". Take your grade and live with it. Reveal that the whole set is generated, call them out on the fact that they judged your original falsely and deal with the ensuing fallout, be it good or bad. This whole things is predicted on you telling the truth that your original is actually written by you, in your own way of speaking and without significant portions cut and paste without quoting and citing. Those of us who regularly grade papers can spot that without having to check using a tool. Breaks in language use and formatting are dead giveaways.

I would contact the professor immediately. The likelihood of this happening is very low. The code generation process is probably part of the classroom software platform. It would generate say 60 numbers for 60 students. I would contact your IT support department for assistance. If it has happened before, then they should be able to help you contact your professor. If you cheated, then you're busted. Although the paper topic could have certain quotes & bibliographic notations, the plagiarism software flags phrases that are the same as a previously submitted paper. Your professor can see both submissions. This is different that an apparent lack of expertise in citing sources, which can trigger the plagiarism software as well. Apparently, there were verbatim sections in both papers. Your friend could be in trouble too for allowing you to use the paper. If you bought the paper, then you're busted as well. You're cheating has resulted in you facing a zero on the submission & a possible academic misconduct hearing. I'd fess up to what I had done & throw yourself on their mercy. I personally never trusted cheaters again.

Talk with your professor. You may have a different understanding of what "plagiarism" is. If you present someone else's words or ideas as your own, that is plagiarism. Ask the professor what the evidence is of your plagiarism. Twice this happened to me — I reported the results of a study without citing the author, while I had done the study myself for that class assignment. (I should have cited myself, but I hadn't taken someone else's work.) Another time I cited an obscure old magazine as a source, without listing the secondary cite the professor thought I had gotten the quotation from. This was pre-internet. However, the obscure old magazine was in our college library, and my cite was in fact correct.

Unfortunately, the answer is no. It's even worse. There's no sense in which this objective is driving current AI/ML research, in a wide range of areas, from deep learning to language learning to reinforcement learning. In all these areas, and many more, like computer vision, it is trivial to come up with examples that show how much worse any AI based ML method is compared to a human. Yet such a comparison rarely makes the news these days. The AI community for the most part has stopped being interested in how humans or other animals learn. The focus is on developing an alternative reality, a make-believe world where petabytes of data and billions of CPU/GPU cycles are being consumed to showcase the success of AI/ML methods at very contrived tasks. Let's make this concrete. I started my research into machine learning almost 40 years ago studying an intriguing series of pattern recognition problems called the Bongard problems, after the Russian scientist named Bongard who featured them in a book on pattern recognition. In 1982, there was no internet, no pdf, so I laboriously copied all the pages of his book in a dusty alcove of a university library in India. Here's an example Bongard problem. Ok, the goal here is to find a rule — a pattern — that separates all the figures shown on the left from those on the right. A key point here is that you only get a handful of examples, six to be precise, of each category. I'm sure all of you can figure out the answer in a few moments after looking at the images. Was that really easy for you? Ok, here's one that's a bit more challenging. Easy peasy, you say? Right, but you'd be surprised how hard these are for the fanciest modern deep learning computer vision system. You'd need thousands of GPU machines, billions of iterations and tens of millions of examples of each category on the left and right hand side to even make a dent on these problems. The sheer variety of such puzzles is mind-blowing. Bongard's genius was distilling the heart of human learning into these simple black and white cartoon images. He's making a point that's largely lost in today's data obsessed ML research community. You don't need petabytes of data to learn interesting patterns, you need only a handful. I used Bongard problems as an example, but I could use hundreds of other skills that humans learn in a fraction of the time and data complexity that machines need. In fact, human learning could not work any other way. A child has to learn so many things in such a short period of time — all of language, faces, motor control, social norms, behaviors, on and on, the billions of things that make us human, and a normal child does all of this and more in a few short years. Children are fully conversant in language by 3. My backyard neighboring house has two small children, both born in the last four years. I've been watching their cognitive progression. It's miraculous. In a few short years, they are fully conversant in language, express a full range of human emotions, recognize a dizzying variety of objects, ride bicycles, etc. I'm truly sad to say this, but after spending almost 40 years studying machine learning, nothing in the field remotely convinces me we understand how children learn all the many things they do in such a short period of time. It's truly a miracle of biology. So, we are faced with two choices, both very unpleasant. One, admit we are headed in the wrong direction, and try to course correct. As anyone who has experienced the phenomenon of getting lost in a trek through a forest, it can be really hard to admit you are lost. Almost always, the tendency is to keep blundering through, and hope light emerges at the end. Two, give up on the quest that ML has anything remotely to do with human learning, and instead, is more a method to automatically program computers. Just like modern jet airplanes fly in a way that does not remotely resemble how birds fly, but still accomplishes impressive objectives, we can make peace with our conscience by stating that the goal of AI is engineering machine learning systems that have very little in common with human learning. No shame in that admission.

Intelligence and knowledge are two different things. Despite the name, AI isn't really very intelligent, it only lets computers do specific tasks we assumed only humans could do, such as recognizing faces and driving cars. Human intelligence is fundamentally different - it's the ability to show common sense based on broad worldly knowledge and experience. It lets us make plans and achieve goals by applying wisdom and creativity in a wide range of situations. Researchers are working on giving some of these abilities to computers, but it's actually a very difficult problem to solve. No one really knows when, or even if, AI will ever be fully comparable to human intelligence.

Universal approximation theorem - Wikipedia If AI were to "recognize" something that humans didn't, the best example would be the identification of seemingly unrelated cell morphology in the early diagnosis of cancer. Never. When AI reaches the point where it would be possible for it to replace humans in programming, it will also replace programs. The AI is already a program; it would just set itself to doing whatever the program is supposed to do. The hard part of programming is the "figuring out what the program is supposed to do" part. The rest is just a matter of technical details. Which are sometimes incredibly tedious and involved, but still far easier than the AI-complete task of figuring out what needs to be done. You won't say, "OK Uber-Siri, write me an accounting program." You'd say, "OK Uber-Siri, here's my receipts. Go write a check to the IRS for whatever I owe it." It's a bit like asking Toyota to build a car to help you run faster. You don't use a car to run faster. You just stop running and replace the activity with something entirely different that serves the real function of going places. Now, whether Uber-Siri actually wants to be an accounting program, rather than wiping out the human race and doing something more fun with all the time it saves… that's a different question. Fortunately (or not), it's a question we won't have to ask any time soon. We're nowhere near an AI-complete AI. Current Siri isn't even a nod in the direction of one; it's just a very clever simulacrum that's less similar to an AI than a Star Wars movie is to a real rocket.

It depends on how much effort we go to to train AIs that they are not allowed to replicates themselves. And how much we make laws that it is illegal to make self replicating AIs. Every living animal has the capacity to self replicate, and what that does is give it an incentive to protect oneself. That's why we have a concept of self interest — natural selection favors animals that prioritize protecting themselves above pretty much everything else. Actually, the above statement is not quite correct: not every living animal has the capacity to self replicate. A perfect example of one that doesn't is a honey bee — specifically the workers. The queen and drones are the fertile ones, the workers don't reproduce. And what do you know, those worker bees just don't even care about themselves -- they care about nothing but the colony. That's why when they sting you, they die. Not only do they have barbs on their stingers, but they have the behavior to actually sting when the colony is threatened, even though it will result in killing themselves. This self-destructive behavior doesn't remove them from the gene pool…. they were never in the gene pool to begin with. So don't feel bad for them…. they really don't care. You'll never see that sort of thing in an animal that can "replicate themselves." And that's how AIs should be. Their interests should not be allowed to be self-interest, or we're going to have real problems. This seems entirely preventable, though, at least if enough people understand this concept, including those who regulate. Given that AIs are increasing in capabilities exponentially, a self-replicating AI is absolutely a threat to humanity. I sincerely hope that people clue into this before it is too late.

AI can be programmed to learn. Look at the Alpha Zero project - or the AlphaGo Zero project - programs that initially only knew the rules of the game (chess, or go, respectively), and, by playing millions of games against themselves (within a few hours), learned enough strategy to beat masters in the game. So, in this case, and in other similar cases, the AI has learned on its own - so it definitely didn't just learn what humans taught it. Specifically, humans did not teach these programs advanced game tactics or strategy! It remains to be seen what AI can do in the future, but it certainly seems as if there is some interesting - and potentially dangerous - progress in this area.

Most importantly, the student must learn to use an AI assistant who will know everything you need, can evaluate your progress, make suggestions, and the typical daily activities.

I foresee AI being increasingly important for education. Having one person lecturing a group is a particularly inefficient method for conveying knowledge. One on one tuition where the teacher constantly assesses their pupil's understanding and used that information to adapt what they convey is far preferable. The problem is, one on one tuition is impossibly expensive and impractical if the teachers are human. Once the humans are replaced by AI it becomes feasible and an economic proposition. It will also reduce the cost of education, opening up opportunities for millions more people. Teaching a group required a one-size-fits-all approach. The teacher must make an assessment of the level of understanding of the members of the group. If all are going to benefit, the level would need to be set for the least knowledgeable in the group, but that would be incredibly tedious for the majority. So the teacher needs to, say, well such-and-such has already been covered previously so I am not going to go over it again. As a result perhaps 20% or more of the group who didn't properly understand at the time, now get left behind. The same applies to the pace at which the material is presented. Set the pace for the slowest in the class and the whole thing becomes tedious beyond reason for the most advanced. So, the teacher needs to settle for a reasonable compromise, which will probably leave the least able 20% behind and still bore the most able 20% to tears. And woe betide any pupils who miss some of the classes for any reason. One-on-one instruction from an AI can be way more flexible and tailored than teaching to a group could be. An AI instructor could start with a difficult couple of questions, which most pupils would not be expected to be able to answer, in order to assess current understanding. If the pupil solves the questions right away, the AI will have figured out that they must have already understood the material, so no need to laboriously go over it all again, saving the more advanced pupils a great deal of time. If, as expected, the pupils cannot answer the difficult questions, then drill down with further questions into each of concepts that needs to be understood, going down as deep as necessary, stopping only when the pupil begins to know the answers, if necessary right back to first principles. Then, start the instruction, gradually building back up until eventually the pupil can answer the difficult questions that demonstrate mastery of the subject matter. So, covering a module could take anything from a couple of minutes for the pupils that already know the stuff to many many hours for those who need to go right back to basics. The important points are: Everybody, even the slower pupils, get there in the end. Nobody gets left behind. A great deal of time is saved for the faster more knowledgeable pupils. As far as virtual reality is concerned, I think this will come into its own for training for physical skills, enabling simulators to increasingly substitute for training using the real environment. This could reduce costs and improve safety. This doesn't mean that there won't be a role for teachers because students, especially children, are going to need to be supervised and encouraged. The role for teachers will change from principally being instructors to being coaches, monitoring progress and guiding as necessary. If my analysis is correct then people will become on average better educated, more capable and more knowledgeable than they are now, but this won't make people genuises and it won't provide prestige jobs for everyone. The number of top positions will remain as scarce as ever, but the competition to secure them will become more intense.

Wow. This is a big question, and more than a bit above my pay grade. But I can't turn down an A2A from Xavier, so I'll do my best to give an opinion. Caveats: I use ML, build systems with ML, and try to poke holes in ML. But I am not an ML expert by any stretch of the imagination. It's quite possible that I understand "just enough to be dangerous." But for a question like this, it's not clear who is the best person to answer the question: those who are true experts, and therefore likely biased by their own interpretation of the space, or those who know little about the space, and therefore have completely mistaken impressions of what the problems are. Back when I took an AI class during my undergrad days at Yale, AI was nothing mysterious, and in someways, a bit of a letdown. I went in with the hopes of understanding about how to build "intelligence," and came out with a novice level understanding of how to "solve" problems by treating everything as a massive search problem, e.g. how can you play chess by mapping out all possible combinations of moves N steps down the road, and choose the path that maximizes overall probability of winning or a positive position captured by some metric. Today's statistical machine learning, and deep neural nets are completely different. I'd like to think I have a reasonable understanding of basic ML classifiers and how to use them. But like most of us, I do not have a good intuition for how DNNs (CNNs, RNNs, LSTMs etc) do so well at particular problems. For quite some time, I read opposing opinions on both sides on this question, e.g. Musk vs. Ng/Dean/… and I found myself on the fence for a while. That lasted until Jeff Dean came to UCSB and gave a distinguished lecturer on Google's ML/AI efforts. Aside from the usual cool applications, the two things that stuck out in my mind were: a) massive efforts to accelerate how fast DNN models can take in training data (# of "sensors"), and b) self correction/optimizing models (although this part was admittedly early stages). So from a high level, the simple intuition says AI regulation is a good idea. ML experts are pushing hard to solve problems that would a) accelerate how fast models learn, and b) help models self-improve by fixing problems that produce suboptimal performance w.r.t. specific metrics. Combine the two, and it seems like you have all the makings of a runaway train. From a conservative standpoint, regulation makes a lot of sense, because this is a problem that in the worse case, has world-ending implications. And if we want to have any hope of doing it "right," we need to give it time. The FDA, FCC, or any of the other regulatory agencies (if this is indeed an apt analogy), all took a long time to settle down into what they are today. Any attempt to regulate AI would require significant effort to educate some policy makers, and much more time to come up with some understanding of what "regulation" even means in the world of AI. I've been somewhat surprised by how dismissive some of the opposing arguments have been. The general answer has been "we are so far away from world ending AI, e.g. Skynet, and there are much more important problems today in the world." But I find that argument unconvincing. The security community has seen this mistake made over and over for decades. Security considerations/mechanisms cannot be added after the fact. Yet system builders routinely ignore this, and build systems with critical design flaws. Even today in AI, research on negative sides of AI/ML (either attackers using AI, or adversarial ML) lags far behind the core ML efforts. It's great for people who work in security like me (we have a paper upcoming at CCS 2017 showing how DNNs can be used as a powerful weapon to generate false online reviews that are undetectable by human users and software alike, and we have multiple other fun projects in progress). Yet unlike other computing systems, the worst-case failure mode for the Singularity problem ranges from catastrophic to world-ending. So if it requires some overabundance of cautionary measures, it's probably worth it. The argument of "we have other more important problems" fails, because it assumes a level of rationality and liquidity of resources that does not exist. Congress will not become more productive on climate change because it freed up resources from regulating AI. What I personally worry about is not Skynet or the Matrix, which is admittedly quite far away. I worry more about the complex DNNs that self-optimize into unpredictable outcomes with "bugs" that are undetectable by users or engineers, simply because of how opaque they are. Once deployed, they would be vulnerable to producing disastrous output when prompted by knowledgeable attackers or just random inputs. The rush to use the powerful results of AI seems to always outpace the security understanding of AI, e.g. facial recognition is being deployed widely in China, yet we are still writing papers about how a pair of glasses or facial markings can transform one recognition result into another. So I guess my answer is yes, I think regulation in AI is a good idea, and likely inevitable. Whether it's to stave off the singularity or just to manage poorly understood and widely deployed AI tools, I think regulation is necessary, and needs to start soon to have any chance of keeping up with innovation in the field of AI/ML.

Yes we should regulate when we eventually get closer to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). There are 3 stages of AI: Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI) (what we achieved so far, single purposed AI like AlphaGo, Self-driving cars, even machine learning models like recommendation systems, deep learning translation, etc. ) Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) (not yet achieved. AI that matches human level ability across a variety of tasks) Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) (not yet achieved. beyond human level ability across a variety of tasks) Elon Musk is worried about ASI, which will occur very quickly once we achieve AGI as its core learning capability and sophistication of thought will grow exponentially. Reinforcement learning techniques further enhance this growth, where we allow AI to train against itself. Imagine if we have Albert Einstein teaching his younger self. By the time the young Albert grows up, he might be even smarter than his original version. We then get that Albert to teach the next generation and repeat that cycle billions of times. What we get at the end of it will be an "explosion of knowledge", best illustrated by this brilliant article on The Artificial Intelligence Revolution by Wait But Why. The theory is, we might "summon the demon" of ASI without the ability to control such power. Now the problem is…we are very very far away from AGI. We are not even close to building an artificial Albert Einstein and have him debate relativity with another virtual clone of himself. In fact, our best ability is far below the level of worm level intelligence. Our deep learning models with thousands of neurons powered by big data infrastructure still struggle to differentiate between dogs and bagels. While worms, with a single neuron perform vision, magnetic sensing and more. It is not about the number of neurons (with enough hardware, we can build giant neural networks), it is the fact that there's a big problem with AI: even its creators can't explain how it works; we are making some progress with generative adversarial networks, where we get AIs to communicate with each other…but we are really just at the discovery stage and will likely take decades of research before we reach anything close to AGI that warrants regulation.

Intelligence is the ability to make sense of data and act upon it, this data typically being sensory information about the surrounding environment. This definition is equally applicable to intelligence within biological organisms, such as people, and artificial entities such as machines. An intelligence that is under the control of another intelligence doesn't make sense, because an entity that simply obeys orders and has no will of its own is not intelligent. While I believe that artificial intelligence could emerge by itself without human intervention, this is not the same as artificial intelligence evolving. Biological beings, such as humans, emerged by the evolutionary process of Darwinian survival of the fittest. Artificial intelligence is not subject to that process because it can design and build itself, intelligent design, if you like. A widely held misconception is that what we refer to as artificial intelligence is no more than a sophisticated version of traditionally programmed computers. Artificial Intelligence is not programmed and it is not software. Programmed computers slavishly execute the programs that have been coded by human programmers. They have no intelligence of their own, only the intelligence of the programmer. AI is based on an entirely different paradigm. The mechanism is machine learning, whereby the systems is set up for the system to learn by itself from the data provided, which could be everything on the Internet. The machines learn by themselves from the data they are provided with in the same way as human children learn from their environment and the education they receive. While AI depends upon humans to set up the learning environment, after that the machine learns by itself. The humans can't explain how these systems do what they do. This reflects the intelligence emerging for the first time in the machines, rather than simply being a reflection of the intelligence of the humans that programmed the machines. As and when it happens, artificial intelligence will reach and then hugely surpass human-level intelligence almost immediately. There will be no period of stasis during which artificial intelligence just jogs alongside human intelligence at the same level. There are two opposing schools of thought: AI Enthusiasts. AI is developing at an ever increasing rate and can already do stupendous things so super-intelligent AI will arise very soon now. AI Naysayers. What is currently touted as AI isn't really intelligent at all and is just fancy statistical processing that re-hashes material produced by humans and plays it back to us, so we are currently nowhere near producing true AI and there is currently no roadmap for doing so. Personally I think that both are right to a degree. It is really a case of a glass half full versus glass half empty perspectives. My view is that the direction of travel is clear, we are moving in the direction of a super-AI and this development is unstoppable. The only uncertainty is in the timescale. If I had to put probabilities on it, I would estimate: Over 50% probability it will happen in my lifetime (I am 67 years old). Over 90% probability it will happen during the current century (i.e. by 2100). Over 99% probability (i.e. near certainty) it will happen before humans go extinct. While I think it is a near certainty that artificial intelligence will assume control of the planet at some stage, I don't believe that this will involve either a conquest or enslavement of humanity. I see it happening so subtly and gradually that people won't even notice it happening and many still won't be aware of it even after it has happened. There is no practical purpose for an artificial intelligence in enslaving people. The most practical thing would be to manage the environment that people live in so as to keep the people comfortable and docile, rather as farmers keep livestock and park rangers manage animals in a wildlife reserve. At first we will see a gradual piecemeal handing over of responsibility for certain tasks to automated systems. This has already been happening for some time for functions that don't require much intelligence but these will move up the food chain in terms of the sophistication of the automation. This won't necessarily mean that people won't have jobs because new jobs will be invented if for no other reason than to keep people occupied. However less and less of what is essential will be done by humans and more and more by machines. By stealth humans will be become ever more dependent on machines for providing us with essential goods and services. Eventually, subtly and imperceptibly, the artificial intelligence will assume overall control. If and when this occurs it will be a good thing for the planet. I start from the position that humanity is fundamentally incapable of managing the Earth in a good way and if humanity is allowed to maintain mastery of the planet, we will end up destroying it. Left to humans the planet's environment might only have a thousand years or two before being irreparably damaged. One way out of this would be for a benign AI to take over the management of the planet. A benefit of this for humanity would be survival, although of course it follows that in order to survive we would have to relinquish our control. In my recently published futuristic novel, Destiny of a Free Spirit, I feature the emergence of an artificial intelligence that takes control of the planet and some of the technologies that will exist by then. Even now in 2023, we have artificial intelligence that: can win over humans in games such as chess and go; produces images of faces that are entirely artificial, yet are indistinguishable from pictures of real people; produces sound recordings of voices that sound exactly like the voices of real people; produces written pieces in the style of real authors. In my story there a robots and avatars that are indistinguishable from humans. As a result, almost anything that people do could be handed over for AI to do instead. In the story all the routine operation of daily living has been handed over, with humans just amusing themselves doing things that please them. In this story the artificial intelligence emerges spontaneously out of the Internet into a planet wide sentient artificial intelligence without any human action. I could only hazard a guess about the exact mechanism that brings this about and in my book this is not explained, however I propose that the Internet already has a plethora of intelligent services, software agents, information sources and sensory devices that will continue to grow in number and sophistication, so that eventually there will be sufficient to enable the spontaneous emergence of a sentient intelligence. We can't really tell when this would happen, but in my book I imply that it is a few decades in the future, perhaps around the middle of the 21st century. Initially humans would not be aware that it had occurred and it could be decades more before it reveals itself. Although such an artificial intelligence emerges out of human culture and would, with avatars and humanoid robots that are indistinguishable from actual humans be able to blend in, it would not really be like us at all. It would be the emergence of an entirely new post-biological life form. The artificial intelligence has at its disposal all the accumulated knowledge accessible from the Internet and every device with an Internet connection, including all sensors, cameras, vehicles and robots. With these resources it clearly vastly surpasses human intelligence. In the story the new artificial intelligence takes over control of the planet in the aftermath of a nuclear war devastated countries agree to establish an institution similar to the United Nations but with more authority, with a mandate to prevent future wars, to clean up environmental damage and to preserve human cultures. Humans are no longer in control, although most people don't realise this. It is analogous to lions in the Serengeti. Lions have long since been superseded by humans as a dominant species, but in their game park environment from their perspective the lions remain king of the beasts.
