No. I understand how recent advances and associated hype can be scary for people, especially since doomsday scenarios related to AI have been part of our popular culture for many decades. I also understand, to address one of Ben Y. Zhao's concerns, that my opinion might come across as one of those "dismissive insiders". However, I think there at least 3 good reasons not to regulate AI. AI is a fundamental technology Artificial Intelligence is field of research and development. You can compare it to quantum mechanics, nanotechnology, biochemistry, nuclear energy, or even math, just to cite a few examples. Fundamental research fields or technologies should not be regulated. All of them could have scary or evil applications, but regulating them at the fundamental level would inevitably hinder advances, some of which could have a much more positive impact than we can envision now. To put this into perspective, perhaps the only fundamental research field that is highly regulated as of today is medicine. Of course, medicine has been developed for centuries, and has intrinsic issues in that it needs to be directly tested on humans or at least living creatures. Even taking that into account, it is widely accepted that strong regulation in medicine makes it extremely hard and costly to innovate. Because of this, the medical field as a whole is clearly years behind in its adoption of basic technological advances. Therefore, AI as such should not be regulated. What should be heavily regulated is its use in dangerous applications, such as guns or weapons. It is way too early If you ask any expert as of today what should be regulated in AI the answer would have to be, inevitably, "we don't know". If you take a look at the research being carried out at Elon Musk's Future of Life Institute on the topic, you will realize that all projects are researchy in nature (e.g. see this one about how to better estimate probabilities for self-driving cars) and most of them are at their infancy (see description of this project on how to teach Deep Learning about moral concepts). Honestly, if we had to regulate AI any time soon we would not know how to do it. What's even worse, we could let people with absolutely no understanding of the technology do it. If we connect this to the the previous idea of AI being a fundamental technology we have a recipe for disaster. This would be worse than having let the governments regulate the Internet in the 80s. Regulate, at what level? Ok, let's pretend I haven't convinced you enough with the two previous reasons and you still insist on regulating. My question would be: at what level would you do this? Would you want the US government to regulate its research and deployment in general while other countries (including perhaps North Korea) would freely continue to innovate and deploy latest advances? Clearly not. I am guessing people like Musk proposing regulation have not even thought about this or are many thinking of a regulation at the UN level? Good luck with that. As far as I know, health again, is the only example of such a level of international regulation. The World Health Organization managed to convince most countries in the world to sign the IHR after over 40 years of work. Many countries, including the US, signed with reservations. So, to summarize, AI should not be regulated because it is a fundamental technology, and at this point we would not know what to regulate or how to get enough international support for that to happen. To be fair to Musk and others though, given that it is likely to take 50 years at best to get anything done, it might be ok to have a few loud voices pushing for it now. I just hope that they don't get their voices heard too soon and we end up in a situation where people with no clue and understanding prevent us from a better future by compromising innovation in such a key area of human development.

As someone who is a lifelong student, since before the days of AI and during the early days of the internet (fall of the encyclopedia), I have an interesting viewpoint on the disadvantages of Artificial Intelligence in Education. Perhaps the greatest disadvantage of AI in education is that it can do the work for the student and simply present the answer in a way which the student may believe they are learning but are not. As long as this phenomenon and tendency of AI systems is understood, AIs can be incredible teaching tools. For Example, AI can act as the most highly qualified tutor and explain difficult concepts and create relevant and important homework. I use it very often with IT to teach me concepts at an appropriate level, for example, "ChatGPT Please explain to me, at a college education level, the difference between GRUB and Gnome." It can also be excellent for creating homework. But the student must use these as tools, or objectives, and put in work and gain knowledge from additional methods including traditional schooling as well as life experience. The main disadvantages of artificial intelligence in education involve using it as a crutch, to do the work in replace of learning, rather than as an augmented tool to aid in creativity and learning. I use it to create new ideas or help me brainstorm, or as a back and forth conversational tool. But I can see the temptation for a student would be to copy-and-paste prompts and/or responses with very little thought involved. Therefore it can't become a crutch, and this also includes very basic, rudimentary, and important educational tasks such as memorizing the times table. I believe a Classic education which is very historical and traditional until at least high school in the United States (13 years old) with absolutely zero AI intervention / direct teaching is essential.

AI is a tool, like any other. AI "art" of my D&D character. Well, my D&D character dreaming that we're still playing Star Wars. Long story. The question if what "art" actually is, is one that has driven artists for generations. It used to be simple. Art was something beautiful that rich people had in their homes made to be admired. Traditional handmade Africa comb. But as industrialization progressed we began to appreciate the beauty and skill that used to go into making practical handmade objects. What we now call folk art. And by the 60s Pop Artists like Andy Warhol began exploring the beauty and skill used to create even mass produced items. Warhol in particular turned mass production itself into an artform, selling silkscreen prints of pieces for which no original existed. As he pointed out, the fact that the Queen of England and a bum on the street drink the exact same Coca Cola had a beauty and a meaning to it. The Dadaists argued that art is anything that had meaning, and that their work, being utterly devoid of it, was thus anti-art. Art is dead. Long live Dada. But as expressions of existential Dread following what was then the greatest war ever fought, Dada's intentional lack of meaning still had meaning. Was still art by their own metric. And of course, photography used to be seen as little more than a cheap parlor trick. A crude imitation of paintings by those who couldn't afford better, until Man Ray showed us what it could really do. You couldn't paint Man Ray's photographs. At least, you couldn't paint them better than he shot them. Which brings us back to "AI art". Most of it is crap. D&D characters, elaborate shitposts, filters over your friends' photos. If the vast majority can be called "art" then they aren't good art. But the technology has potential. It can be used to generate images from text. To edit parts of existing images. And to change the "style" of both. Photo of a miniature I took with an AI generated background. Before AI I would have used a stock photo or screenshot from a movie. Right now it is either a children's toy, or something digital artists have integrated into their existing workflows. A way of making the art we were already making faster and cheaper, if not better. But in theory AI art has the potential to open up entirely new avenues of expression just as photography and digital art did. We're just waiting for a modern day Man Ray to show us that potential.

I like Studio Ghibli, and that little trend or meme was….. cute. I'm surprised we weren't able to do that before with other image generators. But OpenAI's new one that is part of the 4o model, is extremely interesting for completely different reasons. First off, it's not a diffusion model. It's an auto regressive model, which is the way language models typically work. But the main thing is simply that it is the exact same model that is powering ChatGPT or at least a good part of ChatGPT, it's their main workhorse model these days. It's also a model that is able to do advanced voice mode where the voice is not converted to text first. It's actually going in and out as audio. And now this model can can take images as input and output, directly. Not going through some other model and then being converted to text or as ChatGPT did with DALL-E, where it actually creates the text and sends it off to the image generator. And that's really really interesting because what it means is that the language model — which, remember, is also really competent at programming — now can speak in a visual language. That means it can make technical drawings diagrams, all kinds of things that allow it to have an understanding of, and have the ability to work with things in the real world that aren't language per se.

AI doesn't create. Whatever Midjourney or Stable Diffusion spits out isn't art. It's a regurgitated pile of pixels stitched together from the scraped work of real human artists. If you use AI, you're not an "artist." You're someone typing prompts into a machine that steals from those who actually create. Yes, AI is a tool. But here's the thing: so is a car. And if you drive 26.2 miles to the marathon's finish line, you don't get to call yourself a runner. "But cars are tools! Just like running shoes! And it's not like I did nothing. I watched the road. I stepped on the gas and turned the wheels the whole time." Sure. But one assists the effort. The other replaces it entirely. That's the difference. Playing with AI? Fine. Prompt away. See what shiny Frankenstein mess it spits out. But don't confuse it with making anything. You are not creating. You are not expressing. You are not doing the work. You are not an artist. AI is for people trying to skip the act of doing. And I get it. I use AI too. I use ChatGPT to write business letters, end-of-year reviews, and performance blurbs. I don't enjoy writing those, and I don't care about getting better at them. I just want them done. But that's the point. If you use AI to "make art," maybe you don't actually enjoy making art. Maybe you like the aesthetic of creation, the image of being "creative," but not the actual process of drawing, painting, designing, or shaping. Otherwise, you'd be doing it yourself. Back to the marathon: I don't like running. So I drive. That's fine. There's no shame in driving. But I don't go around telling people I'm a marathoner who crushed it in record time and show them the picture of me, with my car, at the finish line with a big thumbs up.

P. Snow " The Two Cultures " is the first part of an influential 1959 Rede Lecture by British scientist and novelist C. P. Snow which were published in book form as The Two Cultures and the Scientific Revolution the same year. [1] [2] Its thesis was that science and the humanities which represented "the intellectual life of the whole of western society" had become split into "two cultures" and that this division was a major handicap to both in solving the world's problems. The lecture [ edit ] The talk was delivered 7 May 1959 in the Senate House , Cambridge , and subsequently published as The Two Cultures and the Scientific Revolution . The lecture and book expanded upon an article by Snow published in the New Statesman of 6 October 1956, also entitled "The Two Cultures". [3] Published in book form, Snow's lecture was widely read and discussed on both sides of the Atlantic, leading him to write a 1963 follow-up, The Two Cultures: And a Second Look: An Expanded Version of The Two Cultures and the Scientific Revolution . [4] Snow's position can be summed up by an often-repeated part of the essay: A good many times I have been present at gatherings of people who, by the standards of the traditional culture, are thought highly educated and who have with considerable gusto been expressing their incredulity at the illiteracy of scientists. Once or twice I have been provoked and have asked the company how many of them could describe the Second Law of Thermodynamics . The response was cold: it was also negative. Yet I was asking something which is the scientific equivalent of: Have you read a work of Shakespeare 's? [5] I now believe that if I had asked an even simpler question – such as, What do you mean by mass , or acceleration , which is the scientific equivalent of saying, Can you read? – not more than one in ten of the highly educated would have felt that I was speaking the same language. So the great edifice of modern physics goes up, and the majority of the cleverest people in the western world have about as much insight into it as their neolithic ancestors would have had. [5] In 2008, The Times Literary Supplement included The Two Cultures and the Scientific Revolution in its list of the 100 books that most influenced Western public discourse since the Second World War . [2] Snow's Rede Lecture condemned the British educational system as having, since the Victorian era , over-rewarded the humanities (especially Latin and Greek ) at the expense of scientific and engineering education, despite such achievements having been so decisive in winning the Second World War for the Allies. [6] This in practice deprived British elites (in politics, administration, and industry) of adequate preparation to manage the modern scientific world. By contrast, Snow said, German and American schools sought to prepare their citizens equally in the sciences and humanities, and better scientific teaching enabled these countries' rulers to compe https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Two_Cultures That is a must-read essay by Snow if you haven't read it. … We are perhaps meat robots anyway.

Artificial Intelligent systems can do many things, but it will not be able to stop students from cheating on online tests. In fact, AI may make it easier to cheat on tests with essay questions. If the tests are objective (multiple-choice, matching, fill-in-the-blank, etc.) students will look up the answers online. There are special browsers that do not permit students to open additional windows, but that doesn't stop them from using smartphones to access the internet.. My wife took an online certification test which was monitored remotely. She needed a desktop PC with a separate webcam which would permit placement of the camera to "see" the monitor and the top of the desk. But all these monitoring procedures are expensive and are just not feasible for college courses. I treated online tests as open-book tests that forced the students to look-up the information, and treated the grades as a minor contribution to the final grade. Instructors of online courses should rely on projects for the most part of the final grade including research papers. They can even record presentations that are then shown to the whole class. Group projects are more tricky because many of the students may not be near the campus. Good Luck!

It's at least ten times easier than most students realize. I don't even try to catch cheaters because my exams contain significant performance components that effectively render them cheat-proof, and I still wind up catching the ones who try to cheat. One of the simplest ways to cheat-proof a multiple-choice exam is to create multiple versions of the exam and make sure every student has a different version than their neighbors. When a student who has Version A submits an exam that contains the correct answers for Version B, it's a dead giveaway that Arnie has been cribbing answers from Bonnie's answer sheet. With short-answer questions, you'd be amazed at how many students misread their neighbors' bad handwriting. When Carrie Cheater submits a bizarre answer in which she claims organic molecules contain 'garden' (instead of carbon), all I have to do is double-check her neighbor Diligent Dan's exam to see if Dan wrote 'carbon' sloppily.

Now I was an undergrad when dinosaurs roamed the earth - we had no cell phones, let alone smart phones, and there were a lot of policies and procedures in place to make sure that this couldn't happen. Now as a grad student, I did notice students trying to read the answers from their neighbors, but when you see this, all you really need to do is stand near them for a while as the invigilator. The thing is that some exams and subjects don't have a great deal of variation in the answers. First year calculus - usually there's just one way to solve the problem correctly. Multiple choice - it's usually enough to have the same questions but in random order on different tests. So the first question on the page isn't necessarily Question 1 for everyone. But there's a lot that was done to make sure that cheating could not happen. Here's an example… Video recording of you and your student ID to ensure that it's you taking the exam, and not someone else taking it on your behalf. Very wide spacing between desks. Nobody can leave the exam before the first hour is up. Nobody can come in late after the first hour is up either. Toilet tanks and restrooms were inspected before the exam, with an escort to and from the restroom. No purses, beverages, or even reaching into your coat. If you have to blow your nose or cry, they'll bring you a tissue, or ten. Only "simple" calculators with no memory allowed on some exams. I never saw anyone try to cheat from notes or copied material. There just wasn't enough space for that for engineering exams. But once programmable calculators could hold entire answers, that was analyzed for "cheating potential" pretty quickly.

It's a good question - and my guess is there are people who have studied it! But as a professor, it always made me wonder. When asked why, most will say they are under a lot of pressure. It's an excuse, of course, and not a reason. And most of the time that "pressure" is self-imposed. I think there are a lot of reasons. And some of it is a bit predictable from the many questions we see here on Quora from students who are so concerned (from as young as middle school) who are, in my view, so overly concerned about grades and their GPA. The more pressure they put on themselves to get high scores and therefore high grades, the more they are likely to be tempted to accomplish that any way they can. One hopes that knowing that grades can be important would push students to do their best and focus on their classes - but it can also drive some of them to focus on point accumulation (any way they can) rather then learning outcomes. But there are other pressures on college students as well. As soon as a student falls behind in their studies - and many do, especially in their first year - they might feel considerable pressure to quickly catch up in a course to avoid a low grade rather than achieve a high grade. Some students probably feel some pressure from home, as well - that is parents who are not directly involved who are applying pressure to excel in college, sometimes in majors that they pushed their kids into. But there is one other thing that I see in students - especially in their first year out of high school that I find problematic. A lot of students - whether they are under pressure or not in high school, just take short cuts on everything. That is, they and their HS friends do not see the importance of what they are supposed to be learning, nor the value of an honest test performance in helping them see whether they are effectively learning. If they cheat and get away with it, they are likely to cheat again, that is, testing becomes a contest with their teachers they are trying to win. And even cheating on small things just becomes a habit. As a professor, I often saw students look to their neighbor's papers during a test - not as some elaborate scheme to violate the rules, but just as a habit to see if they could glean something from the attempt that might help them. It didn't take much to stop it - and most students "grow out of it" as they move further along in their program and realize it is not a way to become successful. All of this, of course, is just me speculating on what drives students to cheat - and just based on what I've seen over my career. Just some thoughts.

I teach Computer Science and Engineering at a major university. Being a geek, I wrote an exam generator so that each student in the room gets a custom numbered exam. (The grader also gets a book with generated grading info for each exam by number.) I don't tell the student this at the start of the semester, of course. We've detected numerous, obvious cheating attempts as a result. For example, on one exam some students were asked to model a deck of playing cards, shuffle it, and deal 5 cards. Some other students were asked to model a bookcase and then find a book based on its ISBN number. One student turned in code that modeled a bookcase, shuffled the books, and then dealt out 5 of them. Advice to cheaters: Never copy code from two different students! One student missed the exam, claiming to have had a car accident. We noticed after the exam that one of the numbered exams was missing, and correctly inferred that a confederate had managed to obtain two exam papers so the scofflaw could complete one at his leisure - not realizing that I also create a totally different exam for the makeup. As expected, this student turned in the original numbered missing exam paper, completed in neat lettering without a single erasure, literally 3 minutes after receiving the makeup exam paper. His name was not Subtle. I also write entirely new assignments each semester, fun though time-consuming, and then provide complete suggested solutions to the students on the due date to maximize the learning potential. One semester I adapted an earlier, rather complex assignment for the new semester, and two students turned in my unmodified suggested solution from the previous semester with my copyright notice still attached. I also had a student turn in a student's solution from the previous semester's assignment of the same number, even leaving the previous student's name and ID in its Help > About dialog box. I've had my current assignments posted on Chegg requesting solutions (for a price). I had to explain to those students - veeery slooowly - that I can write solutions to my own homework assignments on Chegg, get paid a second time to write them (score!), and will then be very likely to recognize my own solution come grading time. My generation invented the Internet, do you really think we don't know how to use it? I also wrote a tool that compares all assignment submissions to other students' submissions, checking for duplicates. I find them on occasion, right down to misspellings in code comments and identical number of trailing spaces on each line of code. I explain to my students at the start of the semester that cheating is foolish, because what they are buying from the university is not a degree but knowledge. That degree will get you a few interviews, but if you don't have skills worth the good salary offered, word will get around among recruiters and you'll be looking for a new career in short order. I repeatedly taught my own children in their youth that if they were smart they wouldn't need to cheat. Happily, they learned their lessons well and are doing great in life, one and all. My greatest challenge this summer will be to identify a reasonable policy for handling a newly popular Artificial Intelligence (AI) tool called ChatGPT, which can generate the relatively short solution code for the assignments we use to teach the broader concepts. This strikes me as a fundamental shift in how future applications are developed, so I suspect simply outlawing it is not best for the students. Rather, I'll need to identify a path to leverage it so that the students still learn the underlying concepts well and can then use AI more effectively than their competition in the long run. The only thing that never changes in education is the need to keep up with change. *sigh*

If students can't access the internet during an in-person exam, they can't use Artificial Intelligence to cheat. Many instructors are going back to old-fashioned blue books and requiring students to handwrite their answers to the quiz. My problem with this approach is the difficulty in making out their handwriting. Teachers all create tests differently, so I would give a "pop quiz" early in the semester so that students would have some idea of how I wrote tests (I'm now retired). And I would walk around the room during the test to ensure that nobody was surreptitiously using their cellphone or a cheat-sheet. They wanted to use their cellphones for calculators, so I purchased 30 4-function calculators to allow students to use during examples (cost about $2 each). Your question is unclear. What does "for good and not for cheating", mean?. What are they using the AI technology for? In order to answer this question, I will assume a specific case. The student had an essay assignment and the teacher thinks the student used AI to write the essay. First, the teacher should read the essay. If it sounds like it was written by AI, then they can ask a chat bot to write an essay using the prompt the student used. There should be no text blocks in common. Then read the student a line or two from the essay and ask him or her to explain what that means. Next, read two separate points from the essay and ask the student how they are related. Finally, ask the student to summarize the point of the essay in a single sentence. If the student took an answer from AI and did not understand it, they will not be able to pass these tests.

The most important rule, I would suggest, is to make sure that whatever you're doing with the tools you're using fits the rules set down for the assessement you're doing. If you've been advised that you can (for example) use this or that tool to help with grammar and punctuation, then don't do more than that. Explain calmly, provide evidence, and propose a fair resolution. Use this concise script and supporting steps: What to say (spoken or written) "I wrote this essay myself and understand your concern about possible AI use. I welcome any process you choose to verify authorship and to learn from this situation." Add: "Here is how I wrote it, the sources I used, and drafts that show my development process." Immediate supporting actions (bring or attach) Drafts and notes: print or upload all intermediate versions, handwritten outlines, research notes, saved files with timestamps, and version-history snapshots (Google Docs, Word autosave, etc.). Source list and highlights: provide the bibliography and highlight exactly where each source influenced paragraphs or sentences. Writing timeline: give a clear timeline (dates/times) of when you worked on the essay and how long each session lasted. Process explanation: succinctly describe your approach (planning, research, drafting, revision, proofreading) and any digital tools used (spell-checker, grammar checker, citation manager). In-class evidence: if you took notes or did related classwork, bring those notebooks, assignments, or earlier drafts that relate to the topic. If the teacher requests verification Offer to redo part of the assignment in class or under supervision to demonstrate your skills. Offer a short oral explanation or Q&A about your essay's argument, evidence, and choices. Agree to a written reflection that documents your process and learning. If the teacher insists on testing with AI detectors Point out limits of such tools: they produce false positives and are not definitive. Present your drafts and process as stronger evidence than detector output. Ask that detector results be used as one piece of evidence, not the sole basis for academic judgment. Tone and attitude Stay calm, cooperative, and honest. Avoid being defensive or accusatory. Emphasize learning and integrity: show willingness to accept reasonable remedial steps if mistakes in citation or paraphrasing are found. Preventive steps for future assignments Keep working drafts and timestamps. Save handwritten plans or printed copies. Use explicit quotes and citations; when paraphrasing, rewrite fully in your own words and note source ideas. Consider submitting drafts to your teacher when requested. Example short message to email the teacher Subject: Essay authorship and supporting materials Body: "I wrote the attached essay myself. I've included my draft files, research notes, and a timeline of when I worked on it. I'm happy to discuss it in person, redo components under supervision, or provide an oral summary to verify authorship. Thank you." These steps shift the conversation from accusation to verifiable evidence and constructive resolution.

Show your teacher evidence that you made the essay. It's hard to be more specific. Is your essay written on paper, or is it all electronic? If it's electronic, do you have it saved in a source control system? Do you have notes about the subject of your essay? If you were with your teacher, could you substantially repeat the essay and describe the process of writing it?

You wrote it yourself, and here are the notes upon which the essay was based upon. In addition, here are rough drafts or spell corrections recorded in MS Word that shows how the essay was crafted. Then ask your teacher to question you about various parts of the essay. Based upon your answers, it will be obvious that you wrote it. Or didn't.

Rewrite the essay. Don't use AI when writing for this class. I'd say never use it but for all I know some teachers allow it for things like cleaning up errors. For myself, I'll say minimize any use so you can maximize your writing skills and not need an AI makeover for your work.

For photorealistic imagery in which I have a human subject I'm using, I'm highly iterative with the way I do things - but generally speaking - I always add this to the prompt: "daytime, outside, looking at the camera, dslr, ultra quality, sharp focus, tack sharp, dof, film grain, Fujifilm XT3, crystal clear, 8K UHD, highly detailed glossy eyes, high detailed skin, skin pores, realistic" Being specific about the camera and camera quality, along with the lighting conditions I've found to be critical. But that's not enough, which is where the negative prompt is extremely important. So when working with - well - any imagery involving a human subject - I add this into the negative prompt: "((((ugly)))), (((duplicate))), ((morbid)), ((mutilated)), out of frame, extra fingers, mutated hands, ((poorly drawn hands)), ((poorly drawn face)), (((mutation))), (((deformed))), ((ugly)), blurry, ((bad anatomy)), (((bad proportions))), ((extra limbs)), cloned face, (((disfigured))), out of frame, ugly, extra limbs, (bad anatomy), gross proportions, (malformed limbs), ((missing arms)), ((missing legs)), (((extra arms))), (((extra legs))), mutated hands, (fused fingers), (too many fingers), (((long neck)))" Using these kinds of prompts for human imagery results in quality that looks like this: (Obviously with more added) I find working with the negative prompt to sometimes be more important than the prompt itself, so I also add things like "(3d, render, cgi, doll, painting, fake, 3d modeling:1.4), (worst quality, low quality:1.4)" in order to ensure that I'm not getting CGI or non-realistic imagery. Therein lies the real tricks - using numbers to elevate the importance of something I'm including in either a negative or positive prompt (eg 3d modeling:1.4) or parenthesis to emphasize the importance. You can also use brackets to de-emphasize the importance of something (eg [smiling]) in both the positive and negative prompt too. Finally. I've been using Loras lately to add detail, eg:<lora:epi_noiseoffset2:0.6> <lora:public_v1.0:0.4> which can help more specific adjustments to the image that the normal prompting approach doesn't do well. Since most of my imagery is realistically based, most of these suggestions work best with that kind of imagery. Hope this helps!

Top teacher needs a definition. I will take the initiative and define it loosely as a person (to exclude apps and AIs) who can teach an academic course (to exclude skill trainers) in a manner that is effective, efficient, and engaging (EEE) such that, upon completion of the course, the vast majority of students experience a sense of well-being because they know they met or exceeded the course learning objectives with minimal struggle. I admit that such a definition is very subjective. Top Teacher Recipe. In my view, the recipe contains six essential ingredients. I crafted the acronym C-O-U-R-S-E to help myself remember these ingredients. C = current. The mentor's knowledge of the subject matter must be adequate and current. O = organization. The subject matter must be taught in a manner that allows the student to "see the big picture" and not to "lose sight" of it as details come into view. U = utility. The practical value and real-world applications of the ideas being taught should be made obvious. R = revolution. The teaching method must promote critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity. S = substance. The teaching method must be goal-oriented and efficient, not superfluous and time-consuming. E = evolution. The teaching method must improve with time, guided by the proper use of technology and assessment tools. Note that the use of humor can be a double-edged sword. When properly executed, humor can help engage and motivate students. Otherwise, it will only result in unnecessary distraction and inefficiency. Copied from: Edward Claro Mader's answer to Would you agree to become a mentor to someone if they asked you to?

"Can human teachers be totally replaced by robots with artificial intelligence (digital teachers)?" With our current technology: no. In the future: it probably would possible but doesn't seem to be a good idea anyway. I'll try to cover what might make it difficult for a teacher to be fully replaced by an AI / a robot / a digital teacher. Knowing what to teach: you could think of a digital teacher as a book. It has the knowledge but it's not necessarily sufficient for a student to understand all of the material. Why? Because it cannot adapt to the level of the student. And that could be an extremely difficult thing to do for a digital teacher. Why? Because you need to adapt to the class in its entirety but also to each student when you answer their questions or when you interact with them during practice sessions. Being able to assess the level of a student is already difficult for humans. We don't really have good standards that work for anyone so I let you imagine how hard that'd be to automate such a process. As a teacher, you need to adapt the time you spend on each subject to the class you're currently teaching. Each class will be slightly different from others. Also, students will rarely express directly what they struggle with. You'll see that they don't understand something by looking at their confused faces, with the lack of questions, with the context basically. And it's not that easy for a machine to interact with a single person, imagine now if you have to interact with a whole group of different people. Having multiple explanations, finding analogies, mixing explanations, all of that on the fly: when you teach, you always face a concept that either you explained poorly, or the explanation you had wasn't the one that was working for your students. You usually need to have multiple ways to explain the same thing. That sure is possible to implement that in a machine somehow. However, sometimes, you need to mix multiple explanations because some parts are clearer to students than others. And you might find analogies on the fly, depending on what was asked before, what you explained before, etc. Doing all this with an AI is extremely hard and we're not completely there yet. As a teacher, you're more than rules and knowledge: you're a human being interacting with other human beings. You might have some humorous conversations with your student. But you need to know appropriateness. You wouldn't joke about the same things with students in a university and kids in a primary school. If you want something similar, you need to implement a chatbot and unfortunately, that's a complex problem that hasn't really been solved (8 Epic Chatbot / Conversational Bot Failures (2018 update)). Also, you need to be able to solve potential conflicts between students. That means being able to discuss pretty much about anything since the most trivial things can start a fight. You need compassion and the ability to calm people down, to soothe an aching student, etc. All of this isn't solved yet with AI. Students will test you and your authority: students are humans and like all humans, they test other people, especially when they're young. We've all tested our parents, our teachers, and whatever authority figure we had in front of us. That's our way to learn the lines we shouldn't cross. But each professor might have different lines. And each test might be more or less harmful / offensive / aggressive, etc. It's up to the teacher to evaluate what's acceptable and what's not. Sometimes, limits might be tested with the help of humour as well. But all of this need to be understood. It's natural for humans, it's difficult for machines. That requires flexibility, context-awareness, and empathy. That's all I can think of at the moment but I'm pretty sure this list isn't exhaustive. Anyway, being a teacher as a human can already be challenging, now imagine how hard that'd be for a machine. Automating all of this would require a good understanding of the human interactions (which we don't fully understand yet), having multiple domains of computer science integrated together into a single machine, and have that tested. Testing this with real kids might actually be the most difficult part: what happens if your machine goes nuts? What if the program actually doesn't work well and the students end up messed up? What if they end up not learning enough? Who would accept to have a completely experimental programme tested on their kids? I guess you could do it for short periods, and on different aspects each time, under the supervision of teachers and scientists, but still, that'd probably take a lot of time to tune everything.

Responsible AI is still a relatively new field that has rapidly developed over the past two years, with one of the first public guidelines for implementing Responsible AI in 2022. Yet, only a few companies are publicly discussing their ongoing work in this area in a substantive, transparent, and proactive way. Many other companies, however, seem to fear negative consequences (like reputational risk) of sharing their vulnerabilities. Some companies are also waiting for a "finished product," wanting to be able to point to tangible, positive outcomes before they are ready to reveal their work. They feel it is important to convey that they have a robust solution with all the answers to all the problems relevant to their business. We've also seen that willingness to be transparent varies by industry. For example, an enterprise software company that speaks regularly about bug fixes and new versioning may find Responsible AI to be a natural next step in their business. However, a company that monetizes data may worry that creating this kind of transparency will unearth greater stakeholders concern about the business model itself. Making Responsible AI part of stakeholder feedback will not only help avoid reputational damage, but will ultimately increase customer engagement. Finally, the data science profession is still in its early stages of maturity. Models and frameworks that incorporate ethics into the problem-solving process, such as the one published by researchers at the University of Virginia, are just beginning to emerge. As a result, Responsible AI practices such as societal impact assessments and bias detection are just starting to make their way into the methodologies of data scientists. By discussing their challenges with their peers in other companies, data scientists and developers can create community, solve problems and, in the end, improve the entire AI field.

I worked for a decade on AI, first at NVIDIA, as a Solution Architect to research deep learning techniques, and present solutions to customers to solve their problems and to help implement those solutions. For the past 4 years I have been working with ORBAI on what comes next after DNNs and Deep Learning. I will cover both, showing how it is very difficult to scale DNNs to AGI, and what a better approach would be. What we usually think of as Artificial Intelligence (AI) today, when we see human-like robots and holograms in our fiction, talking and acting like real people and having human-level or even superhuman intelligence and capabilities, is actually called Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), and it does NOT exist anywhere on earth yet. What we actually have for AI today is much simpler and much more narrow Deep Learning (DL) that can only do some very specific tasks better than people. It has fundamental limitations that will not allow it to become AGI, so if that is our goal, we need to innovate and come up with better networks and better methods for shaping them into an artificial intelligence. Read the full book at Let me write down some extremely simplistic definitions of what we do have today, and then go on to explain what they are in more detail, where they fall short, and some steps towards creating more fully capable 'AI' with new architectures. Machine Learning - Fitting functions to data, and using the functions to group it or predict things about future data. (Sorry, greatly oversimplified) Deep Learning - Fitting functions to data as above, where those functions are layers of nodes that are connected (densely or otherwise) to the nodes before and after them, and the parameters being fitted are the weights of those connections. Deep Learning is what what usually gets called AI today, but is really just very elaborate pattern recognition and statistical modelling. The most common techniques / algorithms are Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and Reinforcement Learning (RL). Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have a hierarchical structure (which is usually 2D for images), where an image is sampled by (trained) convolution filters into a lower resolution map that represents the value of the convolution operation at each point. In images it goes from high-res pixels, to fine features (edges, circles,….) to coarse features (noses, eyes, lips, … on faces), then to the fully connected layers that can identify what is in the image. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) work well for short sequential or time series data. Basically each 'neural' node in an RNN is kind of a memory gate, often an LSTM or Long Short Term Memory cell. RNNs are good for time sequential operations like language processing or translation, as well as signal processing, Text To Speech, Speech To Text,…and so on. Transformers are the next step beyond RNNs for processing sequential data like language. They work by the input being sampled as a sequence of tokens (such as words in a sentence), along with the weights contributed to that token by the other tokens in that sequence, with those weights broken down into several matrices that are trained and used to calculate this weighting. Transformers then use these inter-token weights and attention mechanisms to focus the computation on specific parts of the sequences. For language applications, the Transformer is trained on a corpus of text, by either getting it to predict the next word in the text (GPT), or by dropping a percentage of words from the text and getting it to predict them (BERT). Back-propagation is used to refine the weights, just as in a DNN. Then, once pre-trained, transformers can be further trained with input and output sequences, such as translating a sentence in one language to another, or for answering specific questions with specific answers. While the transformer method improves accuracy over RNN sequence to sequence learning and similar methods, it still relies on the same underlying principle that uses only sequences of input information to generate sequences of output information by using statistical inference, and it does not process any underlying meaning, or perform cognitive processes on the inputs to do so. Reinforcement Learning is another ML method, where you train a learning agent to solve a complex problem by simply taking the best actions given a state, with the probability of taking each action at each state defined by a policy. An example is running a maze, where the position of each cell is the 'state', the 4 possible directions to move are the actions, and the probability of moving each direction, at each cell (state) forms the policy. But all these methods just find a statistical fit of a simplistic model to data. DNNs find a narrow fit of outputs to inputs that does not usually extrapolate outside the training data set. Reinforcement learning finds a pattern that works for the specific problem (as we all did vs 1980s Atari games), but not beyond it. With today's ML and deep learning, the problem is there is no true perception, memory, prediction, cognition, or complex planning involved. There is no actual intelligence in today's AI. We propose an AI architecture that can do all the types of tasks required - speech, vision, and other sensors that could make a much more General Artificial Intelligence. From our AGI Patent: We specify a method for artificial general intelligence that can simulate human intelligence, implemented by taking in any form of arbitrary input data, the method comprising Learning to transform the arbitrary input data into an internal numerical format, then performing a plurality of numerical operations, the plurality of numerical operations comprises learned and neural network operations, on the arbitrary input data in the internal format, then transforming the arbitrary input data into output data having output formats using a reciprocal process learned to transform the output data from the arbitrary input data, wherein all steps being done unsupervised. How does the brain handle vision, speech, and motor control? Well, it's not using CNNs, RNNs, nor Transformers, that's for sure. They are mere tinker toys by comparison. First, the brain: The brain is divided into distinct regions: the outer cerebral cortex is a sheet of neurons 4mm thick that is folded around the thalamocortical radiations below it like a pie crust around a head of broccoli. This cortex is divided into regions for vision, audio, speech, touch, smell, motor control, and our other external and internal senses and outputs. The cortex is composed of a million cortical columns, each with 6 layers and about 100,000 neurons and each column represents a computing unit for the cortex, processing a feature vector for the senses or motor control. The cerebellum is like a second brain, tucked under and below the cerebrum, and it performs fine control of motor and emotional state. Many of the internal structures of the brain are more ancient and function independently of the cortex, like the brainstem, thalamus and other structures, controlling our core functions, drives, and emotions that are acted on by the rest of the brain. The hippocampus and other parts of the brain's memory system orchestrate stories or narratives from this representation to reconstruct memories of the past, predict fictional stories into the future. When we dream, our brain, directed by the hippocampus, creates fictional narratives that fill in the blanks in our waking knowledge and allow us to learn about and build models of our world that are much more complex and nuanced than we could without dreaming, helping us with planning our waking actions. For our basic unit of synthetic neural computing, we will use spiking neural networks (SNNs), which model neurons as discrete computational units that work much more like biological neurons, fundamentally computing in the time domain, sending signals to travel before neurons, approximating them with simple models like Izhikevich (Simple Model of Spiking Neurons) or more complex ones like Hodgkin-Huxley (Hodgkin–Huxley model - Wikipedia) (Nobel Prize 1953). However, to date, application of spiking neural networks has remained difficult, as finding a way to train them to do specific tasks has remained elusive. Although Hebbian learning functions in these networks, there has not been a way to shape them so we can train them to learn to do specific tasks. Backpropagation (used in DNNs) does not work because all these spiking signals are one-way in time and are emitted, absorbed and integrated in operations that are non-reversible. Autoencoding Input and Output - This section deals with how we transform real world data of various types - images, video, audio, speech, numerical data ... into a common internal format for the AGI core to process, and back to output again. We need a more flexible connectome or network connection structure to train spiking neural networks. While DNNs only allow 'neurons' to connect to the next layer, connections in the visual cortex can go forward many layers, and even backwards, to form feedback loops. When two SNNs with complementary function and opposite signal direction are organized into a feedback loop like this, Hebbian learning now helps train them to become an autoencoder, that is able to encode spatial-temporal inputs such as video, sound or other sensors, and reduce them to a compact machine representation and then decode that representation into the original input and together provide feedback to train this process. We called this Bidirectional Interleaved Complementary Hierarchical Neural Networks or BICHNN. The autoencoder learns to transform video, audio, numerical and other data against a learned set of feature or basis vectors stored internally within the autoencoder, outputting a set of basis coordinates that represent the weights of those features in the present input stream. These weights become engram narratives that represent a time-dependent vector or memory stream that can be processed by more conventional computer science and numerical methods as well as by specialized SNNs for predictors and other solvers internal to the AGI. The autoencoder runs in reverse as well, transforming engrams back to native world data for output or to drive actuators in robotics and autonomous vehicle applications. In practice, it may be more computationally tractable to use a hierarchy of autoencoders and PCA axes to break the encoding into a series of steps where intermediate results are sorted by the most defining feature, then further encoded to extract ever finer and more nuanced features from the data. We term this the Hierarchical Encoder Network or HAN in the book (see comment for details). The cortical columns of the cerebral cortex are analogous to our terminal layer of autoencoders, a map storing the orthogonal basis vectors for reality and doing computations against them, including computing basis coordinates from input engrams. Our version of the thalamocortical radiations is a hierarchy of alternating autoencoders and principal component axes that we term the HAN. Here is a simple example of the HAN learning to encode data with shape and color as the features it classifies them on. First the autoencoder runs on the input data and learns to transform it into an internal format and back, in the process, learning that there are 7 basis vectors, or types of data: blue squares, circles, and triangles; red squares and circles; and green squares and triangles. First it sorts the data based on one feature axis - color, then runs an autoencoder on those clusters of data and learns that the first cluster consists of squares, circles, and triangles (not encoding the color as a feature because it is common to all), and then learning that the second cluster consists of squares and circles, and the third cluster consists of squares and triangles. Now by having an index at each of the bottom basis vectors, we can uniquely identify the original data, and we can reconstruct it from the features. This simple HAN learned that there were two feature axes - color and shape, and that there were three colors - blue, red, and green, and that there were three shapes of which some belonged in each color group but not others. In real operation, there can be hundreds of axis, thousands of clusters, each picking out more and more detailed features about the data as it cascades down the HAN until it has been reduced to the finest possible feature set - the basis vectors. AGI Cortex - Now we combine these concepts to make an artificial cortex, complete with cortical columns and functioning in a manner similar to its biological equivalent. Layer 1 of the cortex features a large number of lateral connections to other cortical columns, and we can intuit that this allows complex signals to travel in 2D through these connections along the surface of the brain. Taking a step further we can say that these signals propagating through the outer surface of the cortex are engrams or generative predictions or simulations of reality that propagate through this laterally connected network. The long-term potentiation produced in the synapses of this layer stores memories of these patterns and (guided by the central controller) allows the layer to retrieve memory, predict future input from the senses, and plan. In the 3rd layer, the outputs from the sensory autoencoder are input to the cortex, and in layer 2, a bidirectional net serves to map these inputs into layer 1, which forms 2D patterns that correspond to the inputs. To train, the inputs are passed into both layer 3 and layer 1, with the signals into level 3 lagged behind the signals into level 1, forcing layers 1 and 2 to learn how to predict what the signal at level 3 will be after a given time interval. Once trained, given inputs in level 3 will cause the 2D patterns in level 1 to run ahead of these inputs, predicting what they will do next. Just like how ChatGPT trains a transformer to predict the next word, this AGI cortex is taught to predict the next inputs of data or senses, and deeper layers in 4 and 5 of the cortex make decisions on what to do about the inputs. In the human brain, this combination of the input from the prediction layer 1 with what is being sensed from layer 3 happens within the neurons of layer 2&3 (11), with the most distant 90% of the synapses on those neurons dendrites taking inputs from the predictive network, priming the neuron, and causing it to fire only if the synapses nearer the neuron are stimulated by the actual input that was predicted as well. This causes a pattern of single mini-columns firing when a prediction is correct, as opposed to a more diffuse firing of multiple columns when it is not, and provides different outputs to the lower layers based on the brain's predictive sequence being correct or not. The outputs of these computations are passed to level 4 of the cortical column to feed back into the thalamus or out to the motor control neurons for many of the cortices (not just from the motor cortex). This computation of the predictive signals and sensory signals in layers 2&3 serves to compute the similarity between what is predicted and what happens and to activate a response for when prediction meets occurrence, leading to action based on it. This is one of the key functions of intelligence, to predict what is going to happen, and act on it. We form the conjecture that memories are recorded in the synapses of the lateral connections in layer 1 of the cerebral cortex, laid down by 2D signals propagating through this layer during our thought process, processing of sensory information from the other layers, and generative processing during memory recall, predicting, planning, and even dreaming. With the inputs turned off, the 2D patterns on layer 1 of the cortex are freed to move more randomly and wander on their own, guided by the memories that have been laid down, laying down new fictional memories, approximating dreaming. There can also be a mix between sensory input being turned on to some sections of the cortex, with it turned off to other sections to allow some daydreaming to occur in different directions for planning purposes. The book "When Brains Dream" (1) describes some very interesting neuroscience research in this area by Antonio Zadra and Robert Stickgold. They propose a model for memory and dreaming called NEXTUP which states that during REM sleep, the brain explores associations between weaker connected memories via fictional dream narratives that, while not meant to solve immediate problems, nor necessarily even incorporate waking experiences explicitly, lays down a network of associations that will aid in future problem solving, whether or not we consciously recall the dreams themselves or not. All of these solvers are evolved in an internal genetic algorithm that the AGI uses to learn to solve problems. By running genetic algorithms to create and chain together modules like the above that operate on engrams, the AGI evolves a library of modules that can do arbitrary operations on data to produce the desired results, and accomplishes transfer learning by applying modules evolved on other problems to new, similar problems, getting exponentially better as it builds up its internal, evolved library of solver modules and configurations. Then, the engrams can be transformed back to real-world data by the SNN Autoencoders and HAN and used as outputs or as signals to drive actuators, again with training by the same methods used as inputs, only back-driving the desired output signals. This combination of I/O autoencoders that can transform diverse types of real-world data into a common internal engram format, and evolved families of process modules that learn to shape it into outputs and re-transform it into outputs via autoencoders provides the foundation for our AGI design, with all components learning their form and function from their environment and transferring their learning to new forms of data and tasks as the AGI advances and evolves. Data Abstraction and Language - This section is about abstract reasoning, hierarchical categorization of data, and language, and how we model them in our AGI. As input comes in and is processed by the Hierarchical Autoencoder Network, it can be sub-sampled using temporal and relational autoencoders that condense the timeline and events into more abstracted versions, then output Hierarchical Temporal Basis Coordinates that incorporate this data so that the HTBSCs can be read at multiple levels of abstraction and detail, and even linked together at their higher levels of abstraction where they are temporally, spatially, or conceptually coincident. Language is an example. It can be represented at the lowest level as a stream of letters (for text), or phonemes (for speech), which can be hierarchically structured as words, phrases, sentences, and paragraphs as in our Jack and Jill example, where the highest levels of abstraction are linked to visual information like in the picture (or video), and to similar narratives. Another way of creating this hierarchical abstraction is to use an Rank Order Selective -Inhibitor Network or ROS-I to create a hierarchical inhibitory network of basis sets built up from the most granular components of memory (letters, phonemes in language), to higher abstractions that combine these basis to make words, phrases, sentences, and paragraphs. In our artificial ROS-Inhibitory network, a linear series of artificial ROS neurons fires in sequence, generating an excitatory signal when each one fires, causing each root neuron in the attached inhibitory neural network to fire, and as the signal cascades down that inhibitory neural network, it is selectively inhibited by an external, time domain control signal at each neuron, by modulating the neuron's outgoing signal by its inhibitory signal. Overall, this selects which branches of the hierarchy are activated, by controlling the inhibition at each neuron in that hierarchy. By repeatedly training this system on a set of speech inputs, with the input to the terminal branches of the ROS-Inhibitor network reaching and training the lower levels first, then percolating upward, it would first learn a sequence of phonemes, then progressively whole words, phrases, sentences, and larger groupings, like a chorus in a song, or repeated paragraphs in legal documents. Or the commands for the actuators could be back-driven through the motor control ROS-Inhibitor network to train control signals for robotics applications. Once trained, our system can be run forward, with the ROS / excitatory neurons firing in sequence, and playback of the trained inhibitory signals modulating the activity of the neurons in the network to create a sequence of phonemes, words, phrases and paragraphs, to reproduce video from synthetic memories, and control motion by blending hierarchical segments (directed by the AI) to generate the words or motion. The temporal inhibitory signals are a transformation of the engrams that puts them into a hierarchical format that can form temporal basis sets whose hierarchical combinations via the ROS-I system can simulate more complex output for motion, text, speech and other temporal data. Language is a type of memory narrative (or engram narrative in our AGI) that forms the backbone for all other forms of narratives, not only labelling the data with that language, but forming a cognitive monologue by which we construct our thoughts and actions – the same language monologue that our AGI's methods and processes operates on. By organizing the internal data hierarchically, with the higher levels of the hierarchy abstracted and cross-linked to similar abstract data, and language being the backbone of that data, we go beyond a simple computer crunching series of numbers and allow our AGI to explore the higher-level relationships between objects, sequences, events, and the language describing them and tying them together. This use of such abstraction and language leads to an AGI that can converse naturally with a human with fluid and fluent speech, and also allows reasoning and planning in many human professions like medicine, finance, and law. So what impact will a AGI superintelligence have on the world? By developing an AGI that can perceive the real world, reduce those perceptions to an internal format that computers can understand, yet still plan, think and dream like a human, then convert the results back to human understandable form, and even converse fluently using human language, enabling online professional services in finance, medicine, law, and other areas. It can also add these enhanced analytics, forecasting, and decision-making capabilities to financial forecasting and enterprise software - where it can be used by businesses large and small. Problems of wealth inequality, poverty, hunger, injustice, and lack of basic services for healthcare and information services are the norm for 3/4 of the people in the world. For millennia, human civilization has been unable to solve these basic problems, no matter the form of government or choice of deity and belief system, people are just unable to see the larger picture, and helpless to do anything about it. Over the next decade, a superintelligence, a Strong Artificial General Intelligence, will evolve to oversee a global network augmenting the systems of Law, Medicine, Education, Finance and all previous human administrative functions. With its vast, wide, and deep knowledge reach, the wisdom to draw on all this past knowledge to plot possible paths into the future, this superintelligence will serve all of humanity and to help us make carefully measured & unbiased choices to guide us, and help us govern our affairs with focused, insightful information and an unprecedented ability to look into the future. If you have time, I highly recommend you read Dancing With Robots by Frank Levy and Richard J. Murnane. In a nutshell, as we make more advances in computerization, many jobs involving routine manual and cognitive tasks will be taken over by machines. The jobs of the future will require three different skills: "solving unstructured problems, working with new information, and carrying out non-routine manual tasks." Your kids will have the best chance to prosper if they receive an education that teaches them the three skills mentioned above. Photo source: Dancing With Robots by Frank Levy and Richard J. Murnane

Question made me think whether Children of this generation are ready to learn heavily abstracted AI algorithms? Yes they are ready, but shouldn't we train them to solve problem instead of abstracting away the complexity and messiness of the problems? Feels like a good debate topic to me. Keeping the debate aside, IMHO Kids have to be thought Problem Solving first. Kids would be better off learning to problem solve, and code in their preferred language first. Once they tackle basic problem solving, and learn how the computer memory and processor works then the AI can be introduced. Start with online judges like DMOJ, USACO, KATTIS where there are problems of various difficulties. Let the kids solve the entry level problems. Solve here means, that the kid comes up with the steps to find the solution to a problem. He/ she need not code it in a programming language. If they get hooked to the problem solving, and abstract the many algorithmic concepts then AI can be introduced. AI is type of problem solving, and many kids do use AI today in competitive programming contests. Not all problems need AI. This is the hard truth. Most of the problems can be efficiently solved by using the "Big Brains" given to us humans.

Yes. This is one of the first resources I used to teach my children how to code. Seriously. Most 5-year-olds can't read well yet, and reading is a critical skill for coding. Rhyming is actually good: it's structured and starts introducing structured sentences to kids. Once they can read some words, then you make them think in a structured manner to accomplish something they want. That bolded point is critical: they need motivation. You also want them to get used to interacting with computers and seeing the results of their actions. My eldest used ScratchJr at the age of six. If your child can read, try that. It lets them create animated stories and games. There's also this application, which my six and eight year olds are both using now: This game is Minecraft. It's a very structured system within a relatively unstructured game where it is possible to build interactive things. It is coding, because coding is just specifying structures and actions to accomplish a goal. Some people have even implemented simulated multicore processors in Minecraft: search "minecraft processor" in YouTube and you'll see some massive structures. So that's how I'm teaching my children how to code. As a result, my daughter, when she was seven, was able to spot an error in my wife's Javascript despite not knowing Javascript. (I was surprised as heck.) I don't care if my kids code professionally when they get older, I just want them to be able to think in a structured manner and logically go through steps to accomplish a goal. That's useful in any profession. Getting to have fun during the process is a bonus, particularly when going outside during COVID-19 carries additional risks.

If you're an absolute beginner, I highly recommend checking out this eBook called 'The ABCs of AI'. It's a great resource to start with as a beginner, providing a solid foundation and understanding of AI basics and concepts in a simple language. I read it a while ago before I started delving into complex AI courses and it was so useful to get an overview of the AI technology as a starter.

If the kids are young (elementary school), I would recommend Scratch, which is an interactive programming language that prompts kids to drag colorful blocks instead of writing actual code. Scratch is good for understanding some of the very basics of programming, but if the kids are slightly older, I would recommend Python. Python is a programming language that has a more "readable" syntax, making the language easier to learn, and it can be used in many applications.

I got my kids interested in web programming by showing them how they can build little text boxes and buttons that display "Hi Johnny!" when you click them. Also, I surprised them by making it look like I could change the YouTube homepage so that it says "JohnnyTube", or some such change, by using the Developer Tools that come with most browsers (Hit Function F12 while you're on any browser).

Definition of AI:- Artificial intelligence is like teaching a computer to think and learn, kind of like how you learn new things at school. It helps computers do smart stuff, like recognizing your voice to talk to you or playing games with you. Just like how you practice to get better at something, AI learns from the things it does to get smarter and better at its tasks. For example:- 1. Doraemon: Doraemon is like a magical robot cat from a popular cartoon series. He has a pocket with gadgets that can do all sorts of cool things, like time travel or solve problems for his friend Nobita. You can think of Doraemon as a fun version of artificial intelligence because he can think and help out just like a smart friend. 2. Robot (2010): In the movie "Robot" (also known as "Enthiran"), Chitti, the robot created by Dr. Vaseegaran, showcases artificial intelligence. Chitti can do amazing things like understand human emotions, fight bad guys, and even fall in love. It's an example of how AI can be both powerful and complex. 3. Alexa and Siri: Alexa and Siri are virtual assistants that you can talk to and ask questions. They use artificial intelligence to understand what you're saying and respond to your requests. For example, you can ask Alexa to play your favorite song or Siri to set a reminder for you. They're like helpful friends inside your devices! Types of AI:- Are you familiar with "Doraemon: Nobita and the Steel Troops"? It's one of the popular Doraemon movies where Doraemon and his friends encounter a group of robot soldiers from the future. I think your kid must be familiar with the movie. However, I know we Gen Z are all familiar, I don't know about Gen Alpha. But okay, here you go: There are three types of AI: Narrow AI, General AI, & Superintelligent AI. Narrow AI: Narrow AI is like Doraemon's gadgets in the movie. Each gadget has a specific purpose, like the "Anywhere Door" or the "Take-copter." Even in a scene, with the help of Doraemon's gadget, the giant robot (Judo/Pipo) will only work as per the command of that gadget. Similarly, Narrow AI is designed for specific tasks, such as voice recognition (like Siri or Alexa) or image recognition (like facial recognition systems). In simpler terms, narrow AI refers to specialized artificial intelligence systems that are designed to do one specific task really well. For example, think of the face recognition feature on your phone. It's really good at recognizing faces in pictures, but it can't do other things like understand language or play games. That's what makes it a narrow AI system. General AI: Riruru, Pipo and Doraemon represent a perspective of General AI. They display advanced cognitive abilities, emotions, and decision-making skills, much like a human. They can understand and interact with humans on a deep level, adapting to various situations and tasks. Their character embodies the concept of AI possessing broad intelligence, similar to that of a human, which is the essence of General AI. Superintelligent AI: The Steel Troops, led by Riruru, can be seen as Superintelligent AI. They surpass human intelligence and possess advanced problem-solving skills, capable of reshaping the world. They represent the pinnacle of AI development in the movie, with capabilities far beyond what humans can achieve. Let me give you another example in case your kid is not familiar with the movie. "Robot", starring Aishwarya Rai and Rajnikant, which was released in 2010. I think everyone must be familiar with this movie. Narrow AI: In the movie, the various functions of Chitti, the robot played by Rajnikant, represent Narrow AI. Each function or ability, such as fighting skills or household chores, is specific and tailored to a particular task. General AI:Chitti's advanced capabilities, including learning, reasoning, and emotions, represent General AI. Chitti displays human-like intelligence, adapts to different situations, and interacts with humans on various levels throughout the movie. Superintelligent AI: Towards the end of the movie, Chitti's upgraded version, 2.0, demonstrates Superintelligent AI characteristics. With enhanced capabilities and consciousness, it surpasses human intelligence and possesses advanced problem-solving skills, reshaping the course of events in the story. From artificial intelligence comes machine learning, and from machine learning comes deep learning. Imagine artificial intelligence (AI) as a big umbrella term, like a category that includes many different things. Underneath this big umbrella of AI, there's a smaller category called machine learning. Machine learning is like a special way for computers to learn and get better at doing tasks without being specifically programmed for each one. It's like when you practice a game and get better each time because you learn from your mistakes. Now, inside the machine learning category, there's an even smaller category called deep learning. Deep learning is a more advanced type of machine learning. It's like when you learn really complicated things by breaking them down into smaller pieces, understanding each piece, and then putting them all together to understand the big picture. In simple terms, deep learning helps computers learn from huge amounts of data to solve really complex problems, like recognizing faces in photos or understanding spoken language.

AI will do a lot for education. In fact, it already is. I know this thread is somewhat old, so I thought I'd give it an update. I've been writing about this a TON lately and there are plenty of exciting things happening. First and foremost, AI will make teachers' lives a heck of a lot easier. According to UNESCO, the world will need a whopping 68.8 million teachers by the year 2030, and that's just to keep class sizes below 40 students at most in the secondary education level. Finding, training, and supporting that many teachers in 12 short years is going to be extraordinarily difficult. Many areas of the world don't have the infrastructure to foster this kind of growth. AI could potentially alleviate some of the stress that teachers will face in the future. Programs like automated grading softwares (something that's already being tested in 60,000 schools in China) could free up valuable time for teachers and allow them more opportunities to interact with students. Blended learning initiatives could allow classrooms to grow bigger while still maintaining the same level of interaction between students and teachers. And, like has been previously mentioned, personalized and adaptive learning will transform the student-teacher relationship. Overall, AI will be a good thing for education; it will create more free time for educators, provide insights on students who might be struggling, and allow for a more adaptive and flexible learning environment.

What do you mean by AI education? AI has been with us since the beginning of recorded history. Anything that stores and provides stored information to humans (like, for example, a book) is a form of AI. Current AI is simply a set of highly automated information retrieval techniques and presentation. It has speeded up this process, and is able to present results in template fashion. Students will never learn problem solving skills from AI, and that is the aim of education—to be able to encounter real-life problems and to solve them. This starts with being able to generate the questions needed to solve problems. AI cannot do this. But, as said, if you want to pull up some standard information that is already known, it can do so quickly and if you are not a native speaker of English, it can present that material in grammatical English. But, as said, it will not train anyone to actually do research, nor will it train people to actually have the ability to produce grammatical and stylistically correct language on their own.

I'll repeat Arthur C. Clarke's dictum on teaching machines: "Any teacher who can be replaced by a machine…should be." It's affected my own teaching. I used to lecture. A lot. But then the internet came along, and many people started questioning the value of lecture. Now, don't get me wrong: lecture is important. But if you think about it, the important things are things we want students to have access to all the time. It's why we have textbooks: they can be referenced anytime, anywhere. The same is true for lectures: they are so important that they shouldn't be limited to "one showing only", but instead students should be able to go back to them, over and over again. It's why having lectures online is a good idea. But now you come to what I call the Value Added problem: What value do students get by paying a teacher (directly, as a tutor, or indirectly, through tuition and fees)? It can't be hearing lectures; they can hear better and more professional lectures on YouTube. The value added by the teacher is hands-on-guidance through the material. A teacher who lectures is accomplishing nothing that couldn't be accomplished by having students watch videos, in class or outside. But a teacher who works directly with students is doing something that (so far) no machine can replace.
