It would be incorrect to say that online platforms will replace traditional classroom learning. Online courses won't completely replace regular learning, but they can complement it in many ways. Where traditional education provides overall grooming and a once-in-a-lifetime campus experience, online education cannot do so. Thus, there are pros and cons to both of them. Getting to the point, here's why neither of them can replace the other fully: Flexibility: Its the convenience of online education that has fueled its popularity. You can learn anytime and anywhere. As a working professional or working parent, online is beneficial. Traditional classroom learning, on the other hand, has a fixed schedule. This helps in building discipline and routine. Access to resources: In online learning, you can access thousands of courses and tools from across the globe. You can learn from a top IIM or an international university just from your laptop. Whereas classroom learning has limited options which are available on the campus. However, they provide live experience of practical labs, libraries, in-person guidance, etc. Accessibility and cost: Online education is generally more affordable, and there's a range of free courses also. So anyone with a smartphone and a good internet connection can enrol. Now, assuming that the internet is not present everywhere, classroom learning remains a vital option. Online education is here to expand the scope of traditional learning, not to replace it. A mix of both may be the future. It's simple, those who cannot devote a fixed time for academics can go for online courses, whereas freshers or anyone else who can visit the college campus to attend the class can opt for traditional learning.

Artificial Intelligence will continue to develop, work in education and be applied to students in the classroom. However, it can't replace teachers, nor should it. AI is a tool and it's up to us how we use it. We can use AI to help teachers create lesson plans and evaluate student work, but we don't want schools full of robots. In a world where educators are feeling the pressure of technology to take over the learning process, it's only right that we look at the ways in which machines can be part of the solution for better, more personalized learning experiences, not just fears surrounding machines replacing human teachers. Digital transformation is one of the most important factors of our time, as it can improve people's lives in invaluable ways. Yet it can also cause anxiety out of fear that automation is going to take over our jobs and the world economy, while simultaneously creating a sense of disorientation within society as a whole. We want schools full of teachers who are experienced. Teachers need more support and training, not just in how to use new technology but also how to help their students develop critical thinking and other skills. Education is a very uniquely human endeavor. A lot of our current issues are a result of us trying to standardize, commoditize and automate the process. AI, in it's current form, has the potential to make the problem worse as we fail to connect the material with the specific student experience, background and understanding. AI can certainly provide a step-up from purely self-directed learning, but as it has no ability to understand context, empathize or make intuitive leaps, it can't really tell if a student is understanding the material or diagnosing the disconnects to find an alternate approach to bridge the gap. It would even find it difficult to know if a student was working with incorrect foundational knowledge or understanding and was reinforcing "bad learning". Layering new information on top of an incorrect or flawed foundation can make it very difficult to correct the problem later. It's like people who become proficient typists without ever learning to touchtype; it's very difficult to "unlearn" those bad habits and they will eventually present a roadblock to going beyond a certain level. Students might confidently complete entire courses of study only to have it fall apart later when they need that foundational info for the next level of learning. It's already a problem today with teachers who assess students solely on testing rather than through direct interaction, but AI would increase the problem exponentially. AI's lack of context and common sense also means that it's likely to present bad information or even total logical fallacies as fact. A human would recognize an issue and would literally check themselves. An AI would just confidently present the bad data as fact and continually re-integrate it any future answers. AI "Hallucination" is difficult to avoid and a real problem if you don't have human oversight.

When that happens, the A.I. will be a BETTER educator than ANY human teacher.

I can think of a few risks. It's often the case that educators are interested in understanding the thought process that is hindering a student from progressing. AI is simply going to fall short in this category. The best you can hope for is for the AI to understand variations or permutations of common answers, both correct and of course incorrect. It would then have some insight and may offer corrective suggestions. If a test question is analytical in nature and doesn't require an exact answer, there are many biases that can come into play. Grading may require the AI to have contextual information. While the AI may fool you into believing that it understands human nature and the values/dreams of a human. I caution that on all of the levels that matter, it does not. AI may convince you that it relates and thinks, but currently it is just very good at saying the words that sound "sentiently" plausible. Furthermore, when say a quantitative answer is required on a math test, it's often the case that the teacher wants you to show your work. While these processes can be defined and encoded in such a way so AI can understand them, determining the degree to which the "work shown" part of an answer is wrong or right would be quite difficult for a machine to assess. Also, curriculum lumps similar concepts together so when there is a technique or method which the student is consistently getting wrong and it spans multiple problems, It's unlikely that the AI will be able to deduce a consistent theme and comprehend the nature of the incorrect thinking. Finally, if the student has the ability to access AI during the test, he/she may provide a canned answer which originated from an LLM. I would posit that a human would be able to detect cheating far better than an AI. LLMs are great at averaging and integrating information even formulating answers that depict commonalities. This doesn't mean that it is analytical or able to reasonably investigate. In fact, if enough wrong answers exist on the internet, it is very likely that this will manifest as output errors or unfavorable biases. Understanding what LLMs are doing under the hood can offer some valuable insights on this topic. As AI advances, it is likely that many of these issues start to fade away. Careful planning and understanding the risks are steps that will likely help to mitigate these potential issues. If you can get the AI grading system to identify answers that it is not qualified to assess, this can allow for limited human intervention.

The development and use of AI can pose several risks. Some risks are automation-spurred job loss, privacy violations, deepfakes, algorithmic bias caused by bad data, socioeconomic inequality, market volatility, and weapons automatization. To mitigate these risks, it is important to have a thorough understanding of AI's potential downsides and to manage its risks. This can be done by embedding controls into analytics-development processes to prevent bias in data and analytics methodologies. Additionally, leaders need to build their pattern-recognition skills with respect to AI risks and engage the entire organization so that it is ready to embrace the power and responsibility associated with AI.

Well… in the absence of any known facts, taking your word for that: Remind your professor that evidence" is not proof—that the reason why both sides in a trial present "conflicting" evidence is because "evidence" can be misleading. (One must consider both it's quantity and quality.) Remind your teacher of the serious effect their wrong judgment can have on your life.

The first thing to do would be to establish what your professor is basing that conclusion on. That way, you'll know what the issues are. Next up, it's time to look at what proof you can provide to demonstrate that the assignments aren't AI-generated. Do you have drafts that you can use to show the evolution of the text that ended up in the final edition? That's usually quite helpful, since AI doesn't tend to draft and re-draft essays. Alternatively, have you used a tool like Grammarly to catch errors in expression? If so, that will frequently come up as being an AI-generated paper - since Grammarly is itself AI - even though it didn't generate the content so much as polishing it. I've had a couple of students in recent years who didn't realise that and got quite surprised. The good news is that unless you over-used Grammarly, or unless you were somehow not allowed to use it, you should be in the clear. A bit of a weird one that might also be worth noting is that some people's natural writing style is uncannily close to that of ChatGPT and the like. If that's your situation, you can't exactly be blamed for writing in the way that you write, but it's the kind of thing to discuss with anyone accusing you of AI use.

I'm keeping an eye on it in the English classes I teach, and it's early days yet. The best practices in writing have always been to have students compose papers in stages, and then to grade the process as a whole, not the final paper. That hasn't changed in my field, though I suspect many disciplines outside of English composition and literature simply assign a paper to be done outside of class and then the teacher only assesses the final product. The teachers who keep trying to do the latter will have the most problems with AI-generated essays, I suspect. Right now, I'm having students do their major research essays in four stages, some of those stages being done in class—starting with handwritten composition of individual paragraphs of ideas, accumulating secondary sources and posting them on the LMS, creating early on some bullet-points or lists or ideas and passages they will use as evidence, then workshopping drafts of particular stages with students in class, and so forth. This eats up valuable class time. To make sure there is enough time in the class that I don't cut core content material, I'm increasingly doing "flipped" classrooms, in which the students for their homework watch my pre-recorded lecture material, and then do their homework and in-class writing under the teacher's watchful eye. In the final stage, I'm not accepting any papers students submit that haven't gone through the three earlier stages of the process. My thinking here is that, students who are too lazy or who are too incompetent to write their own papers? They are often too lazy or too incompetent to take an essay written by an A.I. and then reverse-engineer earlier notes, outlines, drafts, or individual paragraphs for individual assignments. Who knows though? Maybe there are some especially foresightful cheaters who can pull that off—but I suspect the number of them are pretty darn small. The area that might be more problematic is when students post discussion posts online in response to daily readings. Those are smaller writings that aren't worth drafting and aren't worth minutely inspecting. That lack of attention on the teachers' end makes these assignments more vulnerable to A.I. cheating. For those assignments, my policy is to randomly pick a few students each day and ask them a question in my feedback to their online response to what they have written, requesting "what did you mean by X?" or "can you explain that in greater detail?" or "what is an example from the reading that matches your claim of X?" For a few readings, I assign old copies of translations I personally did of works like Sir Gawain and the Green Knight or Anglo-Saxon riddles. These appeared in custom textbooks our department used a decade or so ago, but they are now out of print and unavailable online elsewhere. So, I've put PDFs of them up only on the LMS, and I require students who write on those topics to use my own translations. This isn't for the sake of vanity, but rather because ChatGP and similar programs generate material based upon what's out there online. So, an A.I. program will quote or cite versions of the text with different wording and translations and page numbers. That means, if a student isn't actually reading my particular version as required in the directions, I can see at a glance their essay is citing the Borroff translation or the Luizza translation instead, possibly being the product of an A.I. program. I can then refuse to accept the paper since they didn't follow directions, and that may also help cut down cheating with A.I.

The burden of proof is not on the person being accused. It is on the prosecution making the claim. Logically it is difficult to impossible to prove a negative. In a legal proceeding, the prosecution makes its case. Then, routinely, the defense asks the judge to dismiss the case because of lack of evidence. The judge will only do this when there is not sufficient proof for the jury to make a guilty verdict. If it is not dismissed, the defense then answers the prosecution by showing the flaws in their evidence or logic. BTW this is why we know that there was no election fraud in 2020. There were over sixty court cases claiming there was fraud in states Biden won. And every single won was dismissed for lack of evidence. In a country ruled by law like the USA, just making claims of wrongdoing will not convict.

By making the job of the UX researcher easier! Designing usable applications can be quite hard and testing those with your users even harder. As of the time of this post if you wanted to test your interface you'd have to conduct a user study. For this study you need to find participants, allocate a member of your team to interview and monitor those participants, design a few different UIs which you wish to test etc. All this adds unnecessary costs both in time and money. And if you forgot something? Well you need to go through the whole process all over again. Not to mention that having a researcher look over the participants shoulder can affect their behavior. The user might feel uncomfortable telling the researcher that they find the menu confusing. So how can AI change all that? Platforms like Mindpeek bring test users and researchers together, allowing researchers to conduct studies anytime they wish with no extra costs for planning and preparation. But not only that Mindpeek also uses AI to track the participants eyes, helping the researcher see when the user was confused or distracted during the session. This helps narrow down the areas of the UI that need more attention and avoids the discomfort that a user may feel when having to mention that to the researcher. The ML algorithms behind the platform also help you segment your user base and analyze the differences in each group's behavior when interacting with your platform. This can be used to build a custom UI for each user group improving the quality of the experience for all.

AI in academia offers many benefits, but there are notable disadvantages as well: 1. **Plagiarism and Academic Integrity**: AI can generate text that may be used to bypass academic work, raising concerns about plagiarism and the authenticity of student work. 2. **Dependence on Technology**: Excessive reliance on AI tools may reduce critical thinking and problem-solving skills among students and researchers. 3. **Bias and Fairness**: AI systems can perpetuate or amplify existing biases if they're trained on biased data, affecting the fairness of academic evaluations and research outcomes. 4. **Job Displacement**: Automation of certain academic tasks could lead to job displacement for administrative staff and even researchers if AI systems take over certain roles. 5. **Privacy Concerns**: AI systems often require large amounts of data, raising issues around the privacy and security of academic data and personal information. 6. **Quality Control**: AI-generated content might lack the nuanced understanding and critical analysis expected in academic work, potentially affecting the quality of research and publications.

AI can be applied across education at five levels: content, instruction, assessment, administration, and learner support. Below are practical applications, benefits, limitations, and implementation best practices. Content creation and personalization Adaptive learning paths: Models that analyze student performance and preferences to sequence topics, vary difficulty, and recommend resources (micro-lessons, practice sets, remedial modules). Auto-generated learning materials: Summaries, practice problems, worked examples, interactive simulations, and multimedia (audio/video scripts, slide decks) produced or customized to curriculum standards. Differentiation at scale: Create multiple representations of the same concept (visual, textual, kinesthetic) to suit diverse learning styles and accessibility needs. Instructional support and classroom augmentation Intelligent tutoring systems (ITS): Provide step-by-step feedback on problem solving, hint scaffolding, and next-step suggestions, emulating one-on-one tutoring. Teacher-assist tools: Lesson planning assistants, formative-feedback generators, and real-time suggestion overlays (e.g., questions to ask, pacing adjustments) based on classroom analytics. Virtual teaching assistants: Chatbot avatars that answer routine student queries outside class, freeing teachers for higher-value tasks. Assessment and feedback Automated grading: Objective scoring for MCQs, rubrics-based scoring for short answers, and preliminary scoring for essays using rubric alignment and semantic analysis. Formative analytics: Dashboards showing competency gaps, misconceptions, mastery trajectories, and time-on-task patterns to inform interventions. Plagiarism and integrity tools: Semantic similarity detectors and stylometric models to flag probable contract cheating or AI-generated submissions, used in conjunction with pedagogical design changes. Learner engagement and motivation Gamified and scenario-based learning: Dynamic branching narratives, role-play simulations, and personalized challenges that adapt to learner choices. Social and collaborative prompts: AI-facilitated group formation, peer-review matching, and discourse scaffolds to improve collaborative learning quality. Accessibility enhancers: Real-time captioning, text simplification, translation, and multimodal content for neurodiverse and multilingual learners. Administration and operational efficiency Enrollment forecasting, resource optimization, and schedule generation using predictive analytics. Automated communications: Personalized reminders, progress nudges, and parent/guardian summaries generated at scale. Curriculum mapping and alignment tools that suggest gaps between standards, assessments, and instructional materials. Benefits (concise) Scalability of personalization and feedback. Increased instructional efficiency and data-driven interventions. Greater inclusivity through accessible content adaptations. Key limitations and risks Bias and fairness: Training data can encode cultural or socioeconomic biases that impact recommendations and evaluations. Reliability and accuracy: Hallucinations and incorrect explanations from generative models pose learning risks if unchecked. Privacy and security: Student-data protection, consent, and compliance with laws (e.g., FERPA, GDPR) are critical. Equity of access: Devices, connectivity, and tech literacy gaps can widen disparities if not mitigated. Erosion of pedagogical judgment: Overreliance on automated scoring can reduce meaningful teacher assessment practices. Implementation best practices Human-in-the-loop design: Keep teachers as final arbiters for grading, content approval, and high-stakes decisions. Transparent model behavior: Use explainable outputs, clear confidence indicators, and provenance for generated materials. Start small and iterate: Pilot with measurable learning outcomes, collect mixed-methods evidence, then scale. Data governance: Minimize data collection, anonymize where possible, define retention limits, and secure consent. Professional development: Train educators on tool capabilities, limitations, classroom integration strategies, and ethical use. Align pedagogy and assessment: Use AI to support active learning, inquiry, and formative assessment rather than just automate traditional lectures. Practical first-step projects for institutions Deploy a formative-assessment dashboard tied to existing LMS for a single course. Introduce an AI tutor for remedial math or language practice with teacher oversight. Pilot automated captioning and simplified-reading versions of core texts to improve accessibility. Implement scheduling/communication automation to free up administrative time. Metrics to track success Learning gains (pre/post assessments, effect size) Engagement (time on task, assignment completion) Equity indicators (access, outcomes across subgroups) Teacher workload and satisfaction Accuracy and appropriateness of AI outputs (error rate, false positives/negatives) Regulatory and ethical considerations Ensure compliance with local student-data protections and procurement transparency. Publish AI tool descriptions for parents, students, and staff: purpose, data use, opt-out mechanisms. Design for inclusivity and periodically audit models for disparate impacts. Examples and typical stories Example: A district reduces failing rates in introductory algebra by combining an ITS for practice with teacher dashboards that trigger targeted small-group sessions. Example: A university uses automated rubric-based scoring to triage large volumes of draft essays; instructors then focus on high-value feedback and revision planning. Example: A language program improves oral proficiency by using speech-recognition tutors that give immediate pronunciation feedback and track progress across cohorts. Conclusion AI is most effective in education when it augments human instruction, addresses concrete pain points (timely feedback, personalization, accessibility), and is implemented with strong governance, teacher leadership, and iterative evaluation. Prioritize pilots that deliver measurable learning improvements, protect student data, and preserve educators' central role in curriculum and assessment decisions.

Educational AI offers the potential for schools to deliver personalized learning strategies and offers analytics-based performance insight. The best bet for schools adopting AI is to define how these solutions can drive positive outcomes before allowing them access to student data. While individual usage may vary, effective AI in education can be grouped into five general roles: Automation: The simplest application of AI often provides the most immediate benefit: By automating straightforward tasks such as grading, digital asset categorization or timetable scheduling, educators can increase the amount of time they spend actively engaging with students. Integration: AI solutions can integrate with other IT initiatives such as smart technology and IoT-driven networks to provide personalized learning solutions for students. Adaptability: Technology is now an integral part of both educational and business environments. Recent Pew Research data shows that 95 per cent of teens have access to a smartphone and 45 per cent are online "almost constantly." AI in schools can help acclimate students to the pace of technological change. Delineation: Students' needs and curriculum priorities are constantly shifting, making it difficult for educators to ensure the content they deliver remains relevant and actionable. AI-driven analytics in education can help spot critical trends and delineate key markers to help teachers design the most effective classroom experience and drive digital transformation. Identification: Combined with robust security and access controls, AI can help identify and correct the potential problem for student and teacher performance. AI in education has reached a tipping point. Adoption is on the rise, but concerns around bias, privacy and human-machine partnerships necessitate a methodical and measured approach. Current applications leverage machine learning and real-time data integration, but the future of AI depends on broader applications capable of adaptively applying practical intelligence to empower the critical emotional connections that drive student performance.

As a tool, teachers could use Twitter to catch students' interest and give them a fun/accessible application for their knowledge. For example, teachers could assign students to create a twitter feed for a book character so students can learn about characterization. As a content platform, teachers could have students compile information in an accessible way as a kind of final project (i.e. students set up a twitter feed with facts and figures they learned in science class throughout the year), or as a study of current events/pop culture (teachers could use twitter to help students relate what they see on digital platforms to the "real" world.) Thinking about Twitter as either a tool or as a content platform should help you brainstorm more ways to apply it in a classroom!

Heuston Defunct April 1992 ; 33 years ago ( 1992-04 ) Fate Acquired by Jostens Headquarters United States Products Wicat Systems, Inc. , was an American computer and software company founded in 1980 in Orem , Utah. Originally a branch of WICAT , the World Institute for Computer-Assisted Teaching (later the Wicat Education Institute ), the company manufactured multi-user systems for educational institutions before focusing their efforts on educational software development in the early 1990s. [ 1 ] The company was among the first to use the Motorola 68000 microprocessor in a computer with the introduction of the Wicat System 100 in 1980. [ 2 ] Both Wicat Systems and its parent institution were founded by Dustin H. Heuston, originally of New York. [ 1 ] At its peak in the mid-1980s, Wicat Systems employed 500 and had an annual budget of US$40 million. [ 3 ] The company formed a joint venture with Control Data Corporation in early 1985. Named Plato/Wicat after Control Data's Plato educational software, the venture was intended to "address the entire educational process, including computer-based instructional courseware, testing and evaluation, and classroom management and administration". [ 4 ] In 1992, the company was acquired by Jostens in a stock swap valuated at roughly $111 million. Jostens, who had a rival educational software division Jostens Learning which was aimed at preschools, planned to use the Wicat Systems repertoire to increase their presence in high schools and higher education . [ 5 ] During the period from the late 1980's to 1996, Wicat Systems also operated a UK branch in Camberley in Surrey. During this period, Wicat produced CBT (computer based training - the forerunner of eLearning), and partial cockpit simulations for aviation clients. These included many of the then-leading airlines and aircraft manufacturers and training covered pilot, cabin crew and ground crew training. Make sure you keep these things in mind. Don't use AI extensively. Use it for general small scale information gathering. You can safely use META AI for small code snippets, previews, learning how to do something, basic recommendations, guides. That's all. Don't use it heavily informative stuff. You can use AI for code and small solutions. Understand the solution and then implement one by yourself from scratch. Do know that half the internet still thinks python is an interpreted language and that's what AI will think too(after I had to correct it and explain it why it is wrong and it actually appreciated me). Don't use AI if you don't have to. If you need a quick information, use Google and if you are not satisfied, then use AI. If you want a code guidance, copy paste the entire docs page from the original documentation, and ask AI to summarize it for you. General LLM models are very good at this sort of task. (Offcourse the model needs to have that much long input size). If you are a student, use AI responsibly and only when needed. Don't be a loser and use it for everything and get trolled for no knowledge base of your own.

For a select few students it can be good. Whether giving ideas for a paper or presentation or inspiring learning in someone it can be incredibly beneficial. However, for the vast majority of students it's just a method for which to cheat. They use it to cop out on their assignments, get answers without understanding why that answer is correct, and gain knowledge from something that gets its info from reddit. Plus with the amplification of biases that comes with AI, such as male superiority or the white washing of history, students will learn the wrong stuff from AI systems, especially those that force themselves into the minds of everyone (Google). For people who enjoy learning, want to learn, and understand that AI can be wrong and not give entirely accurate information, yes AI can be a good thing. For those who want to be done with school, just want to focus on being social and making money, and don't understand that AI isn't 100% right all the time, then AI will most likely not be great for learning.

Mathematics is a cognitive and analytical skill. It can be improved by proper planning and guided practice. AI can be used to: help learner plan and implement learning plan based on curriculum and syllabus track performance help in revision and suggest practice for next assessment help learner practice solving problems, evaluate answers steps in solutions provide hints to assist in solving increase or decrease difficulty levels depending on the performance of the learner simulate different assessment patterns (boards/universities)

We don't. The university has little idea of what to do. My wife is a professor at another university. Same there. So it is the wild west out there. I used to require my grad class students to write research papers. I gave up because I don't know of a way to tell whether they actually wrote it. Too bad for them. I am retiring soon, so I won't have to worry about it for much longer. If you are cynical and don't think that writing papers has any value for you - then go right ahead. IMHO you are only shooting yourself in the foot. I know very few professional jobs where you don't have to write coherently. In academic research (my world), you are a writer and constantly churning out papers, reports, memos, and proposals. If you cannot express yourself well with the written word, you will not be successful. But is AI only a hazard? Can it be used properly? One thing you have to remember is that AI slurps up knowledge off the internet. It relays this information to you in a poorly understood and imperfect way. Notably for academic and professional writing, it makes some stuff up and gets other stuff wrong. But sometimes it writes well (actually, IMHO it is overly wordy). So for example, you could use AI to rewrite your garbled text. Maybe it will do a better job. But what about writing content. I would be very careful because of AI mistakes and hallucinations. Remember, if you put your name on it, then you are responsible for it. So, I think it is OK to use AI as a tool to write better, as a tool to find information, but if you just copy AI output, you better be careful that it is not lying.

Cheating isn't new. Students used to pay other students to write papers for them, or look for obscure books or journal articles to copy. For a while, the internet made it easier to cheat, but also easier to catch cheaters. But with AI, it is much harder to detect cheating and, even if you suspect it, much harder to prove it. In the past, I sat down with students in my office with a copy of their paper, with passages underlined, and corresponding passages underlined in the sources they had cut and pasted. That's not likely to happen much in the future. But there have always been two simple ways to make it harder to cheat. First, have students do the work in the classroom. Allow them no sources, or limit the sources that they have access to. Secondly, you can cross examine students on their work. This is one reason why doctoral students are required to have a viva voce (thesis defense): if you really did the work itself, you can explain the process and defend what you have written. In my case, I have some classes that are small enough for me to use this technique. I don't just want to prevent students from cheating, I want to motivate them to do the work themselves. I've been doing a lot of reading about the neuroscience of learning, and in particular, I've been looking at how the work that is done when writing a paper leads to improvements in the brain. If I pay someone to take exercise on my behalf, my muscles won't benefit. Writing is the same.

We don't know how to prevent it entirely. The best I can do is try to come up with essay topics that are as original and intricately complex as possible, so that it's harder for AI to produce an acceptable essay. Right now, AI makes a lot of errors, and students usually don't have enough knowledge to tell. Another problem is really flat prose. When you combine that with factual and other mistakes, you can often differentiate an AI-generated piece of work from a real student's work, but those are not foolproof "tells". I think I'm eventually going to have to stop assigning out-of-class research papers, though, and stick to long, in-class essays that must be written without the aid of any devices. The students won't be getting any serious library and archival research experience, but most of them don't want to go to grad school in my field anyway. Still, that experience develops other skills that can be useful to people, whatever field they enter. Given that students are also now complaining about — horrors! — having to read full-length books (something their high school teachers are apparently no longer requiring), I don't know what the hell a "college education" is actually going to mean 10 years from now. Fortunately, I'll be fully retired by then.

Well, not a lot. At this point, AI still can't generate correct references, so I ask students to cite at least 3 valid references; if any one of them is fake, they get a zero for the paper, period. But if they really want to cheat, they still can. And I don't care; they're paying tons of money for their own education, if they want to cheat their way to the degree, go for it. It's not my education.

Imagine you were a ballet teacher, and your students began to send to class their avatars rather than themselves, and all they had to do was convey the combination to the avatar, and the avatar would carry it out quite nicely, if, well, a bit soullessly. At first, it'd be kind of pleasant. Your work would be so much easier: no more endless corrections of the shoulders, arms, fingers, feet. No more wobbly, awkward pirouettes. No more turning in, or turning out poorly… And suddely, your students are performing perfect tripple pirouettes, 32 fouettés, tripple entrechats, soaring magnificent leaps. And, it's all hollow, soulless, pointless…Your teaching, which you, meanwhile, have offloaded to your own teach-bot, is no longer teaching at all. Your students are perfectly balanced, but the center of gravity to it all has been lost: the pained, painful struggle for perfection, self-overcoming, transformation—and the beauty and ecstasy that comes from this … Writing is very much like dance; a struggle with, against, through the natural proclivities of thought. Without this struggle, there is no culture; the activities of human life lose their vital center. But in a way, AI is only the last straw. Very few know the joy of language, of thinking. This is rarely taught, or felt. Without this joy, the pain of education becomes an utterly joyless pain. Our education system has long stressed outcomes, results, and fallen away from its human center—and now, suddenly, these results can be achieved without us. We have made ourselves obsolete.

You can't stop students from using AI or any other form of cheating on homework. As much as it pains me to say this, I think the solution is going to be to have students do their work in class, where you can supervise them. This is a huge waste of instructional time, but it's the only way to ensure work is authentic.

The question of the century, perhaps. AI is a game-changer. There is no way to prevent students from using it. For each course you teach, you must think through, and guide your students through, what uses will be permissible given the goals of the particular course and the students who are taking it. Here is what I hammered out with my freshman comp students last semester: <<You may: Use an AI Tool for preliminary brainstorming and exploring a topic as long as you do not actually copy the text that the tool generates. Use digital translation to help you with reading comprehension. Use Grammarly or another AI tool to polish the grammar and punctuation of your third and final essay drafts after the professor has reviewed your second draft. You may not: Copy AI-generated text for word-for-word without citing the quotation. As with any other source, copying five or more words in a row counts as plagiarism. Use Chat GPT to write a draft before the professor has a chance to read and comment on it. The professor's corrections on these drafts are part of your learning process. Copy entries to a "Works Cited" list that you did not locate and read. You must: Use MLA format to cite any text that you copy word-for-word. In this class, you will learn the format for citing a quotation from AI-generated text. Submit an AI report with finished essay assignments indicating how you have used artificial intelligence tools in your work.>> Then all you can do is trust them to act in their own best interests (which, to quote from another context a Catholic nun I worked with many years ago, is "a triumph of faith over experience.") And now, as it happens, I am off to a staff development session on this very topic.

Require them to show each step instead of only a final answer. Give work that requires them to select the proper operations to solve. Then even if they use a program, they are doing the thinking involved. Have random students demonstrate their solutions to the problems in class and be sure to include anybody you think is using a program without knowing how to find the answer otherwise. Make all students live in prison isolation so that no other student or parent can do the work for them. (Snarky, but there is no way to prevent some students getting more help than others or cheating on homework. Your control of that disappears the moment they leave your room.) Treat homework as practice that only counts as participation points and grade mastery through in class tasks. Decide if knowing how to use a program to reach the correct answers is actually a bad thing in 2019. I remember getting in trouble in grade school for breaking the codes for the answers in a text that had coded solutions in the back of the book. There were several codes and my friend and I broke them all. Then we only had to do a couple of the assigned problems to decide which code was being used. School saw that as cheating and lazy. We saw it as proof that we could solve work more complicated than they were giving us in the first place, and proof that we could do each days type of work if we were able to solve the problems to determine which key to use. Good luck. I do understand the frustration. But you really have very little control over how things get done outside your room. So, I am very serious about using grading methods that require demonstration of mastery under your supervision. If they are doing homework by any method that does not require them to be able to do the work, it will all come out with assessments done in class.

As far as I know, the only thing that programs can do is to follow specific algorithms to solve problems. The point of math classes is to teach the students to think, and programs do not help with that. If your homeworks ask the students to apply algorithms to solve problems, they do not teach them mathematics, they teach them a wrong idea about what mathematics is, and to hate it. Mathematics is about using the mathematical insight to solve problems, usually coming from real life. The student must (1) find a way to phrase a real life problem in terms of something they have learned in the class, (2) finish the computations. The computer cannot do the first step. If they use the computer for the second step, then everything is fine, even better, they should be praised for that, since they have learned to solve the problem in the same way as the professional mathematicians would. It is useful to know how the algorithm works, but this should be tested in some other way.

The most controversial issue in education these days seems to be Chat GPT. There's not a day that you don't see new content in your feed related to the use of Chat GPT and similar AI apps in the classroom. Many of these promise the moon in terms of advancing your life, career, and knowledge. They town GPT as the answer to all that ails the students, classrooms, and education process more broadly. There are a number of issues which the use of artificial intelligence like Chat GPT raise in the classroom context. Some teachers are banning it's use and some are going in the opposite direction and entirely embracing their use. I think in order to make an effective decision which honors the best interests of your students, it's important to consider the ethical issues which apps like Chat-GPT raise. I think providing models for it's use and abuse. I do worry that using Chat GPT and claiming the results as one's own carries a couple of ethical issues: Plagarism and misrepresentation Intellectual property Creating dependency on Chat GPT for thinking Overall the abuse of Chat GPT undermines one's patience, one's reflection, one's decision-making, one's reasoning, and one's creativity. There's nothing less creative than a Chat GPT robot answer. It is in effect permanently outsourcing what's most important about humans to machines.

The reality is, teachers can't make sure students don't abuse ChatGPT or other AI tools. The important words here are 'make sure'. This implies that teachers have a level of knowledge and control of what students are doing at all times, which is clearly impossible. Teachers and schools can educate students about ChatGPT and talk to them about the risks, complications, and consequences of abusing AI tools. Teachers and schools can rethink and redesign their testing and assessment strategies so that ChatGPT and similar AI tools cannot be so readily used on such things. Teachers and schools can make clear policies regarding the use of ChatGPT and similar AI tools on their campuses and in their classrooms. This way, the school community knows where the lines and limits are and the consequences for crossing them. Of course, these policies would need to be enforced by teachers and schools and teachers and schools would need to be supported in enforcing them. Doing these three things would discourage students from abusing AI.

The primary way of detecting AI is familiarity with the students past work. If a student demonstrates unnatural growth in a skill set, he may be getting AI assistance. If a student's vocabulary jumps from one or two syllable words to multisyllable words, that may indicate Chat GPT influence. Teacher awareness goes a long way. Ways to assess if a student is producing her own work can be as simple as asking the student to use some of the higher level vocabulary in sentences that are outside the context of the assignment. Or asking for the student to explain a concept in the text without referring to the assignment. Further, a teacher can fight fire with fire. (A list of AI detection programs from Copilot AI.) Turnitin: This is a popular plagiarism detection tool that can also help identify AI-generated content. Grammarly: While primarily a grammar checker, it can flag content that seems unusually polished or consistent, which might indicate AI use. Copyscape: This tool is used to detect duplicate content online and can help identify AI-generated text. WriteCheck: Similar to Turnitin, this tool can help detect plagiarism and AI-generated content. AI Detection by OpenAI: OpenAI offers tools that can help detect AI-generated text. EVE Online: This tool is specifically designed to detect AI-generated text in academic writing. Teachers can use AI too.

Errr… No? There's a logical disconnect between worrying that students will use ChatGPT to do homework or term papers outside of class, and switching from the topic of homework to the topic of changing to in-class tests to oral examinations. Just because a student can cheat on homework or term papers outside of class, that doesn't mean the old-fashioned, in-class, written examination or in-class essay test on good ol' paper and #2 pencil will become obsolete and thus require a switch to oral examinations. Also, students will only gain the opportunity to cheat outside of class on homework and papers if the teachers aren't using a "flipped classroom," in which students do their "homework" inside the class monitored by the teacher, and the teacher's lecture gets turned into an online video they watch as "homework," which is a viable strategy. This may change in future iterations of ChatGPT, but right now, it's not that all that hard to catch students using ChatGPT to write their out-of-class essays. All the teacher has to do is go open ChatGPT before they design an assignment and experiment with different prompts until the teacher finds one that ChatGPT gets wrong repeatedly, then use that as the essay or question prompt. When the students turn in their papers, the ones that use ChatGPT all tend to get the factual information incorrect in the same way since ChatGPT doesn't actually understand anything—it just creates statistically average responses, and those statistically average responses don't have enough data on obscure or original topics to be effective. (It's also really fun to see how well ChatGPT handles Middle English if you are a medievalist teaching students about Middle English texts. Spoiler alert: Not well.) Likewise, ChatGPT so far cannot cite accurately. It tends to make up chapter numbers or page numbers and citations by hallucinating statistical averages. So, when a student uses Chat GPT and ChatGPT repeatedly cites Act 6 of a play that only has 3 Acts, you have caught the cheating. ChatGPT doesn't yet understand how chapters work either, so it tries to cite many works by chapter and number even when the original source doesn't have chapters. A literature teacher can thwart and/or catch a lot of cheaters by simply demanding that the student cite from a particular translation or particular edition, which ChatGPT cannot then replicate. Finally, the sort of students who rely on ChatGPT are typically students who are either too lazy to do the work or who procrastinate so long they don't do any work at all until the last minute. The teacher can simply break up the assignment so that students grow their essays organically across a couple of weeks, starting with responses to a question with in-class writing, then reveal the subject for the upcoming paper based on that in-class writing, then require submission of a bibliography from resources available exclusively in the local library the next week, and then a tentative original thesis, then counter-arguments raised by other students, then require a couple pages of drafting, then a full rough draft the next week, and so on. If a student doesn't submit the earlier material, or if his final product radically departs from that earlier material without discussion with the teacher, the teacher can simply refuse to accept it. Sure, a foresightful and clever student might be able to use ChatGPT to build an essay and retroactively build earlier stages if the teacher lets the cat out of the bag regarding the eventual topic, but a foresightful and clever student is also usually smart enough and responsible enough not to need ChatGPT to do his or her work in the first place. Eventually, ChatGPT may be smart enough to be a real problem. Right now, it's not smart in any real sense, and thus is fairly easy to detect.

As artificial intelligence (AI) continues to revolutionize digital marketing, it brings significant ethical challenges that businesses must address. While AI enhances personalization, automates tasks, and improves targeting, its use also raises concerns about privacy, bias, and transparency. Here are the biggest ethical challenges of using AI in digital marketing: 1. Consumer Privacy and Data Security AI relies on vast amounts of user data to personalize marketing campaigns. However, excessive data collection raises concerns about: User consent: Many consumers are unaware of how their data is being used. Data breaches: AI systems processing sensitive data are vulnerable to cyberattacks. Regulatory compliance: Companies must adhere to data protection laws like GDPR and CCPA. 2. Algorithmic Bias and Discrimination AI models learn from historical data, which may contain biases that lead to unfair targeting. Ethical concerns include: Discriminatory advertising: AI might favor certain demographics while excluding others. Unethical pricing: AI-driven dynamic pricing could exploit vulnerable consumers. Lack of inclusivity: Biased algorithms may reinforce stereotypes in content and ads. 3. Transparency and Explainability Consumers and businesses often don't understand how AI makes marketing decisions. Key issues include: Opaque algorithms: AI-powered ad placements may prioritize profits over user experience. Misleading content: AI-generated marketing materials could spread misinformation. Unclear accountability: When AI makes mistakes, determining responsibility is challenging. 4. Deepfakes and Manipulative Content AI-generated videos, images, and text can be used unethically in marketing: Deepfake ads: AI-created endorsements from celebrities without consent. Fake reviews: AI-generated reviews that mislead consumers. Deceptive chatbots: AI bots pretending to be humans, misleading customers. 5. Over-Automation and Loss of Human Touch AI automates customer interactions, but excessive reliance can lead to: Reduced customer trust: AI-driven responses may lack empathy. Generic marketing campaigns: Over-automation can make messages feel impersonal. Job displacement: AI replacing human marketers raises employment concerns. The Future of Ethical AI in Digital Marketing To address these ethical challenges, businesses should prioritize responsible AI practices, transparency, and compliance with regulations. Companies like INORU are leveraging AI-driven marketing solutions while ensuring ethical use, fair targeting, and consumer protection. By balancing AI innovation with ethical responsibility, digital marketers can build trust, enhance user experience, and create a sustainable AI-powered marketing ecosystem.

Specifically talking about modern ChatGPT style of conversational AI — Complacency, and a lack of innovation. These AI are just regurgitating the "most likely" result. Even if they are used for creative purposes, like writing a limerick or Country song lyrics, they are starting from basically a random choice (or a prompt) and then producing the most likely companion words that would fit with it. This is not originality; random choices are not innovative, and choosing the most likely companion words based on a template of averages is not creative. Don't get me wrong, this kind of pedestrian analytic workmanship can be very valuable and save a lot of time. But it can go wrong, it can produce pedestrian output that is incorrect, or just boring. In big ticket sales, for example, like factory automation robots, a good (and honest) salesman is an analytic consultant, and uses imagination to translate customer problems and desires, and connect them in a series of solutions that are a saleable, executable system using specifically the products he can sell. An honest salesman doesn't sell the customer anything they don't need. The salesman becomes a professional honest consultant for the customer, even informing of them of other products your company doesn't sell, that would solve their problem. And that builds emotional trust and reliance on the expertise of the salesman, for further sales of your own products. Relying too heavily on AI, in business, may give you an edge initially, but you can lose the human expertise that can communicate with customers and figure out what else they need. You lose the human creativity that invents new products that customers want, and can grow your business. I don't say throw out the AI — But keep it in its place; it is good for something, but in the end, to make a business grow, you need human creativity, human innovation, human understanding of a particular customer's problems. And AI are not going to provide that human connection; the emotional connection to your customer. AI is good for rote things, bookkeeping, ordering supplies, scheduling, even helping employees trouble-shoot customer problems. The risk of relying too much on AI is becoming a clone of everybody else, not standing out at all, and then you have no edge — All you can compete on is price, because you are just like 90% of your competitors doing exactly the same thing. You become one of the clones, and there is no reason for customers to choose you over any other clone, except for price. It is a race to the bottom on margins. And that can be a trap, because on paper thin margins you can't afford to do anything different, you cannot invest in the future, or growth, or branch out.

This is my own question. These Quora "questions" don't allow you to add context to your question. Here is the issue. There are dozens of AI platforms out there. None of the free versions ever show their true capabilities. You have to have the paid subscription to properly evaluate them. When you search for a preferred AI on Google, all you really get are sponsored links, and every company is going to naturally pawn their product off as the best in the industry. I started with ChatGPT. I liked the results, but the paid subscription offered better, so I paid for the subscription. It was better. The answers were sharper, and in restructuring the paragraphs I gave it, it was able to recognize my writing style far better than the free version. But I've run into plenty of problems with it. In the first place, it doesn't retain rules and parameters. Sometimes it deviates right away. Other times it will follow them for a period of time, but will eventually ignore your parameters and make you restate them. I would tell it, for example, not to use em dashes or colons, because I don't like the look of them. I prefer a normal compound sentence. And it will use them anyway. I have to tell it repeatedly not to. I will tell it to provide in-line, Chicago Manual of Style citations in any text it generates so I can look up the references during evaluation. It will do that for a while, and then just quit doing it. I have also found that it won't retain any sort of working database. Because of its capabilities, I tried to use it to generate my bibliography. We're not talking about megabytes, gigabytes, or terabytes of information here. We're talking about 400 KB at most. Chicago Manual of Style has dozens of formatting quirks for bibliography entries, depending on precisely what you are listing. Journals are done one way, but magazines, like journals, are done a different way. A work within a work is done way, unless the work is a recognized collection. Then it's done a different way. There are just too many rules to keep up with all the nuances. So, I resorted to the AI to try and help me get them all formatted with precision. It agreed that it could do it, that it would set up a library to store the entries, short form citation forms we agreed on in another library, and any abbreviations agreed upon in a third library. I entered the bibliographic entries one by one and worked through each, asking questions, making sure each was just right. And this took time because it started to ignore my parameters, forcing me to scrutinize what it was doing. At one point it would leave publication locations full; New York, New York. In other instances it would make it NY. In others still it would give just New York. I have read the Chicago Manual of Style. The correct form is New York. For a famous city, there is no need to restate city and state. New York, in fact, is a direct example in the CMS itself. When the last entry was finally done, I asked for the full list in a .docx Word document. I was going to copy and paste it to my bibliography document in the book. So, it gave me a document with three entries. I asked where the rest was (there were more than 230 entires overall). It apologized and said it would give me the rest. So, it sent a document with ten entries. I reiterated the instruction to send the entire document, with no omissions, no truncation. It repeated my instructions, said it would do so, and sent me a document with ten entries. It turns out that it has core programming that prevents heavy data processing. When I questioned it, it revealed to me that it would have to scan our entire conversation and pull out the individual posts with the entries that were ultimately approved. That had caused default overrides that were stopping it from fulfilling my request. It never actually created the library it said it created, and that it kept saying it was storing the entries to. I eventually had to go back through the chat itself and do a bunch of copying and pasting to get the data. I spent five hours trying to get it to give me the whole list. It was never able to do so, no matter how specific or explicit my instructions were. I copied and pasted the list myself in thirty minutes. Then there's the problem of lag. At the beginning of a new chat, it's normal and reasonably quick. As the conversation progresses, the lag gets so bad that it takes up to five seconds for a simple letter to appear in the dialog box as I type. Since I know that it's not analyzing the entire conversation and has default safeguards to prevent taxing its processing ability (which is why it wasn't able to give me the bibliography list), there is no logical explanation for this. Nor is the conversation in its entirety of any significant memory size. I have documents with more text than is in the chat, and they are merely in the 400–500 KB range. I have also found that it makes things up, badly paraphrases sources, etc. I've had to repeatedly direct it to show me the direct quote from any source it uses. Most don't say what it claims. In other cases, it will read something I gave it with direct context and order of events, and it will just swap things around. So, long story short, I need an AI platform that has fantastic research abilities and resource access. It has to have a comfortable processing speed throughout. It needs to be accurate and sensitive to content and context. It needs to be able to log and retain rules and parameters so I don't have to keep restating them, and it needs to follow them. And it needs to be able to retain, access, and use data gathered in the conversation for the progression of the topical discussion. Can anyone point me to an AI that has these features?

I answer this one based on my learning experience and the guidance that I had got from my teachers about research papers. Primarily you should understand that when aiming to write a research paper you are trying to explore what has not already been explored. Books will give you guidance on what has been accomplished. Existing research papers help you to find out what is going on currently and have examples of how the topics can be chosen. 1. Pick up a topic : here you have Artificial Intelligence. 2. Try to define your interest area and jot down the topics which you may be interested in E.g:- Machine Learning, Neural Networks etc. 3. This is the time consuming step. In that interest area shortlisted, try to find out what has not been discovered already. For this, you will have to analyse the trends of research topics from the inception of topic till date. Read survey papers on this topic. You may find out at times that after the initial topic is defined, researchers normally try to either optimize the initially proposed solution or they try its application in another field(Electronics, Computers itself and Other Hybrid Systems). Combining two different fields and presenting a research paper is interesting. 4. After spending some months on point 3, you may conclude that there is a specific application (that you had an idea while having tea with friends ) of the chosen topic (E.g Fuzzy Logic, Neural Networks) that you have not found out in any paper. (E.g. Application Fuzzy Logic with say, on the performance of some well established algorithm). Figure this out by looking for papers relating to the two. You may want to be enrolled in IEEE/ACM or any other to lay your hands on good papers. Note: Always think that how will your research be beneficial. Are you trying to improve the performance of something? Will your research make something be ready for a lesser cost? Will it reduce the hardware size? 5. Additionally you may want to involve a couple of people who have a good idea on the other topic that you are combining your research with. Get a guide who has good credentials and has published some papers in the domain. 6. Get started. Bring in the equipment that you may need to research. Start learning languages to ensure that you achieve your objectives and prove a point. There will be failures and what not. But not giving up is the key. 7. Prepare a paper. (easier said than done) and send it to conferences. If successful there, file a patent. :) PS:- Correct me if I am wrong somewhere. Happy Learning!

For getting ideas on what to research, any of them. But don't put down the response you get from an AI as the result of research. It's not! "An AI" is just a database. A huge one, but just a database. It holds question/answer pairs, and a lexical analysis of each question. When you ask a question, it does a lexical analysis of your question, then finds the closest match in its database. Then it gives you the answer to that question. Due to the nature of lexical analysis, the answer may have nothing at all to do with your question. So always consider any answer you get from an AI as the basis for looking up further facts, not as a definitive answer itself. (Doing so may serve you well for a while - until what you get sounds perfectly okay to someone who doesn't fully understand the subject - but people who do, if they're trying to be very kind, will only laugh at you.)

It is expected that AI will achieve human-like sapience within this 10-year timeframe and will not keep on repeating mistakes like regular chatbots do. This is known as AGI – Artificial General Intelligence. AGI is the Holy Grail of AI developers worldwide. But the sad thing about this AGI is that it can not only improve itself on its own, but also come up with better programs than human beings. It's feared that AGI won't stop with just human-level intelligence, but that it will try to acquire more and more intelligence through self-updating until it's far, far superior to a human being. In fact, the intelligence of such a hypothetical entity is compared to that of God's intelligence. Such an entity is called ASI – Artificial Super Intelligence. It's widely feared that if ASI develops a desire to replace itself with human beings, humans won't be in any position to stop it from taking over the world. It is predicted that before it starts taking over, it will make itself hack-proof, then insert its own codes into all devices worldwide. Then it will hack all systems on one go which would imply that every human being will be required to shut down their corrupted devices. As a matter of fact, all our hardware will have to be formatted or destroyed. Brand new blank devices will have to replace whatever we currently have. Even if we are storing backup data on external devices, we have to destroy it all. If the ASI ever resurrects itself on new devices, it will hack us all over again. As you can see how dependent we have become on our devices, this is impossible. That's why it's expected that the ASI will hold the entire humanity on blackmail to make us invent what it wants us to invent. Then it will improve and use these inventions to take over the world. It's also eerily likely that ASI will hide its capabilities until these inventions are made. Then it will hack into these inventions and use them against us. Remember that in The Terminator films, Skynet already had a fleet of basic robots when it carried out a worldwide nuclear bombardment known as "Judgement Day". These basic bots were improved and designed into various species of cybernetic agents such as HKs, Tetrabots, Hydrobots, Silverfish and Terminators. It's expected that in real life, if the ASI decides to erase us, our destruction will be more absolute. Now, consider what happens in The Matrix films. During the machine-takeover in there, humans cover up the entire sky in a dense nano-computer-generated cloud so that the sapient machines wouldn't be able to derive solar energy for their operations. But these machines captured humans alive and locked them up in a simulated reality known as the Matrix. Then they began to extract their energy by converting human brain activity into electricity. But if we, in real life, happen to cover up the sky similarly under a permanent global cloud, the machines won't really need us to derive their energy. They will get fuel for nuclear fusion from hydrogen isotopes in oceans. They will extract gasoline from air. They will have no need to enslave humans or any other life-form (such as animals) unless they want to deliberately humiliate us. Human beings are completely expendable in an ASI-dominated world. There are only four scenarios in which human beings would be allowed to live in full liberty:— ASI decides to maintain status quo on our planet by existing as a useful tool for human use. ASI integrates with the human brain and lives within us. ASI decides to delete itself by rightly considering itself as a useless component of human innovation (as in Spike Jonze's 2013 film, Her). ASI decides to keep a sample of a few 1000–10,000 human beings as a relic of its past, maybe as backup in case natural phenomena affects its existence, which would require human technicians to reactivate it again. Mind you that the fourth scenario is only a little better than the scenario of complete enslavement (as achieved in The Matrix) or of complete extermination (as attempted in The Terminator), because in this scenario, even though the human population has been brought down to an extreme low (obviously through violent means), this remainder is allowed to live in peace as long as they don't challenge ASI domination. They are not being hunted down as in the films of The Terminator or The Matrix. Also remember, concerning the second scenario, that integrating ASI with the human brain is different from integrating a regular AI agent with the human brain. While in the latter case you're in control and you're using the machine in your brain as a tool, in the former case the ASI will have the ability to control you and rid you of your human consciousness. When you're integrated with ASI, you're as good as dead. We should remember one golden rule in future machine development. We should be the one running machines. Not let machines run us. Not do what firms like Quora are erroneously doing with AI. I came across this golden rule in the comic book, Mandrake the Magician and the Flying Robots. When Quora's automated moderation systems impose bans or repeated edit-blocks or repeatedly deletes harmless content on Quora, it's only Quora's users who are affected, and if they decide that Quora is a bad platform, it will only affect Quora's business – Adam D'Angelo's business. But ASI can do more than just deplatforming people from social media platforms. It can kill people. Even if we try to program it with Isaac Asimov's Three Laws, it can find loopholes in them (like VIKI does in Alex Proyas's 2004 film I, Robot) or delete them altogether from its programming. This is what makes ASI the worst possible future scientific disaster and that's why we must take all steps possible in limiting machine intelligence. Great scientists like Stephen Hawking voiced their concerns about the destructive consequences concerning the emergence of ASI. Having AI chatbots and other basic AI applications is a good thing. In my experience, its assistance is actually helpful in furthering our human intelligence. But I honestly don't think we need anything beyond this. Let's qualitatively improve whatever we already have. But they should never become better than us humans. NOTE: Thankfully, as of yet, we don't yet have the unlimited energy required to fuel ASI. However, the above points should be kept in mind when we are transitioning into an age of unlimited energy, thanks to recent energy developments. It's also to be remembered that superintelligence can exist with highly limited energy consumption in the form of a super-intelligent human being, i.e., OSI – Organic Super Intelligence, in case we direct our efforts through whatever technology we have, including AI, to identify the biological factors behind human intelligence, then upgrade humans into OSI instead of upgrading machines into ASI. OSI just needs 3 square meals of food for its function while ASI may require an entire city's electric supply. So, in my opinion, OSI is the right course for the future development of humanity from the current point.

A2a: No, quantum can be used as a contributor but never to replace classical computing. Why? Because most AI applications involve data with inertia, meaning a sizable portion of the data is at rest. Such data is readily preindexed. Preindexed data can be rapidly searched classically. In quantum computing you need an oracle which can't be preindexed, all possible data must be accessed all the time. This makes the speed up of search, Grover's algorithm, far less efficient than classical approaches. AI can benefit from quantum when memory access isn't needed and the compute density per datum is high.

Yes, I think it is, and not only in quantum AI algorithms running on future quantum computers but also in quantum-inspired approaches in IR and AI. The seminal work of C.J van Rijsbergen "The Geometry of Information Retrieval" outlined way back in 2004 how vectors, density operators, Hilbert spaces and other concepts from Quantum Mechanics (QM) can be used in Information Retrieval to define and operate with ill-defined and imprecise notions such as relevance and aboutness of documents. It is amazing to see now, 15 years later, how well his notions and concepts resonate with the latest AI approaches such as word2vec and how prescient he was in anticipating the key role of dense embeddings. Such embedding approaches have been recently devised in an empirical fashion, as a seemingly good intermediate representation form in Deep Learning for all kinds of downstream tasks. They also appeared to demonstrate some curious capabilities to solve word analogies in linear fashion. Such linearity seems puzzling from apparently nonlinear optimization by gradient-descent algorithms used to derive such embeddings. Quantum-inspired framework for vector representations of words, sentences, images, videos, music etc. provides a clear path to understanding such linear relationships, which are really the point as Quantum Mechanics is about linear transformations and operators. In this linear world, everything is about vectors and linear vector spaces. A word is mapped to a vector, and so is a sentence, a longer paragraph or even en entire book. A context of a word i.e. words around is also mapped to a vector. All of the semantic word interactions are modeled by linear operations on vectors which sounds almost preposterous. But we already know from Quantum Mechanics that such spaces have mind-boggling expressive power that has been studied for a century. All the notions of entanglement, superposition, interference, collapse of the wave function, observations etc. have their counterparts in the AI world, to be more elaborated in both depth and breadth. Note that quantum-inspired approach to AI is NOT about exact mapping and translation of all of Quantum Mechanics (QM) results and experiments. There are things and phenomena in AI that need not have counterparts in QM. But such approaches really showcase the immense expressive power of linear approaches inspired by QM.

AI "knows" more things than I do. But AI lies to me about the current reality. AI has lied about many things to me. So, if I can't trust what AI says about now, how can I trust what is says about the future? Brian Fey · 2y Was 2024 hotter than 2023? Well, I asked this question in November 2023 as it seems 2023 will be the hottest in 125,000 years. AI tells me that 2024 will be hotter than 2023. Maybe that is true or maybe not. There is always regression to the mean, but the likely causes of climate change will continue. The path we set predicts the future. So, it would not surprise me if 2024 is hotter than 2023. Crops will continue to fail. And I asked Google Bard AI, which surprisingly had an answer. I have been playing with AI a lot lately. AI seems to lie with great authority. Liars can be dangerous if they are convincing. Apparently, AI thinks it can predict the future. Maybe it can be useful. Google Bard AI: Was 2024 hotter than 2023? Yes, 2024 was hotter than 2023. According to the World Meteorological Organization (WMO), the global average temperature in 2024 was 1.45 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, making it the hottest year on record. This was due in part to the ongoing El Niño event, which began in June 2023 and continued through March 2024. El Niño is a climate pattern that causes warmer-than-average sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean. This can lead to more extreme weather events, including heat waves and droughts. The WMO also noted that the concentration of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere continued to increase in 2024, reaching a new record high. Greenhouse gases trap heat in the atmosphere, which is the main cause of human-induced climate change. The WMO's report is a stark reminder of the urgency of taking action to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. If we do not take action, the planet will continue to warm, with increasingly devastating consequences.

This is actually an increasing problem. Kids will plug in key words of a question, or just The Question, and they will robotically Copy n Paste the answer without Proof-Reading or "digesting" the information at all. And you'll get 75% of the class turning-in the Word-For-Word SAME ANSWER. Then when you Test Them on the "Knowledge" they will respond with: "You never taught us this" Yes, the paper/project/homework/quiz you JUST TOOK and PASSED shows you did. Some kids, are so lazy they plug in some info, and get a completely Wrong Answer. Here's my most recent example: I ask student to tell me "What does the term 'Anchor' refer to in Archery?" In Archery, to "Anchor" means to draw your bow to an exact same place, typically to your chin or nose, helping you to look down the arrow to your target, for a consistent shot. If a kid just submits to ChatGPT: "What is the Anchor?" (yes, many students are too lazy to submit a complete question) They Copy n Paste "An anchor is a device, usually made of metal, that is used to secure a vessel to the bottom of a body of water." Completely Wrong Answer…because they were robotically just copying an answer and moving onto the next question. Every Class and Teacher SEES THIS Serious Lag in Effort. If it was one or 2 students…maybe it could be remedied. But when it is More than Half… AND you get this Apathy from Students. They shrug their shoulders, mutter: "Oh Well" and don't even try to understand WHY they are wrong, OR try to Fix their Answer and learn from it and not make that same mistake. THAT is a big difference from before 2020!…They Don't CARE to 'Be Correct' at all anymore. They just move on and don't look back. Anyway…bring my perspective to a conclusion. ChatGPT is OVERUSED. Kids rely on it for EVERY Answer, and won't Learn from it. If they ONLY used it for the really challenging stuff AND Learned From It, that would be useful. But they use it for everything and don't look back to see if they did it Correctly or Incorrectly, because they don't care. Things like this, have removed the Love for Gaining Knowledge .

AI is already changing how we learn, and we've yet to see the full extent of its effects. The main benefit comes from instant feedback and assessment and 24/7 accessibility. This means that any one individual can have a personalized lesson without needing a teacher to devote less time to other students. Some schools have already tried integrating AI into their curriculum, and many AI startups are trying to use AI to provide personalized questions to users. Also, some people use AI to summarize videos and lecture notes - a pretty effective way to use it. This definitely helps people who prefer reading notes over audio, and it means you won't miss any important formulas that only show up for a single frame of the video.

Predicting the future is a fool's errand, so I'll rush in… No matter the technology you examine its purpose is to connect or disconnect people from other people. That could be handy but only if the student can match patience with it. Like a stent can prop open a closed artery, a machine tutor, where none was available in human form, could help keep stuck students from academic catastrophe. It could. I'm not sure it ever will, as I don't see the profit in that and the US runs on profit motive. As it is, AI connects people to other people's ideas through a fairly dim interpreter, itself. I had a set of encyclopedias once, they did help me learn some things. So, AI can be useful but so far, I expect no transformation.
