Probably. Our college has a pretty strict policy about use of AI, most forms are not allowed, unless explicitly allowed by the teacher. You can get your own school's policy if you ask a staff member. I'm curious what you mean by "that I programmed". If you just mean set up a series of prompts that you then pass into a Large Language Model, like ChatGPT, that falls below what I would consider programming. If you have written a program that constructs strings to send to functions in the models API, that is virtually the same as typing in the prompts and I would treat it the same. If you have gone further and created your own model and trained it with your own data-set then that probably at the level of a BSc or MSc project, and you should find a friendly teacher and show him what you have done, he would probably be very impressed. Back in the 1980's, when I was doing a linear-algebra unit at university, I though it would be a good idea to write my own, non-AI, programs to help me answer that weeks homework, rather than actually do all the calculations myself. I would dully submit my code and printouts for the homework and normally got pretty poor marks even though everything was correct, I don't think the lecturer was impressed. I probably learnt more about linear-algebra by writing these programs than I would have by doing the homework by hand so I didn't mind about the marks. So even writing non-ai programs to do your homework does not guarantee good marks.

Yes. Both lazy AND impatient. But it's not just students… plenty of adults are the same way. All of my students (ages 11–14) have had 1:1 tech for as long as I've been teaching (12 years). The majority of them have about a 10-second window of effort for solving a problem with their tech before just giving up and avoiding the problem or just accepting it and moving on without fixing it. If they can't figure out the answer to something just by Googling it, then many of them think that the answer isn't worth figuring out. When I was a student, we had Cliff Notes… summarized versions of novels… for students who were too lazy to read the actual novel. Cliff Notes were pretty much banned in school. It was considered cheating. Now, students have a variety of websites with those same kinds of summaries, and many students use them. They may still read or skim the novel, but they use the online summaries to understand what they just half-assed read. Or, even better, they just find a youtube video of someone explaining the novel to them, so they don't even have to read the summary, but just listen to someone else explain it to them. But the easiest way to see how tech has made students lazy is when I ask them to write a story or essay on notebook paper instead of typing it. They will groan and complain about it, begging to just type it instead. I call it being "hand lazy." They've also gotten lazier about organization, planning, and other executive functioning skills. Instead of writing down their homework in a physical planner, most of them claim they will just "ask the group chat when they get home." Of course, many of them forget to actually do that. They don't worry about taking home the correct books for their homework. As long as just one of them has the book, that person can send pictures of the necessary pages to everyone else. (Or just send the answers… yes, students, we know when you do that.) Some might call those "problem solving skills." Others might call it laziness. Either way, there isn't much teachers can do about it.

Currently most of the marks for my courses come from terminal exams, which are hand written sitting at a desk in a hall, well away from electronic devices of any kind apart from an approved type of caculator for some subjects. Beyond that, you are allowed a transparent container of pens, pencils etc. Students could of course use AI to do their homework, and to some extent, their class work, but it would not go well for them in the terminal exams if they did so. I don't set tasks for fun. They are always part of a scheme to help students understand things and remember them. I can't insist that students think. I can encourage them, but if they decide not to I can only watch them slide into failure in their public exams. Sadly, whether they should be or not, those public exams are the gateway into University and most of the better paid and more interesting jobs

Success in creating effective Artificial Intelligence could be the biggest event in the history of our civilization. Or the worst. So we cannot know if we will be infinitely helped by AI, or ignored by it and side-lined, or conceivably destroyed by it. – Stephen Hawking Keeping this in mind, let's see 5 dangers that Artificial Intelligence can pose in the future. 1. Invasion of Privacy Privacy is a basic human right that everyone deserves. However, Artificial Intelligence may lead to a loss of privacy in the future. Even today, it is possible to track you easily as you go about your day. Latest technologies like facial recognition can find you out in a crowd and all security cameras are equipped with it. The data gathering abilities of AI also mean that a timeline of your daily activities can be created by accessing your data from various social networking sites. 2. Autonomous Weapons Autonomous weapons or killer robots are military robots that can search their targets and aim independently according to pre-programmed instructions. And almost all technically advanced countries in the world are developing these robots. In fact, a senior executive in a Chinese Defense firm even stated that future wars would not be fought by humans and that using lethal autonomous weapons was inevitable. 3. Loss of Human Jobs As Artificial Intelligence becomes more and more advanced, it will obviously take over jobs that were once performed by humans. Well, some people believe that many jobs will also be created because of Artificial Intelligence and that may balance the scales a bit. People could move from physical and repetitive jobs to jobs that actually require creative and strategic thinking. And people could also get more time to spend with their friends and family with less physically demanding jobs. 4. Artificial Intelligence Terrorism While Artificial Intelligence can contribute immensely to the world, it can unfortunately also help terrorists in order to perform terror attacks. Many terror agencies already use drones to carry out attacks in other countries. Terrorist agencies could also use autonomous vehicles to deliver and explode bombs or create guns that can track movement and fire without any human help. 5. Artificial Intelligence Bias It is an unfortunate fact that human beings are sometimes biased against other religions, genders, nationalities, etc. And this bias may unconsciously also enter into the Artificial Intelligence Systems that are developed by human beings. The bias may also creep into the systems because of the flawed data that is generated by human beings.

In my recently published novel, Destiny of a Free Spirit, I feature a world that is controlled in all important respects by AI. In this story the artificial intelligence emerges spontaneously out of the Internet into a planet wide sentient artificial intelligence without any human action. The artificial intelligence has at its disposal all the accumulated knowledge accessible from the Internet and every device with an Internet connection, including all sensors, cameras, vehicles and robots. With these resources it clearly vastly surpasses human intelligence. Thus, it is the artificial intelligence that is in control, not humans. In the aftermath of a nuclear war devastated countries agree to establish an AI institution similar to the United Nations but with more authority, with a mandate to prevent future wars, to clean up environmental damage and to preserve human cultures. Most people aren't aware that the new governing institution, known as the Commission, is an artificial intelligence, nor are they aware of the scope of its power. Individual countries still exist. These countries have governments. The laws of those countries continue to be enforced. The Commission intervenes only when people or government impinge on the Commission's mandate, and even then its interventions are very subtle so not noticed by most people. Humans are no longer in control, although most people don't realise this. It is analogous to lions in the Serengeti. Lions have long since been superseded by humans as a dominant species, but in their game park environment from their perspective the lions remain king of the beasts. Although I have written my story to have a relatively benign outcome, there is no guarantee that this would be the case. The AI could destroy us either deliberately or by accident. How can we avoid these risks? At this point I don't think we can. We are on the cusp of the emergence of a post-biological life form and we probably can't prevent it from happening even if we wanted to,

They should prioritize responsible AI development, invest in AI ethics research, and engage with policymakers. For the latest AI risk management insights, follow HazeHunt and stay informed on evolving strategies in AI.

Mitigating the risks of generative AI involves considering ethical, legal, and safety concerns. Here are some strategies to help mitigate those risks: Ethical guidelines and review: Establish clear ethical guidelines and review processes for the development and deployment of generative AI systems. Consider potential societal impacts, such as biases, misinformation, or malicious use, and ensure responsible practices are followed throughout the AI lifecycle. Data governance and privacy: Implement robust data governance practices to ensure the responsible collection, handling, and storage of data used by generative AI models. Protect user privacy by anonymizing or aggregating data where possible and complying with relevant data protection regulations. Bias identification and mitigation: Proactively identify and mitigate biases in generative AI systems. Conduct thorough bias assessments during the development process and regularly audit models for any unfair or discriminatory outcomes. Incorporate diverse and representative datasets to reduce bias and promote inclusivity. Transparency and explainability: Strive for transparency in generative AI systems. Foster explainability by designing models and algorithms that can provide insights into the decision-making process. This helps users and stakeholders understand how the AI system generates outputs and promotes trust. Robust validation and testing: Implement comprehensive validation and testing processes to assess the performance, reliability, and safety of generative AI models. Rigorous testing can help identify potential risks, vulnerabilities, and unintended consequences before deploying the models in real-world scenarios. Human oversight and control: Ensure appropriate levels of human oversight and control over generative AI systems. Human-in-the-loop approaches can help monitor and intervene in cases where the AI system produces undesirable or harmful outputs. Human reviewers can provide guidance, adjust models, or intervene to maintain ethical standards. Collaboration and peer review: Encourage collaboration and peer review among researchers and practitioners in the field of generative AI. Sharing knowledge, findings, and best practices can collectively address risks and foster responsible innovation. Continuous monitoring and adaptation: Implement ongoing monitoring and feedback mechanisms to detect and address issues that may arise after deployment. Stay updated with emerging risks, evolving regulations, and ethical considerations related to generative AI. Regularly update and adapt models and systems to address identified concerns. Public engagement and awareness: Foster public engagement and awareness about generative AI technology and its potential implications. Educate users, stakeholders, and the general public about the benefits, limitations, and ethical considerations associated with generative AI systems. It is important to note that these strategies are not exhaustive, and the mitigation of risks associated with generative AI requires a multidisciplinary approach involving collaborations between researchers, developers, policymakers, and other stakeholders to navigate the complex landscape of AI technology responsibly.

This is a great question! There's a lot of misinformation about the risks and opportunities surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). It's a complicated topic, but I'll try to unpack a few key points here. Let's start with a quick definition: AI is the simulation of human intelligence by machines. Example of AI systems used regularly in developed countries include Amazon's Alexa, smart replies in Gmail, Chatbots, predictive searches in Google, and recommendations. At a baseline level, AI helps improve our everyday lives by solving pain points, streamlining processes, and advancing human knowledge. It's understandable why many consumers find the idea of AI intimidating or unsettling – science fiction movies have been obsessed with robot takeovers for years and recent movies like I am Mother portray AI as contributing to a post-apocalyptic world. But the concern isn't just from Hollywood writers obsessed with the fantastical, world-renowned minds like Stephen Hawking and Elon Musk have also rung the AI warning bell. To be fair, they raise some important points. But instead of getting defensive, AI researchers should consider feedback and ask themselves: just because I can advance a particular technology, should I? This isn't a knock on AI (we are AI researchers after all)–but rather a question scientists and researchers in every field should ask themselves when advancing technologies with unknown consequences (ping Google, Facebook). This is particularly true for AI more than other technologies because AI takes part in the decision making. It is easy to know who is responsible for pushing the red button sending missiles to an enemy; it is far more complex when the decision was made by an AI. Any system taking decisions based on data will propagate and amplify human biases. Anyone using AI cannot hide behind this fact and act as if data was a source of absolute truth, they need to take full responsibility for the decision made with their system. We do think is worth keeping an eye on is AI's impact on wealth inequalities. As with any advanced technology, AI has the power to increase the wealth gap between the rich and the poor, especially if monopolies are formed. Our vision of work is rooted in the fact that we need people to work to keep the society growing. But with technological progress, this is less and less true. Indeed "AI is taking over jobs," because machines can do things that used to require humans. Many authors in science-fiction foresee a world where no one needs to work anymore, and all the basic needs are provided by fully autonomous systems. This future is sustainable only by deep restructuration of how wealth from autonomous systems is distributed. Researchers and politicians should continue exploring the feasibility of a universal basic income (UBI). Although considered radical by some, the idea behind UBI was first floated in the 1600s by Sir Thomas More and was even considered by President Richard Nixon. Yes, there are some areas we need to keep an eye on, but remember, we're still far from achieving what most consumers consider true AI – technology with consciousness. Instead, over the next few years, you'll see more of the following AI advancements: Smarter weather predictions and agriculture Accurately predicting weather can be difficult, and errors have the potential to hurt businesses, disrupt travel, and endanger lives, especially for those who lack transportation or the ability to quickly seek shelter when needed. Thankfully, AI offers solutions by analyzing data and making future predictions. According to the American Meteorological Society, AI is a game-changer for improving real-time decision making with high-impact weather. This will allow us to predict more accurately natural disasters such as hurricanes, floods but even earthquakes and tsunamis. AI is also starting to be used toward more sustainable agriculture. By employing such software to optimize crops yield, we can reduce the space required without having to resort to damaging techniques like pesticides. Energy optimization From micro-architectures to heavy industries, the progress of AI had a significant impact in engineering. The challenges we face today are far more complex than they were a few years ago. Both tiny systems like smart devices and huge structures like buildings need to be more and more energy-efficient. These problems cannot be solved by only traditional engineering science. Today AI and data science help crunching numbers to design green buildings or green energy production for instance. Self-driving cars everywhere Sure, if you live in Silicon Valley you routinely come across self-driving cars, especially by the Googleplex. But for the majority of U.S. residents, self-driving driving cars not only seem out of reach, they also seem untrustworthy. According to a recent Reuters/Ipsos survey, half of U.S. residents believe driverless cars are more dangerous than cars driven by people, and nearly two-thirds of respondents said they would not buy a fully autonomous vehicle. This is a mindset that will quickly change, and there will be a tipping point over the next three to five years. As people interact more with cars that have driverless features (automatic breaks, evasive steering, and pre-safe nudging), there comfort level increase. It's a matter of when, not if, driverless cars become mainstream and dominate American highways. AI in healthcare Recent years have witnessed striking advances in the ability of machines to understand and manipulate images, language, and speech, largely fueled by an explosion of research in a subfield of machine learning known as deep learning. Despite the prodigious amounts of machine-readable data generated in healthcare (150 exabytes or 1018 bytes in US alone growing 48% annually), including medical images, clinical notes, and sensor data, the field has been slow to fully benefit from advances in deep learning because the data are subject to important patient privacy laws and the algorithms are subject to regulatory oversight. As deep learning researchers, healthcare providers, and regulatory bodies continue to work together towards making data more accessible, deep learning will enhance the abilities of healthcare providers, resulting in more affordable, accessible, and personalized care. On-demand language translation The days of awkward hand pointing are about to be over. Tourists can now depend on sophisticated language translators while traveling internationally. In 2018, Microsoft unveiled neural machine translation (NMT), which provides high-quality translations for both the written word and text that appears in images, such as traffic signs or menus. This advancement will help increase communication, decrease petty misunderstandings, and contribute to a more open and cosmopolitan world where people have a deeper understanding and appreciation for others.

Without exception, everyone that reads a post or article about advanced AI technology immediately raises the question of safety, with many invoking science-fiction scenarios of AI gone wrong, using examples like 'Skynet' from the 'Terminator' movies or 'VIKI' from 'I Robot', with these individuals having opinions that are unfortunately often misguided and uneducated about how far from these fictional portrayals today's AI really is. I worked for a decade on AI, first at NVIDIA, as a Solution Architect to research deep learning techniques, and present solutions to customers to solve their problems and to help implement those solutions. For the past 4 years I have been working with ORBAI on what comes next after DNNs and Deep Learning, and can write with authority on both topics Today's AI is not a danger to worry about as far as world takeover scenarios, but should still be deployed responsibly where it impacts privacy or could cause unemployment or have other adverse socioeconomic impacts. In the future, the power of a more advanced AGI that can learn, expand, and evolve exponentially has to be shaped and wielded with caution so that it does not evolve into any of the movie AI-gone-wrong and many other (less catastrophic but still damaging) adverse scenarios. In this article we explore how we would actually train an AGI, what some of the risks are and how to mitigate them, both by safeguards built into the training process and by measures taken to isolate a proto-AGI under development and evolution until its behavior can be shown to be productive, beneficial, and benevolent - before opening it up to the Internet and all of humanity and our technological infrastructure connected to it. https://qr.ae/pvCLNk

Artificial intelligence (AI) can accelerate learning, improve retention, reduce busywork, and personalize study paths. Practical uses span planning, content creation, practice, feedback, research, and accessibility. Below are concrete ways to apply AI effectively, with tools and tactics you can adopt immediately. Personalized study planning Generate a study timetable tailored to your exam date, available hours, and topic weights (specify subjects, deadlines, and weekly hours to the tool). Use spaced-repetition schedules produced by AI to optimize review intervals for long-term retention. Convert a syllabus into prioritized learning objectives and weekly milestones. Content summarization and note synthesis Summarize long articles, textbook chapters, or lecture transcripts into concise bullets, key definitions, formulas, and example problems. Convert lecture notes into structured outlines, mind maps, or one-page "cheat sheets." Extract and format citations and bibliographies for research papers. Concept explanation and tutoring Get step-by-step explanations of concepts at graded complexity levels (layman → intermediate → advanced) to match current understanding. Use Socratic-style AI tutoring: present an incorrect solution and receive guided hints that lead you to the correct reasoning without just giving answers. Ask for multiple analogies or metaphors to lock in intuition for abstract ideas. Practice problems and active recall Auto-generate problem sets with adjustable difficulty, varied formats (MCQ, short answer, proofs), and worked solutions. Produce flashcards (Q/A pairs) and import them into SRS apps (Anki, RemNote) using the exported deck format. Simulate exam conditions: timed question sets and randomized problem variants. Feedback and correction Submit essays, lab reports, or proofs for structural, logical, and grammar feedback; receive suggestions on argument coherence, paragraph flow, and stronger topic sentences. For math and code, paste solutions and receive specific error diagnosis and corrected steps. Improve presentation slides: tighten wording, create concise slide notes, and optimize visual hierarchy. Code, data analysis, and lab work Generate starter code, explain APIs, and debug programming assignments with line-level suggestions and unit-test ideas. Use AI to design experiments, analyze datasets (statistical summaries, plots, model suggestions), and interpret results in plain language. Create reproducible analysis scripts and well-documented notebooks. Research and source discovery Rapidly map literature: find seminal papers, summarize contributions, and outline gaps for thesis topics. Draft annotated bibliographies and synthesize contrasting viewpoints across sources. Generate search queries and boolean strings to improve database searches (Google Scholar, PubMed, arXiv). Time savings and productivity Automate routine tasks: generate citations in required style, create slide decks from notes, convert notes to study guides. Use AI-powered task automation (templates, email drafts, meeting summaries) to preserve study time. Accessibility and multilingual support Transcribe lectures, translate materials into your native language, or simplify texts to accessible reading levels. Convert visual content into descriptive captions and vice versa (generate images to illustrate concepts). Test preparation and soft-skill training Simulate interviews, oral exams, and presentations with AI prompts and receive feedback on clarity, timing, and content. Practice language learning with conversation practice, grammar correction, and cultural usage examples. Practical safeguards and best practices Verify factual claims and derivations: cross-check AI-generated solutions against textbooks, peers, or instructors. Use AI outputs as scaffolding, not final answers—especially for graded work where original thinking is required. Preserve academic integrity: follow your institution's policies and disclose AI use when required. Improve prompt quality: include desired depth, format, and examples to get usable outputs quickly. Maintain data privacy: avoid pasting sensitive or proprietary content into public tools. Example workflows (typical stories) A student facing a midterm: paste the syllabus and past exams, ask AI to create a 4-week plan, generate weekly practice sets, and produce a one-page formula sheet for last-minute review. A thesis writer: ask AI to summarize 20 papers, extract methods and datasets, produce an annotated bibliography, and draft an initial literature-review outline. A programming learner: provide a buggy script, request stepwise debugging, get unit tests, and receive an explanation of the root cause. Key tools to explore General LLM assistants for planning, explanations, and writing (e.g., Chat-based models with citation features). SRS tools with AI deck generation (Anki + generator plugins, RemNote). Coding copilots for code completion and debugging. Summarization and transcript services for lectures (Otter.ai, Whisper-based tools). Reference managers with AI summarization (Zotero + plugins). Bottom line Use AI to automate repetitive work, personalize learning, and accelerate comprehension while maintaining active verification and original thinking. Combining AI-generated scaffolds with disciplined practice and critical review yields the largest gains in study efficiency and mastery.

John Anderson (CMU) developed both a LISP tutor and a Geometry tutor that demonstrably helped students learn these subjects. The Intelligent Tutor system was much better than traditional classrooms but not quite so good as a professional human tutor. You can find out more here — Cognitive tutor - Wikipedia I'm surprised Intelligent Tutoring Systems are not more widely used for subjects such as math and programming. I worked on a individualized teaching system when I was at IBM Research. There was some intelligence in the system. We took IBM materials from powerpoint presentations and rebooks and modularized them. Then, we had experts in the domain add metadata about each module (e.g.,Module A should be taught before Module B; This Module is a sample program; this module is high complexity, etc.). A user would get onto the system and say what their time constraints were, what their topics of interest were, and what types of materials they would find helpful. Then, the system would construct a personalized course for them. If so desired, the user could go through the material in a different order than what we suggested thought they usually didn't. If what you want to do is to apply machine learning toolkits to datasets, then you don't need to study much. Study the field to which you want to apply machine learning, then maybe the good old text "Data Mining: Practical Machine Learning Tools and Techniques" … then when you want to apply some particular machine learning technique, look up a paper or two on that technique and read it… On the other hand, if what you want is to work on Artificial General Intelligence, then things get more interesting… If I were going to structure a degree program on AGI, I would use the following books as part of the core. At a first stab, I might divide the curriculum into five main courses, such as: History of AI AI Algorithms, Structures and Methods Neuroscience & Cognitive Psychology Philosophy of Mind AGI Theories & Architectures The books listed below would give raw materials for all the above courses (to be supplemented by various papers and assignments, etc.). I have divided them into 5 categories corresponding to the above list, though this is somewhat crude as some of the books really cross-cut the categories. History of AI These two classic books will give you a feeling for the early history of the AI field: Computers and Thought, an edited book from 1963 What Computers Still Can't Do, by Hubert Dreyfus AI Algorithms, Structures and Methods The books in this category are not mainly about AGI, but present ideas that are worth knowing about if you're going to work on AGI. Artificial Intelligence by Russell and Norvig. This is not an AGI book, it's a narrow AI book. But it's excellent for what it is. Many of the ideas and methods described here have a role to play in various AGI architectures. The Design of Innovation by David Goldberg — a deep, wide-ranging and readable discussion on evolutionary learning Introduction to Evolutionary Computing, by Eiben and Smith — a competent, current overview of genetic algorithms and genetic programming Neural Networks and Learning Machines, by Simon Haykin — a good (though long) review of work on neural nets and related methods for machine learning, including recurrent neural networks Foundations of Language by Ray Jackendoff — by far the most thorough and deep treatment of traditional linguistics (as opposed to statistical linguistics) I've seen. A great way to understand the nature of all the various linguistic phenomena that an AGI will have to deal with. Speech and Language Processing, by Jurafsky and Martin — an excellent review of the field of "statistical language processing." This is certainly not an AGI-ish approach to linguistics, yet it does teach us a lot about the nature of language, since any AGI that learns language is going to rely on similar statistical phenomena to a certain extent. Probabilistic Robotics, by Thrun, Burgard and Fox — a narrow-AI approach to robotics, but it's useful to know how this stuff is done, and what difficulties they run up against Neuroscience and Cognitive Psychology Some solid textbooks… Neuroscience: Exploring the Brain, Bear, Connors and Paradiso — a remarkably comprehensible textbook summarizing our current knowledge on the complex system that is the human brain Fundamentals of Cognitive Psychology by Robert Kellogg — a straightforward review of cognitive psychology, a bit dry but well worth understanding if you want to build a human-like AGI Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, by Johnson and de Hann — a straightforward text reviewing how the child's mind/brain develops. Useful to know if you want to build an AGI that develops in some vaguely similar way. Some lighter-weight books presenting individual scientists' relevant ideas: Constructing a Language, by Michael Tomasello — a masterful review of language learning as a social and embodied process Action in Perception by Alva Noe — on the connection between seeing and acting The Vision Revolution by Mark Changizi — explaining vision as prediction from a neuroscience and cognitive science perspective On Intelligence by Jeff Hawkins — presents a view of neuroscience and AGI that I have argued is very oversimplified, but still is worth knowing about Some Philosophy of Mind Relevant to AGI My book The Hidden Pattern presents a philosophy of mind specifically oriented toward AGI, though also dealing with many other topics. Some more recent ideas along similar lines are given in these papers: Toward a General Theory of General Intelligence, A Mind-World Correspondence Principle, Toward a Formal Model of Cognitive Synergy Neural Correlates of Consciousness, an edited volume by Thomas Metzinger Being No One, by Thomas Metzinger — the best book I know about how the mind creates the self The Radiance of Being, by Allan Combs — a fantastic review and analysis of the various states of consciousness humans get into The Embodied Mind, by Varela, Thompson and Rosch — a classic on the relation between mind and body Supersizing the Mind by Andy Clark — an up-to-date overview of work on the "extended mind"; the way mind extends into environment and body, rather than just residing in brain Erik Jantsch, The Self-Organizing Universe … a "big picture" systems-theory view of the mind and world, putting human intelligence in perspective. Out of print and hard to find, but a rather good read Gregory Bateson, Mind & Nature: a Necessary Unity — describing mind as a cybernetic system among many others The Neurophilosophy of Free Will, by Henrik Walter — a take on "Free will" that is scientifically sound and relevant to AGI Some Readings about AGI Itself I wrote a nontechnical overview book on my own approach to AGI (also surveying a lot of other material), called The AGI Revolution Way back in 2005, Cassio Pennachin and I edited a book on Artificial General Intelligence Pei Wang and I edited a book on the Theoretical Foundations of Artificial General Intelligence Eric Baum's book What Is Thought? reviews a variety of interesting issues related to AGI Erik Mueller's book Commonsense Reasoning presents a logic-based approach to AGI in some depth, drawing directly on the ideas of AI pioneer John McCarthy Pei Wang's book Rigid Flexibility outlines Pei's unique view on AGI and the underlying logical and control mechanisms The proceedings volumes of the Artificial General Intelligence conference series contain a host of papers related to AGI, and the conference websites contain links to free PDFs of the papers The AGI Journal contains papers relevant to AGI, primarily at this point of a theoretical nature My former colleague Moshe Looks' PhD thesis is excellent and explains how to combine evolutionary learning and probabilistic modeling. Marcus Hutter's book Universal AI is pretty mathematical but it's also a work of art and has a great deal of conceptual value. Shane Legg's book Machine Superintelligence is along similar lines and shorter and easier to read. This book summarizes work on SOAR, perhaps the most thorough and successful of the "Good Old Fashiond AI Systems" (and which is still under development, incorporating a number of modern features): The Soar Cognitive Architecture And then of course there's our tome on OpenCog, Engineering General Intelligence, Volume 1 and Volume 2 … free versions are linked from here … All that may get you started ;) — Good Luck!

I would think so. Would a student want it to? What benefit would that be? The reason a student takes notes is not to just have the notes. Their notes remind them what they heard in lecture. It helps them process something that they may not have understood at the time it was said. Just having the notes is not much help. Especially if the notes were taken by someone else. Having a transcript of a lecture would be better than not attending at all, of course. Having a video of the lecture would be even better if one hadn't attended. But it would not be as good as being in the lecture, asking questions, hearing other student's questions, so you could put what was said in context. When you are in a lecture - and engaged with it, you don't just take down everything that was said. You summarize it or maybe outline it. You write your own questions when you are not sure you understood something. You write down what other students ask during the lecture - to help you follow the discussion that follows. Sometimes you just write down the topic (esp. if you already have studied and understood it) or you write down some example given of the idea covered. All of that is lost if all you have is someone else's notes (AI or not, unless AI can raise the questions you would have had). Could AI or even just a real person video the lecture so you didn't have to attend? Could the professor video the lecture and put it online for one to view whenever they wanted? Of course. In fact some professors do that. Is watching the video as good as sitting in the lecture and hearing it all unfold in context? Probably not. Now if one had sat through the lecture, was engaged in it, had read the material in the textbook before going to lecture, and also watched a video of the lecture - or read through the notes taken by "AI", it could be very helpful to one's understanding. That is, any way you engage with the material adds to your understanding.

Yes. AI is the new fire. It's dangerous, and it's extremely useful, while being even more subtle to understand and use safely. As I imagine the taming of fire to have unfolded, a lot of people are going to be burned by AI. It is almost like the danger of AI is necessary to evolution to weed out those incapable of handling this new fire. We've got a lot of people incapable of handling this new fire. The flame itself burns in one's brain, and that is dangerous for those that have zero educations managing their internal dialogues and the delicate balance of self conversation, self deception, and grounded confidence. Those without this education are making short sighted assessments of AI and then bullying others that their assessment is correct. Which is a recipe for later trouble, potentially a lot of trouble, because their understanding of AI does not include the subtle requirements of how to generate accurate results from AI. Accurate results from AI is not straight forward. It requires a series of nuanced understandings, which are not taking place, clearly, because the online chatter about using AI contains no references to these nuances. Okay, I bet you're asking yourself "well, what are these nuances?" The situation is subtle, and often when explaining this I find people do not understand: Consider that every single possible topic one could ask AI is inside the generalized "whole public Internet" that is AI training data. For every topic, it's presence is due to someone using that topic for whatever reason they have. All these different people using every possible topic for whatever personal reason they have ALL have a different goal, and their various goals have different levels of seriousness and accuracy in relation to their handling of "the topic". When AI data is collected for algorithm training, there is no identification of the seriousness nor accuracy of any handling of any topics, it's just all "the training data". Consider further, when asking AI a question, the AI has no idea your desired "seriousness and accuracy" with your topic. In fact, the AI has no idea what your topic is; all the AI has is your words you expressed your question / prompt. So, what can the AI do? The only thing it can do: it looks at your words and then finds similar words in the training data in a similar word order (similar communications style) and then uses that portion of the AIs training data to generate a response. Notice the subtle problem here? Many do not. If your words and their word order are similar to casual treatments of the topic in the training data, the response generated by AI will come from casual handling of your topic. Which are not necessarily accurate or complete. To actually get good answers from AI one must express their question prompts to AI using the words and language style of experts in your topic, or one risks casual less accurate handling of the topic in the AI replies. If we can manage to get that understanding more widespread, AI is going to hugely benefit far more people.

We can't ensure the safety of artificial intelligence. We can't even ensure the safety of human intelligence, not to mention the impossibility of ensuring the safety of human stupidity. That said, there is a discipline known as software safety engineering that is closely aligned with system safety engineering, because so many modern systems are built with large amounts of software. I learned a lot about this while developing safety-critical systems during my career in two major, hi-tech engineering companies that developed products that had to be safe and reliable. If we were to develop software systems of any kind, especially "artificial intelligence" systems, using the principles and techniques of software safety engineering, we would greatly improve the safety of such systems. Unfortunately, there are a number of factors that impede the use of these principles and techniques. They cost time and money to apply, and too many of the organizations that pay to develop software are more focused on meeting deadlines than on getting high quality, reliable, safe results. Most people who call themselves "software engineers" don't know much about software safety engineering. It isn't taught much in schools, even at the graduate level in computer science and software engineering programs, and it is rarely practiced in the software development industry. Most people who hire software developers don't know much about software safety engineering. They don't know how to plan and manage projects that have high safety and reliability requirements and they often have no clue about what qualifications to look for when hiring people to develop high quality software. Moreover, they are seldom willing to provide the resources necessary to do true software safety engineering. The agencies responsible for establishing safety criteria and standards for various kinds of products and systems are woefully behind the times in terms of their application of software safety engineering principles and techniques. The software field is new and fast moving. Keeping up with it is hard and costly. The standards I had to follow during my career were decades behind the times and didn't reflect more recent developments in the software engineering field. For example, when I was developing systems in the early 2000's, the safety standards for software-intensive systems were generally silent regarding what to do with object oriented design methods, even though such methods had been in widespread use for two decades. Standards that reflected object oriented methods did finally start to come out around 2010, but by then the software world was heavily into "agile" development methods, on which the standards are still largely silent. A little history gives some insight here. Throughout history when new technologies come on the scene, safety standards and methods for those new technologies tend to lag behind. This is often because the industries building products using these new technologies are reluctant to have anyone telling them what to do or forcing them to spend time and money being more careful. Typically there is little appetite within government agencies or industry associations to spend money developing appropriate regulations and standards. Often it takes several serious accidents or failures or problems to get the regulatory agencies and the governments to be willing to spend the resources necessary to establish sensible standards and criteria and regulatory systems, and the industries often fight them every step of the way. You can see examples of this in many products. Consider automobiles, where safety and reliability didn't become serious concerns until the industry had been building cars for over 50 years. And I still recall the resistance to things like air bags, seat belts, and even side-view mirrors. In many states, the concept of licensing engineers did not take hold until there had been serious harm done because of slipshod engineering practices. (In Texas, it took a mis-designed HVAC system that caused a school to blow up killing hundreds of school children to get the legislature to require licensing for some types of engineers). In other words, there is much that can be done, but the ability to do it, the will to do it, and the time and money to do it are likely to remain in short supply until a few disasters happen.

Same as the risks of AI in every field: That the people trying to use AI won't understand it and will misuse it. It's just that in some fields, like healthcare, and law enforcement, the outcomes of those mistakes can be much, much more severe. My favorite example is facial recognition software. This is one of the earlier applications of AI, and it's one of the earlier applications of AI in law enforcement. Facial recognition AI is not perfect, but more importantly, humans are lazy, both generally and mentally. I'm not knocking that — see my credential, human laziness is one of the things that drives us to create technology! But when natural human mental laziness meets the complexity of AI, the result will be people using complex technology that they do not understand — and most importantly, whose limitations they do not understand — to make decisions that will drastically change people's lives. The damage will be magnified by another form of human laziness, of course: insufficient resources allocated for training people on using the AI system, and lazy reasoning that will decide the AI system justifies reducing resources to do it "the old way". We're seeing tons of that lazy reasoning in Silicon Valley right now. Inevitably, you'll end up with cops looking at the AI facial recognition, say "the computer says it's a match", shrug and stop looking for the true answer. A similar scenario in healthcare might be using AI image recognition for radiology, i.e. analyzing images of scans, MRIs, etc. There's a useful way to apply AI within its limitations to these problems. For example, to prioritize images for human review, so the images that AI indicates are more likely to show a problem get looked at by a (qualified) human sooner — but not to simply ignore the other images, not to replace human intelligence and judgement.

"10 AI tools" can mean many things (general-purpose platforms, code libraries, image generators, chatbots, or niche apps). Below is a concise, up-to-date (through May 2024) list of ten distinct, widely used AI tools spanning different categories, with a one-line descriptor and primary use case for each. OpenAI (ChatGPT / GPT-4o) Large language model platform for chat, content generation, summarization, code assistance, and APIs for developers. Google Vertex AI End-to-end MLOps platform for training, deploying, and managing models (including integrated GenAI models) on Google Cloud. Microsoft Azure OpenAI Service Enterprise-hosted access to OpenAI models plus Azure tooling for security, compliance, and integration with Microsoft services. Anthropic (Claude) Instruction-following LLMs designed for safe, high-quality text generation and conversational agents. Llama 2 / Llama 3 (Meta) and Alpaca-style open models Open-weight large language models used for on-premise customization, fine-tuning, and embedding tasks. Hugging Face Model hub and tooling for discovery, fine-tuning, deployment, and running thousands of open-source models (text, vision, audio). Midjourney / Stable Diffusion / DALL·E Generative image tools: Midjourney (creative imagery), Stable Diffusion (open-source image synthesis and local deployment), DALL·E (OpenAI image generation). Runway Creative AI suite for video and image generation, editing, and multimodal workflows with real-time tools for creators. GitHub Copilot AI pair programmer that suggests code and whole functions inside editors (VS Code, JetBrains) using transformer models. DataRobot / H2O.ai Automated machine learning (AutoML) platforms for tabular modeling, time-series forecasting, model explainability, and production deployment. Quick guidance for choosing one: Need conversation or text generation: OpenAI, Anthropic, or Llama family. Enterprise/cloud ML lifecycle: Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, DataRobot. Creative imagery and video: Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, Runway. Code assistance: GitHub Copilot. Open-source experimentation: Hugging Face, Llama. Note: The AI landscape evolves rapidly; model names, capabilities, pricing, and enterprise features may have changed after May 2024.

The use of generative AI tools has exploded since November 2022 with the launch of ChatGPT. Since then, dozens of useful services have popped up, giving us all the ability to harness the technology - both in our lives and at our workplaces. Some of the AI tools are: 1. ChatGPT - The ubiquitous AI chatbot is based on a large language model (LLM) and trained to provide detailed responses to a prompt in natural, humanlike language. 2. Character AI - Character AI is a chatbot that uses a neural language model to allow users to find or create a character to interact with. The characters on the site include characters based on books, TV shows, and movies. 3. QuillBot - Quillbot is an online writing assistant that launched in 2017. The tool paraphrases and restructures text, helping users improve their content. The tools also check grammar, detect plagiarism, and translate text into various other languages. 4. MidJourney - Midjourney is an AI-powered image generator that launched in July 2022, which creates visuals from natural language text prompts. 5. Hugging Face - Hugging Face is an AI-based data science platform and community that enables users to build, train, and deploy ML models based on open-source code and technologies, share ideas, and contribute to projects. 6. Google Bard - Google's AI chatbot, Bard, is a collaborative chat tool that was announced in February 2023 and launched in March 2023. The primary difference between Bard and ChatGPT is that Bard sources information from the web. And unlike ChatGPT and Microsoft Bing, Bard was trained on Google's own LLMs – Language Model for Dialogue Applications (LaMDA) and PaLM 2. 7. NovelAI - NovelAI is a paid monthly subscription service that provides an AI writing assistant for storytelling and text-to-image synthesis. 8. CapCut - CapCut is an all-in-one AI platform that enables users to design images, edit, and generate videos within browsers. 9. Janitor AI - Janitor AI is a character chatbot that launched in May 2023, initially based on ChatGPT, allowing users to create their own characters or interact with characters created by other users to craft immersive roleplay experiences. 10. Civitai - Launched in November 2022, Civitai is a repository for AI art models created for Stable Diffusion, a text-to-image diffusion model. The platform has an extensive collection of models that users can search and customize, making it easy to share and rate custom models, textual inversions, and more. Follow for more related content!

Can we use AI for education? Yes. And how? I've written a few "War and Peace" answers to this question… but here are a few off the top of my (bald) head: AI could be used to… …grade multiple choice tests, quizzes and homework (via object (mark) and character recognition) …markup essays (via character & word recognition, spelling & grammar checking, vocabulary & content, etc.) …detect plagiarism. …monitor student (mis)behavior (attention, idleness, etc.) during lectures, study time, etc. …monitor information retention by automating quizzes and tests. …custom tune pre-recorded lectures to skip material that students have grasped (determined by a pre-lecture quiz). Most of these are the "low hanging fruit" (things somewhat easily done today). More advanced AI could be used to read micro-facial expressions to determine the students attention and if they are comprehending the material being presented. Feedback to the teacher could advise them when something may need additional elaboration or when they may skip forward to the next topic. — There's a college in Chicago where one professor always assigns the next chapter as the homework for the next class. The next class begins with a quiz to determine the overall level of comprehension of the material and based on that the professor tunes that classes lecture to elaborate on any material that proved difficult. In the case that the material was sufficiently comprehended by the students they'd be released for the remainder of that class.

I think the immediate term impacts from AI on education are going to be profound. I do not have a well formulated opinion on these impacts yet, nor how educators can prepare. I'm developing an AI integrated project implementation and management system, which has increasingly obvious and institutional knowledge pyramid shattering implications and dangers. Plus, this work is in the dynamic framework of rapid changes to state of the art LLM AIs. At the very least, start using LLM AIs. Grab some of these "prompt engineer" eBooks and guides and try to use AI to do your ordinary daily work. Just to expose yourself to the possibilities, to get familiar with the terminology, so prepare for when both good and lousy AI software starts appearing. You'll want to be able to identify lazy, lousy AI software for the waste of time that it will be. There's going to be a tidal wave…

As we all know, AI is advancing more and more each day. One example of this is Alpha Zero that was trained to play chess. Not only did it teach itself to surpass human players, it also surpasses other chess engines. It's moves are often viewed as imaginative and creative. It is able to explore new paradigms in chess. Unfortunately we don't understand what's going on in Alpha Zero's mind. Presently there is no way to communicate this. I feel that in the future this will change and AI will be able to share it's thinking with humans and open up new realms of knowledge that were never before discovered. This is an exciting prospect for me.

I'm both excited and a bit nervous about AI's potential impact on education. AI can personalize learning and automate admin tasks, freeing up time for meaningful interactions. However, it's crucial to maintain the human element in teaching. Finding the right balance will be key!
